Patient Prep: No fasting required. For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Checks blood levels of lacosamide (brand name Vimpat), an anti-seizure medication, to help confirm the dose is in the expected range.
Long Description: Lacosamide is prescribed to help control certain seizure types. This test measures the amount of lacosamide in the blood, which can help a prescriber confirm the medication is being taken as directed and that levels fall within the range typically seen at recommended doses. It can be especially useful for patients with kidney or liver impairment, older adults, or those taking other seizure medications that can affect drug levels. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Product Name (Source): ALTN-0370 QST-000037010- Cytomegalovirus DNA, Qualitative Real-Time PCR, Saliva (CMV Saliva)
Test Name: Cytomegalovirus DNA, Qualitative Real-Time PCR, Saliva (CMV Saliva)
Short Description: Detects active cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection using a simple saliva swab — commonly used to screen newborns.
Long Description: This qualitative PCR test looks for CMV DNA in a saliva sample to determine whether an active CMV infection is present. CMV is a very common virus, but when passed to a baby before birth (congenital CMV), it's a leading cause of hearing loss and other developmental issues. This saliva-based test is often used to screen newborns — particularly those who don't pass a hearing screening — or to check for active infection in someone with symptoms. Results are reported as positive or negative (qualitative), not as a viral load.

Product Name (Source): QST-00014512 Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor (VEGF), ELISA
Short Description: Measures blood levels of VEGF, a protein that drives new blood vessel growth, often used in cancer-related evaluations.
Long Description: VEGF is a protein that stimulates angiogenesis (the growth of new blood vessels). Because many tumors rely on angiogenesis to grow and spread, VEGF levels may be of interest when evaluating patients with certain cancers, including those being considered for or currently on anti-angiogenesis therapy. This is a supportive lab value meant to be interpreted alongside other clinical findings and imaging, not a standalone diagnostic or cancer screening test.

Product Name (Source): QST-00090871 Acute Myeloid Leukemia Prognostic Panel (Normal Karyotype)
Test Name: Acute Myeloid Leukemia Prognostic Panel (Normal Karyotype)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; typically ordered alongside or after a cytogenetics/karyotype workup.
Short Description: Genetic panel (NPM1, FLT3, CEBPA) that helps refine the prognosis and treatment approach for AML patients with a normal karyotype.
Long Description: For patients already diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia (AML) whose chromosomes look normal on standard cytogenetic testing, this panel looks for mutations in three genes long used for risk stratification: NPM1, FLT3, and CEBPA. Favorable NPM1 or CEBPA mutations are generally associated with better treatment response and survival, while FLT3 mutations (particularly internal tandem duplications) are generally associated with higher-risk disease. Results are used by oncology providers, in line with NCCN guidance, to help classify risk and guide treatment decisions in normal-karyotype AML.

Product Name (Source): QST-34052 Active Chickenpox (Varicella) DNA Test - PCR Blood Test - Varicella-Zoster Virus DNA, Qual
Test Name: Active Chickenpox (Varicella) DNA Test - PCR Blood Test - Varicella-Zoster Virus DNA, Qualitative, Real-Time PCR
Specimen Type: Blood draw (whole blood, EDTA or ACD tube)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Best collected while the patient is symptomatic.
Short Description: PCR blood test that detects active varicella-zoster virus (chickenpox/shingles) infection in someone with current symptoms.
Long Description: This qualitative real-time PCR test detects varicella-zoster virus (VZV) DNA, the virus that causes chickenpox (varicella) on first infection and shingles (herpes zoster) if it reactivates later in life. It's intended for people currently showing signs or symptoms of a VZV infection, to help confirm the diagnosis, and is not intended as a screening test for people without symptoms. Results are reported as positive or negative.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 12 hours prior to the blood draw is recommended.
Short Description: Advanced cholesterol test that measures the number and size of LDL, HDL, and VLDL particles, beyond a standard lipid panel.
Long Description: A standard cholesterol panel measures the amount of cholesterol carried by lipoproteins, but this NMR-based test goes further, measuring the actual number and size of the particles themselves (LDL particle number, small LDL particle number, LDL size, HDL particle number, large HDL particle number, HDL size, large VLDL particle number, and VLDL size). Particle number can be a more precise indicator of cardiovascular risk than cholesterol level alone. This test is typically used for people at intermediate or high cardiovascular risk, or to track how well lipid-lowering therapy is working over time.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (whole blood, special ACD Solution B tube - not centrifuged)
Patient Prep: No fasting required. Not available for patients under 16 years old.
Short Description: Measures total glutathione, the body's key antioxidant, to help assess oxidative stress and detoxification capacity.
Long Description: Glutathione is the body's most abundant antioxidant, helping protect cells from oxidative damage and supporting liver detoxification and immune function. This single-marker test measures total glutathione in whole blood. It's often used in wellness and functional-medicine settings to explore nutritional status, the effects of toxin exposure, or general antioxidant capacity as part of a broader health picture, rather than to diagnose a specific disease.

Product Name (Source): QST-4605 Culture, Fungus, Skin, Hair, Nail with Direct Fluorescent/KOH
Test Name: Culture, Fungus, Skin, Hair, Nail with Direct Fluorescent/KOH
Specimen Type: Skin scraping, hair, or nail clipping
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample should be collected from the affected area before starting antifungal treatment when possible.
Short Description: Identifies fungal infections of the skin, hair, or nails by combining a direct microscopic exam with a fungal culture.
Long Description: This test combines a rapid direct exam (KOH prep with fluorescent staining) with a fungal culture on samples from skin, hair, or nails. The direct exam can quickly flag whether fungal elements are present, while the culture grows out the organism for identification — useful for confirming suspected conditions like ringworm, athlete's foot, or nail fungus, and for identifying which specific organism is involved so treatment can be targeted appropriately. If the culture is positive, further organism identification may be performed at an additional charge.

Product Name (Source): QST-91238 Human Transforming Growth Factor beta 1 (TGF-b1)
Patient Prep: No fasting required. Careful specimen handling is needed to avoid hemolysis, which can falsely raise results.
Short Description: Measures TGF-beta1, a signaling protein involved in immune regulation, tissue repair, and inflammation.
Long Description: TGF-beta1 is a cytokine that plays a role in cell growth, immune regulation, wound healing, and the formation of scar or fibrotic tissue. Abnormal levels may be associated with inflammatory conditions, fibrosis, immune dysfunction, or certain cancers. This test is commonly used in functional and integrative medicine settings to help assess immune status or track changes over time, and should be interpreted alongside a patient's broader clinical picture.

Patient Prep: Fasting 8 hours preferred but not required. Should not be drawn while on certain blood thinners (heparin, direct thrombin/Xa inhibitors) as these can affect results.
Short Description: Screens for resistance to activated protein C, the most common inherited clotting risk factor (linked to Factor V Leiden).
Long Description: Activated Protein C (APC) normally helps keep blood clotting in check. This test checks whether a patient's blood responds normally to APC; a reduced response ('APC resistance') is most often caused by the Factor V Leiden gene variant and raises the risk of abnormal blood clots (venous thromboembolism). It's typically ordered as part of a workup for unexplained blood clots or a family history of clotting disorders. An abnormal result is often followed by confirmatory Factor V Leiden DNA testing.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps diagnose myasthenia gravis, an autoimmune condition causing muscle weakness.
Long Description: Myasthenia gravis (MG) is an autoimmune disorder where the immune system attacks acetylcholine receptors (AChR) at the junction between nerves and muscles, leading to muscle weakness and fatigue. This test detects AChR binding antibodies, which are found in roughly 85-90% of people with generalized MG, making it a key test for confirming a suspected MG diagnosis and for monitoring response to treatment over time. If this antibody is negative but MG is still suspected, additional antibody tests (blocking, modulating, or MuSK) may be considered.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma, special handling - often collected on ice)
Patient Prep: Fasting and morning collection are often recommended, since ACTH follows a daily (circadian) pattern. Follow your provider's specific timing instructions.
Short Description: Measures ACTH, a pituitary hormone that helps evaluate adrenal gland function and conditions like Cushing's or Addison's disease.
Long Description: Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) is produced by the pituitary gland and signals the adrenal glands to produce cortisol. Measuring ACTH alongside cortisol helps determine whether a hormone imbalance originates in the pituitary or the adrenal glands, and supports the workup of conditions such as Cushing's syndrome (excess cortisol) or Addison's disease/adrenal insufficiency (cortisol deficiency). Because ACTH levels vary by time of day and the hormone degrades quickly outside the body, collection timing and handling matter for accurate results.

Short Description: Measures albumin, a key blood protein used to help assess liver function, kidney function, and overall nutritional status.
Long Description: Albumin is the most abundant protein in blood plasma, made by the liver. It helps keep fluid in blood vessels and transports hormones, vitamins, and medications throughout the body. Low albumin can point to liver disease, kidney protein loss, poor nutrition, or chronic inflammation, while it's commonly included as part of routine wellness, liver, or kidney panels. This is usually ordered as part of a broader metabolic or hepatic panel rather than as a standalone test.

Patient Prep: No fasting required for most patients, though your provider may request fasting if ordered alongside other tests.
Short Description: Measures alkaline phosphatase (ALP), an enzyme used to help evaluate liver and bone health.
Long Description: Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an enzyme found mainly in the liver, bile ducts, and bones. Elevated levels can point toward liver or bile duct conditions (such as blockage) or increased bone turnover (including normal growth in children, or bone conditions in adults). This test is commonly included in liver panels and general wellness screening, and results are typically interpreted alongside other liver enzymes and, if needed, ALP isoenzyme testing to pinpoint the source.

Short Description: Measures alpha-1-antitrypsin, a protein that helps protect the lungs and liver — low levels can point to a genetic deficiency.
Long Description: Alpha-1-antitrypsin (AAT) is a protein made by the liver that protects lung tissue from damage. An inherited deficiency in AAT is linked to early-onset emphysema/COPD and certain liver diseases. This quantitative test measures the amount of AAT in the blood and is often used to evaluate unexplained lung disease (especially in younger patients or non-smokers), unexplained liver disease, or a family history of AAT deficiency. Low results are often followed by AAT genotyping to confirm a diagnosis.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (AFP) most often used to help monitor liver cancer or certain germ cell tumors.
Long Description: Alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is a protein that's normally high in fetal development but low in healthy adults. Elevated AFP in adults can be associated with liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma) or certain testicular/ovarian germ cell tumors, and the test is commonly used to help monitor known cancers or assess treatment response, often alongside imaging. AFP can also be elevated in non-cancerous liver conditions, so results are interpreted together with other clinical findings rather than used alone to diagnose cancer.

Short Description: Measures amylase, a digestive enzyme, most often used to help evaluate pancreas-related symptoms such as abdominal pain.
Long Description: Amylase is an enzyme produced mainly by the pancreas and salivary glands that helps digest carbohydrates. Elevated blood amylase is most often associated with pancreatitis (inflammation of the pancreas), but can also be elevated in other abdominal or salivary gland conditions. This test is commonly ordered for patients with abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting to help support or rule out a pancreas-related cause, often alongside lipase testing.

Short Description: Screening antibody test for autoimmune disease, with automatic follow-up testing (reflex) if the result is positive.
Long Description: The antinuclear antibody (ANA) test screens for autoantibodies that target components of the cell nucleus, which are associated with autoimmune conditions such as lupus, Sjogren's syndrome, and other connective tissue diseases. This version includes reflex testing: if the initial ANA screen is positive, the lab automatically performs further antibody testing (at an additional charge) to help characterize the pattern and identify the specific autoimmune condition involved. It's typically ordered when a patient has symptoms suggestive of an autoimmune disorder, such as joint pain, rash, or unexplained fatigue.

Short Description: Antibody blood test used to help support a lupus diagnosis and monitor disease activity.
Long Description: Anti-double-stranded DNA (anti-dsDNA) antibodies are strongly associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE, or lupus) and are relatively specific to the condition compared to other autoantibodies. This test is typically ordered after a positive ANA screen to help confirm a lupus diagnosis, and antibody levels can also rise and fall with disease activity, making the test useful for monitoring flares and treatment response over time in patients already diagnosed with lupus.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps evaluate suspected primary biliary cholangitis, a chronic liver condition, with automatic follow-up testing if positive.
Long Description: Anti-mitochondrial antibodies (AMA) are found in the large majority of people with primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), a chronic autoimmune liver disease that damages the small bile ducts. This test includes reflex testing, meaning if the initial screen is positive, additional antibody testing is automatically performed (at extra charge) to further characterize the result. It's typically ordered when a patient has unexplained liver enzyme abnormalities, itching, or other symptoms suggestive of PBC.

Short Description: Antibody test used alongside thyroid cancer follow-up and some autoimmune thyroid evaluations.
Long Description: Thyroglobulin antibodies (TgAb) are autoantibodies directed against thyroglobulin, a protein made by the thyroid gland. This test is most commonly ordered in patients who have had thyroid cancer treated with surgery and/or radioactive iodine, because these antibodies can interfere with thyroglobulin tumor-marker testing used to monitor for cancer recurrence — so checking for them helps your provider interpret those follow-up results correctly. It can also be part of a broader workup for autoimmune thyroid conditions such as Hashimoto's thyroiditis.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgE antibody response to scallops.
Long Description: This test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to scallop, a common shellfish allergen. A positive (elevated) result suggests the immune system has produced antibodies against scallop proteins, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true food allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside a patient's symptom history. It's often ordered for patients with a suspected shellfish allergy, or as part of a broader food or shellfish allergy panel.

Short Description: Measures direct (conjugated) bilirubin, used to help pinpoint the cause of jaundice or liver/bile duct problems.
Long Description: Bilirubin is a byproduct of the normal breakdown of red blood cells. The 'direct' (conjugated) form has already been processed by the liver. Elevated direct bilirubin points more specifically toward liver or bile-duct problems (such as blockage) rather than causes of jaundice originating from red blood cell breakdown. This test is typically ordered alongside total bilirubin and other liver panel components to help narrow down the cause of jaundice or abnormal liver tests.

Short Description: Measures total bilirubin, a standard marker of liver function and cause of jaundice (yellowing of skin/eyes).
Long Description: Total bilirubin reflects the combined amount of direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin in the blood, a byproduct of normal red blood cell breakdown that's processed by the liver. Elevated levels can cause jaundice and may point to liver disease, bile duct blockage, or increased red blood cell breakdown. This is a routine component of liver function panels and general wellness screening.

Short Description: Measures blood urea nitrogen (BUN), a standard marker of kidney function and hydration status.
Long Description: Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) reflects urea, a waste product filtered out by the kidneys. Elevated BUN can point to reduced kidney function, dehydration, or a high-protein diet, while low levels can be seen with liver disease or malnutrition. BUN is a routine part of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels, most often interpreted together with creatinine to assess overall kidney function.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) - calculated from BUN and creatinine results
Short Description: Calculated ratio of BUN to creatinine, used alongside kidney function testing to help identify the cause of abnormal results.
Long Description: The BUN/creatinine ratio is a calculated value (not a separately drawn test) that compares two kidney-related markers, blood urea nitrogen and creatinine. Because BUN and creatinine can each rise for different reasons, looking at their ratio helps a provider distinguish between causes such as dehydration, reduced blood flow to the kidneys, or direct kidney damage. It's typically reported as part of a metabolic panel rather than ordered on its own.

Short Description: Measures total blood calcium, used to help evaluate bone, kidney, parathyroid, and overall metabolic health.
Long Description: Calcium is essential for bone health, muscle function, nerve signaling, and blood clotting, and its levels are tightly regulated by the parathyroid glands, vitamin D, and kidneys. Abnormal calcium levels can point to parathyroid disorders, certain cancers, kidney disease, vitamin D imbalance, or other metabolic conditions. This is a standard component of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels and general wellness screening.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Best collected as soon as possible after suspected exposure.
Short Description: Measures carboxyhemoglobin in the blood, used to check for carbon monoxide exposure or poisoning.
Long Description: Carboxyhemoglobin forms when carbon monoxide (CO) binds to hemoglobin in place of oxygen, reducing the blood's ability to carry oxygen. This test measures the percentage of hemoglobin bound to carbon monoxide, which helps confirm and gauge the severity of CO exposure — for example, from smoke inhalation, faulty heating systems, or occupational exposure. Smokers can also have mildly elevated baseline levels, which is taken into account when interpreting results.

Short Description: Measures carbon dioxide (bicarbonate) level, a standard marker used to help assess the body's acid-base balance.
Long Description: This test measures total carbon dioxide (mostly in the form of bicarbonate), which reflects how well the body is maintaining its acid-base (pH) balance. Abnormal levels can be related to kidney function, breathing (respiratory) conditions, vomiting/diarrhea, or metabolic disturbances such as diabetic ketoacidosis. CO2 is a routine component of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels, usually interpreted alongside electrolytes.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended, consistent with standard lipid testing.
Short Description: Estimates VLDL cholesterol, a triglyceride-carrying particle used alongside a standard lipid panel to assess cardiovascular risk.
Long Description: VLDL (very-low-density lipoprotein) cholesterol is a type of cholesterol carried mainly by triglyceride-rich particles made in the liver. It's usually calculated from a standard lipid panel and used together with LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol to build a fuller picture of cardiovascular risk. Elevated VLDL is often associated with high triglycerides and can be a factor in assessing heart disease risk, especially in people with metabolic syndrome or diabetes.

Short Description: Measures ceruloplasmin, a copper-carrying protein used to help evaluate copper metabolism and Wilson's disease.
Long Description: Ceruloplasmin is the main protein that carries copper in the blood and is made by the liver. Low ceruloplasmin, especially together with low blood copper, is a hallmark finding in Wilson's disease, a genetic disorder causing toxic copper buildup in the liver and brain. It's also used more broadly to help evaluate copper status and certain liver or neurological symptoms, since ceruloplasmin can also rise as part of a general inflammatory response.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of carbamazepine, a seizure and mood-stabilizing medication, to help guide safe dosing.
Long Description: Carbamazepine is used to treat certain seizure disorders, trigeminal neuralgia, and as a mood stabilizer in bipolar disorder. Because there's a fairly narrow range between an effective dose and one that causes side effects, this test measures the total drug concentration in blood to help a prescriber confirm the patient is taking the medication as directed and that the dose is well-tuned. It's a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Measures chloride, an electrolyte used to help evaluate the body's fluid and acid-base balance.
Long Description: Chloride is an electrolyte that works closely with sodium to help regulate fluid balance, blood volume, and blood pH. Abnormal chloride levels can be related to dehydration, kidney problems, or acid-base disturbances (such as from vomiting, diarrhea, or breathing conditions). It's a routine component of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels and general wellness screening, typically interpreted alongside other electrolytes.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results.
Short Description: Measures total cholesterol, a standard screening marker for cardiovascular risk.
Long Description: Total cholesterol reflects the combined amount of cholesterol carried by all lipoprotein particles in the blood (LDL, HDL, and VLDL). It's one of the most common screening tests for cardiovascular risk and is typically ordered as part of a full lipid panel, alongside LDL, HDL, and triglycerides, to give a fuller picture of heart disease risk rather than being interpreted alone.

Patient Prep: Keeping the sample warm until it reaches the lab may be requested, since cold temperatures can affect results — follow your provider's collection instructions.
Short Description: Antibody test that helps identify cold agglutinin disease, a cause of certain types of anemia triggered by cold temperatures.
Long Description: Cold agglutinins are antibodies that cause red blood cells to clump together at lower temperatures. This test helps evaluate suspected cold agglutinin disease, an autoimmune condition where these antibodies destroy red blood cells and can cause anemia, especially in cold weather. It's typically ordered when a patient has unexplained anemia along with symptoms triggered by cold exposure, or is being worked up for certain infections (like mycoplasma pneumonia) or blood disorders known to be associated with cold agglutinins.

Product Name (Source): QST0000361 COOMBS DIRECT / DIRECT ANTIGLOBULIN TEST
Short Description: Blood test that helps determine whether red blood cells are being attacked by antibodies, often used to evaluate certain types of anemia.
Long Description: The direct Coombs test (direct antiglobulin test, DAT) checks whether a patient's red blood cells are coated with antibodies or complement proteins, which can lead to the immune system destroying them (hemolytic anemia). It's commonly used to help investigate unexplained anemia, an unexpected drop in blood counts after a transfusion, or suspected autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and is also used in newborns to check for antibody-related jaundice linked to blood type incompatibility with the mother.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — trace-element (metal-free) collection tube typically required
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is often needed — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures blood copper levels, used to evaluate copper deficiency, toxicity, or conditions like Wilson's disease.
Long Description: This test measures the amount of copper circulating in the blood. Copper is an essential trace mineral, but too little or too much can cause health problems: low levels can be linked to nutritional deficiency or malabsorption, while elevated levels can be seen with inflammation or copper overload conditions such as Wilson's disease. It's often ordered alongside ceruloplasmin and zinc to build a fuller picture of trace mineral and metabolic status.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures how much copper is excreted in urine over 24 hours, most often used to help diagnose and monitor Wilson's disease.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of copper excreted in urine over a full 24-hour period. It's most commonly used to help diagnose Wilson's disease (a genetic disorder of copper overload) and to monitor how well copper-lowering treatment (chelation therapy) is working in patients already diagnosed with the condition. Because results depend on a complete, accurately timed collection, following the 24-hour collection instructions closely is important for a reliable result.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) — timing matters, since cortisol follows a daily pattern
Patient Prep: Morning collection (typically before 9-10 AM) is usually recommended, since cortisol levels naturally vary through the day. Follow your provider's specific timing instructions.
Short Description: Measures total cortisol, the body's main stress hormone, to help evaluate adrenal gland function.
Long Description: Cortisol is a hormone made by the adrenal glands that helps regulate metabolism, blood pressure, immune response, and the body's response to stress. This test measures total cortisol in the blood and is commonly used to help evaluate suspected Cushing's syndrome (excess cortisol) or adrenal insufficiency/Addison's disease (cortisol deficiency). Because cortisol naturally rises and falls throughout the day, the time of collection affects interpretation, and additional testing (such as an ACTH stimulation test) is sometimes needed to fully evaluate adrenal function.

Patient Prep: Fasting is often requested, especially when evaluating blood sugar or insulin-related conditions — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures C-peptide, a byproduct of insulin production, used to help evaluate how much insulin the body is naturally making.
Long Description: C-peptide is released in equal amounts to insulin when the pancreas produces it, making it a useful marker of the body's own insulin production — including in patients who take insulin as a medication, since C-peptide isn't affected by injected insulin. This test is commonly used to help distinguish between type 1 and type 2 diabetes, evaluate unusual cases of low blood sugar (hypoglycemia), or assess remaining pancreatic function in people with diabetes.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Strenuous exercise shortly before the test can temporarily raise results.
Short Description: Measures total creatine kinase (CK), an enzyme used to help evaluate muscle, heart, or brain injury.
Long Description: Creatine kinase (CK) is an enzyme found mainly in muscle tissue (skeletal muscle, heart muscle, and brain). Elevated CK can indicate muscle damage from causes such as intense exercise, muscle disease, muscle injury, or a heart attack. This test is often ordered to help evaluate unexplained muscle pain or weakness, monitor certain muscle-related medication side effects (such as from statins), or as part of a cardiac workup, sometimes together with CK isoenzyme testing to identify the tissue source.

Short Description: Measures creatinine, a standard marker of kidney function.
Long Description: Creatinine is a waste product from normal muscle breakdown that's filtered out of the blood by the kidneys. Elevated blood creatinine usually points to reduced kidney function, since the kidneys aren't clearing it as effectively. This is one of the most common kidney function tests, included in basic and comprehensive metabolic panels, and is often used together with BUN and estimated GFR to assess overall kidney health.

Short Description: Breaks down creatine kinase into specific isoenzymes to help pinpoint whether elevated CK is coming from the heart, skeletal muscle, or brain.
Long Description: When total creatine kinase (CK) is elevated, CK isoenzyme testing separates it into its component forms (CK-MB, CK-MM, CK-BB) to help identify which tissue it's coming from — CK-MB is associated more with heart muscle, CK-MM with skeletal muscle, and CK-BB with brain tissue. This helps a provider narrow down the likely cause of an elevated CK, such as distinguishing a cardiac cause from a skeletal muscle one.

Specimen Type: Urine sample (random or timed collection, per your provider's instructions)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific collection method (random vs. timed) may be requested.
Short Description: Measures creatinine in urine, often used alongside blood testing to help assess kidney function or to normalize other urine test results.
Long Description: Urine creatinine measures how much creatinine is being excreted in the urine. It's commonly used together with blood creatinine to help calculate creatinine clearance, a more detailed measure of kidney filtering function, and is also frequently used as a reference point ('normalizing' value) for other urine tests, such as urine protein or urine drug levels, to account for how concentrated or dilute a sample is.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) — special warm-collection handling required
Patient Prep: Requires special collection: the blood must be drawn into a pre-warmed tube and kept warm (body temperature) until it clots and is processed, since cryoglobulins can be lost if the sample cools. Coordinate collection with your provider or lab.
Short Description: Qualitative blood test that checks for cryoglobulins, abnormal proteins linked to certain autoimmune, infectious, or blood-related conditions.
Long Description: Cryoglobulins are abnormal blood proteins that clump together in the cold and dissolve again when warmed. Their presence (cryoglobulinemia) can be associated with autoimmune diseases, certain chronic infections (such as hepatitis C), and some blood cancers, and can cause symptoms like joint pain, skin rash, or nerve or kidney problems when exposed to cold. This qualitative test simply checks whether cryoglobulins are present or absent; because they're temperature-sensitive, correct specimen collection and handling (keeping the sample warm) is essential for an accurate result.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected before starting antibiotics.
Short Description: Throat culture used to identify bacteria causing a sore throat, most commonly strep throat.
Long Description: A throat culture involves swabbing the back of the throat and tonsil area to grow and identify bacteria that may be causing an infection — most notably group A Streptococcus, the cause of strep throat. It's typically ordered when a patient has sore throat symptoms and a rapid strep test is negative, inconclusive, or unavailable, since culture is more sensitive and can also identify other, less common causes of throat infection.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination; follow your provider's collection instructions.
Short Description: Urine culture used to identify bacteria causing a urinary tract infection (UTI) and guide antibiotic choice.
Long Description: A routine urine culture grows out any bacteria present in a urine sample to help diagnose a urinary tract infection (UTI) and identify the specific organism responsible. If bacteria grow, susceptibility testing is often performed to show which antibiotics are likely to work, helping guide effective treatment. It's typically ordered for patients with UTI symptoms (such as burning with urination, urgency, or pelvic pain) or a urinalysis suggestive of infection.

Patient Prep: First-morning urine is often preferred for the most concentrated (and accurate) sample, though not always required — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Qualitative urine pregnancy test that checks for hCG to confirm whether a person is pregnant.
Long Description: This test checks urine for human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), a hormone produced during pregnancy, and reports a simple positive or negative result rather than a specific hormone level. It's the standard test used to confirm pregnancy and is typically most reliable when done after a missed period, since hCG needs time to rise to a detectable level after conception.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though morning collection is sometimes preferred.
Short Description: Measures DHEA-sulfate, an adrenal hormone used to help evaluate adrenal gland function and certain hormone imbalances.
Long Description: DHEA-sulfate (DHEA-S) is a hormone produced almost entirely by the adrenal glands and serves as a precursor the body can convert into other hormones, including testosterone and estrogen. Because it's more stable throughout the day than some other adrenal hormones, it's a useful marker of overall adrenal androgen production. This test is commonly used to help evaluate symptoms such as excess or unwanted hair growth, irregular periods, or suspected adrenal gland conditions, including certain adrenal tumors.

Short Description: Antibody blood test that checks for past exposure to cytomegalovirus (CMV) — commonly used in pregnancy and pre-transplant screening.
Long Description: This test detects IgG antibodies to cytomegalovirus (CMV), which indicate a past infection and typically lifelong immunity, as opposed to a current active infection. It's commonly used to check immune status — for example, in pregnancy planning, before organ or stem cell transplant, or in immunocompromised patients — to understand a person's CMV exposure history. It is different from CMV PCR testing, which detects an active infection rather than past exposure.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw, generally at least 6-8 hours after the last dose, is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of digoxin, a heart medication, to help confirm safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Digoxin is a medication used to treat certain heart rhythm disorders and heart failure. It has a narrow therapeutic range — meaning the difference between an effective dose and a toxic one can be small — so this test measures the amount of digoxin in the blood to help a prescriber confirm dosing is safe and effective, particularly important for patients with kidney impairment or those on other interacting medications. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Measures erythropoietin (EPO), a kidney hormone that stimulates red blood cell production, used to help evaluate certain types of anemia or elevated red blood cell counts.
Long Description: Erythropoietin (EPO) is a hormone made mainly by the kidneys that signals the bone marrow to produce red blood cells. This test is used to help investigate the cause of anemia (low red blood cell count) or polycythemia (elevated red blood cell count) — for example, distinguishing kidney-related anemia (where EPO is often low) from other causes, or evaluating certain bone marrow conditions where EPO may be inappropriately low or high relative to red blood cell levels.

Specimen Type: 24-hour or random urine sample, per collection instructions — requires special handling
Patient Prep: Requires specific specimen handling: the sample must typically be protected from light and shipped frozen; supplements containing vitamins or minerals are sometimes asked to be paused beforehand. Follow your provider's or lab's specific collection instructions.
Short Description: Urine test measuring pyrrole compounds, sometimes used in functional/integrative medicine to explore stress, mood, and nutrient status.
Long Description: This test measures pyrrole compounds (sometimes called kryptopyrroles or the 'mauve factor') in urine. It's primarily used within functional and integrative medicine as a marker some practitioners associate with physiological or psychological stress and with zinc/vitamin B6 depletion, though its clinical validity and reference ranges are not well established in mainstream/conventional lab medicine, and research on it remains limited and debated. It's typically ordered as part of a broader nutritional or functional workup rather than to diagnose a specific medical condition, and specimen handling (protecting the sample from light and heat) is important for a valid result.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to menstrual cycle may matter for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures total estrogen, used to help evaluate reproductive hormone balance and certain menstrual, fertility, or menopause-related concerns.
Long Description: This test measures total estrogen (the combined activity of the main estrogen hormones) in the blood. It's used to help evaluate a range of concerns including irregular or absent periods, fertility issues, symptoms of menopause or perimenopause, and monitoring of certain hormone therapies. In many cases, more specific estrogen fractions (like estradiol) are ordered instead of or alongside total estrogen, depending on what's being evaluated.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — timed peak and trough samples
Patient Prep: Requires two timed blood draws coordinated with a gentamicin dose (a 'peak' shortly after dosing and a 'trough' just before the next dose) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Pharmacokinetic monitoring service for gentamicin, an antibiotic, using timed peak and trough blood levels to help personalize dosing.
Long Description: Gentamicin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing because too little may not effectively treat infection, while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This service measures gentamicin levels at two specific times relative to a dose — a peak level (to confirm the dose reached an effective level) and a trough level (to confirm the drug is clearing before the next dose) — and uses pharmacokinetic calculations to help a clinician fine-tune the dose and interval for that specific patient.

Short Description: Measures ferritin, the body's main iron storage protein, used to help evaluate iron deficiency or iron overload.
Long Description: Ferritin reflects how much iron is stored in the body. Low ferritin is one of the earliest and most reliable indicators of iron deficiency, often before anemia develops, while high ferritin can point to iron overload conditions (such as hemochromatosis) or can rise simply as part of inflammation, since ferritin is also an acute-phase reactant. This test is commonly ordered to investigate fatigue, suspected anemia, or to monitor iron status in people with known iron disorders.

Short Description: Measures fibrinogen, a clotting protein, used to help evaluate bleeding or clotting disorders and surgical risk.
Long Description: Fibrinogen is a protein made by the liver that's essential for normal blood clot formation. This quantitative test measures fibrinogen levels and is commonly used to help evaluate unexplained bleeding or bruising, assess clotting risk before surgery, monitor certain liver or clotting disorders, and investigate conditions like disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), where fibrinogen can drop significantly.

Short Description: Measures folate (vitamin B9) in the blood, used to help evaluate nutritional status and certain types of anemia.
Long Description: Folate (vitamin B9) is essential for red blood cell production and DNA synthesis. Low folate can lead to a type of anemia (megaloblastic anemia) and is often linked to poor diet, alcohol use, malabsorption, or certain medications, and is especially important to monitor during pregnancy due to its role in preventing neural tube defects. This test is commonly ordered as part of an anemia workup or general nutritional assessment.

Short Description: Measures folate levels inside red blood cells, offering a longer-term picture of folate status than a standard serum folate test.
Long Description: Unlike serum folate, which reflects recent dietary intake, RBC (red blood cell) folate reflects the average folate status over the roughly 3-month lifespan of red blood cells, giving a more stable, longer-term picture of the body's folate stores. This test is often used when a more reliable assessment of folate status is needed — for example, to clarify borderline serum folate results or evaluate certain causes of anemia.

Short Description: Measures alpha-1-acid glycoprotein, an inflammation marker sometimes used alongside other tests to assess acute illness or infection.
Long Description: Alpha-1-acid glycoprotein (also called orosomucoid) is a protein made by the liver that rises during inflammation, infection, tissue injury, or certain cancers — a so-called 'acute phase reactant.' It's used to help support the assessment of inflammatory or infectious conditions, and is sometimes considered when interpreting how certain protein-bound medications behave in the body, since this protein can bind and carry some drugs in the bloodstream.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle can matter for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), used to help evaluate fertility, menstrual cycle irregularities, and menopause.
Long Description: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) is produced by the pituitary gland and plays a central role in reproductive function, stimulating egg development in the ovaries and sperm production in the testes. This test is commonly used to help evaluate fertility concerns, irregular or absent menstrual periods, suspected menopause or perimenopause, and certain pituitary or gonadal conditions, often alongside other reproductive hormones like LH and estradiol.

Short Description: Measures GGT, a liver enzyme often used to help evaluate liver and bile duct health, and sometimes alcohol-related liver stress.
Long Description: Gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) is an enzyme found mainly in the liver that's sensitive to liver cell damage and bile duct problems. It's often used together with other liver enzymes (like ALP) to help confirm whether an elevated ALP is coming from the liver or from bone, and can also be a useful marker when evaluating alcohol-related liver stress or monitoring liver health over time.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 8-12 hours is often recommended for the most standard interpretation, especially if being evaluated for diabetes — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures blood glucose (sugar) level in serum, a core test for evaluating diabetes and overall metabolic health.
Long Description: Glucose is the body's primary energy source, and this test measures how much is circulating in the blood at the time of the draw. It's one of the most common lab tests, used to screen for and monitor diabetes and prediabetes, evaluate symptoms like excessive thirst or fatigue, and as a routine part of general wellness and metabolic panels. Results are interpreted differently depending on whether the sample was fasting or random/non-fasting.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 8-12 hours is often recommended for the most standard interpretation, especially if being evaluated for diabetes — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures blood glucose (sugar) level in plasma, a core test for evaluating diabetes and overall metabolic health.
Long Description: This test measures glucose in the plasma portion of blood rather than serum; clinically it's used the same way — to screen for and monitor diabetes and prediabetes, evaluate symptoms like excessive thirst, fatigue, or blurred vision, and as part of routine wellness or metabolic panels. Results are interpreted differently depending on whether the sample was fasting or random/non-fasting.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin A1c, a 2-3 month average of blood sugar control, widely used to diagnose and monitor diabetes.
Long Description: Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c) reflects the percentage of hemoglobin in red blood cells that has glucose attached, which builds up in proportion to average blood sugar levels over roughly the prior 2-3 months. Unlike a single glucose reading, A1c provides a longer-term picture of blood sugar control, making it a standard test for diagnosing diabetes and prediabetes, and for monitoring how well a diabetes treatment plan is working over time. No fasting is required, which makes it a convenient test to combine with other routine bloodwork.

Product Name (Source): QST0000498 HEP B SURF AG W/CONF STD TEST
Test Name: HEP B SURF AG W/CONF STD TEST
Short Description: Screens for hepatitis B surface antigen, with automatic confirmatory testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) is the first marker to appear in an active hepatitis B infection, and a positive result indicates the person is currently infected (either acutely or chronically) and potentially able to spread the virus. This version includes a confirmatory ('supplemental') test that automatically runs if the initial screen is positive, to help rule out a false-positive result before reporting a confirmed diagnosis. It's commonly used for hepatitis B screening in at-risk individuals, pregnancy, or before certain medical treatments.

Product Name (Source): QST0000499 HEP B SURF AB QL
Short Description: Qualitative antibody test that checks for immunity to hepatitis B, often used to confirm vaccine protection.
Long Description: Hepatitis B surface antibody (anti-HBs) develops after a person has been successfully vaccinated against hepatitis B, or has recovered from a past infection, and indicates immune protection against the virus. This qualitative (positive/negative) test is commonly used to confirm immunity after vaccination — for example, for healthcare workers, students, or others who need to document hepatitis B immunity for school or employment.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Best avoided during an active hemolytic episode, as results can be falsely normal at that time.
Short Description: Screens for G6PD deficiency, an inherited condition that can cause red blood cells to break down under certain triggers.
Long Description: Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency is a common inherited condition where red blood cells are more vulnerable to breaking down (hemolysis) when exposed to certain triggers such as specific medications, infections, or fava beans. This test screens for the enzyme deficiency and is often ordered when a patient has unexplained anemia or jaundice after one of these triggers, before starting certain medications known to cause problems in G6PD-deficient individuals, or as part of newborn screening in some regions.

Product Name (Source): QST0000501 HEP B CORE AB TOTAL
Short Description: Antibody test that helps identify current or past hepatitis B infection, including cases missed by other hepatitis B tests.
Long Description: Hepatitis B core antibody (anti-HBc total) develops in response to hepatitis B infection and can remain positive during both current infection and after past infection has resolved, unlike the surface antibody, which mainly reflects vaccination or recovery. This test is often included in hepatitis B panels to help distinguish between vaccinated individuals (who typically only have surface antibody) and those who have been infected with the virus at some point, and to help detect certain hard-to-catch chronic infections.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for current or past hepatitis A infection, or immunity from vaccination.
Long Description: Hepatitis A total antibody detects both IgM (recent infection) and IgG (past infection or vaccination) antibodies to the hepatitis A virus, making it useful as a general marker of exposure or immunity. A positive result means the person has either been infected with hepatitis A at some point or has developed immunity through vaccination; further IgM-specific testing can help clarify whether an infection is recent if that's in question.

Short Description: Measures hematocrit, the percentage of blood made up of red blood cells, used to help evaluate anemia or other blood conditions.
Long Description: Hematocrit reflects the proportion of blood volume that's made up of red blood cells. It's a standard part of a complete blood count (CBC) and helps evaluate anemia (low hematocrit), dehydration or certain blood disorders that increase red blood cell mass (high hematocrit), and general blood health as part of routine screening or symptom workups such as fatigue or shortness of breath.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin, the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells, used to help evaluate anemia and overall blood health.
Long Description: Hemoglobin is the protein in red blood cells responsible for carrying oxygen throughout the body. Low hemoglobin is a hallmark of anemia and can cause symptoms like fatigue, weakness, or shortness of breath, while high hemoglobin can be seen with dehydration or certain blood disorders. It's a core component of a complete blood count and one of the most commonly ordered lab values in routine and symptom-driven testing.

Short Description: Antibody test that specifically identifies a recent or current hepatitis A infection.
Long Description: Hepatitis A IgM antibody rises early in a hepatitis A infection and typically indicates the infection is recent or currently active, as opposed to a past infection or vaccination (which are reflected by IgG antibodies instead). This test is commonly ordered when a patient has symptoms of acute hepatitis (such as jaundice, fatigue, or abdominal pain) and a current hepatitis A infection is suspected.

Patient Prep: Fasting and morning collection are often recommended, since growth hormone levels vary throughout the day and with exercise/stress — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures human growth hormone (HGH), used to help evaluate suspected growth hormone excess or deficiency.
Long Description: Human growth hormone (HGH), made by the pituitary gland, plays a central role in growth during childhood and in metabolism throughout life. Because it's released in pulses and varies significantly through the day, a single measurement is often just a starting point; abnormal results may prompt further specialized testing (such as growth hormone stimulation or suppression tests) to evaluate conditions like growth hormone deficiency, acromegaly, or gigantism.

Short Description: Genetic marker test associated with certain inflammatory and autoimmune joint conditions, including ankylosing spondylitis.
Long Description: HLA-B27 is a genetic marker (a type of protein found on white blood cells) that's strongly associated with a group of inflammatory joint conditions known as spondyloarthropathies, most notably ankylosing spondylitis, as well as reactive arthritis and certain cases of uveitis (eye inflammation). A positive result doesn't confirm a diagnosis on its own — many people with the marker never develop disease — but it can support a diagnosis when combined with symptoms like chronic back pain and stiffness, and imaging findings.

Short Description: Measures immunoglobulin A (IgA), an antibody class important for mucosal immunity, used to help evaluate immune function.
Long Description: Immunoglobulin A (IgA) is an antibody found mainly in mucosal areas like the gut, respiratory tract, and saliva, where it helps defend against infection. This test is used to help evaluate recurrent infections, certain autoimmune or gastrointestinal conditions (like celiac disease, where IgA levels matter for interpreting related antibody tests), and suspected immune deficiencies, including selective IgA deficiency, one of the more common inherited immune deficiencies.

Short Description: Measures total immunoglobulin E (IgE), an antibody linked to allergic reactions and certain parasitic infections.
Long Description: Immunoglobulin E (IgE) is the antibody class most closely tied to allergic reactions, released in response to allergens like pollen, foods, or insect stings, and also elevated in some parasitic infections. This test measures the total amount of IgE in the blood, which can support (but not by itself confirm) a diagnosis of an allergic condition such as asthma, eczema, or allergic rhinitis; more specific allergen IgE testing is often used to identify particular triggers.

Short Description: Measures immunoglobulin G (IgG), the most abundant antibody class, used to help evaluate immune function and past infections.
Long Description: Immunoglobulin G (IgG) is the most common type of antibody in the blood and reflects longer-term immune memory, including past infections and vaccinations. This test is used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, certain chronic infections or autoimmune conditions, and overall antibody production, and is often ordered alongside IgA and IgM as part of a broader immune function panel.

Short Description: Measures immunoglobulin M (IgM), the antibody class typically produced first during a new infection.
Long Description: Immunoglobulin M (IgM) is usually the first antibody the immune system produces in response to a new infection, making it useful for identifying recent or acute immune responses. This test is used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, certain autoimmune or blood disorders, and general antibody production, typically as part of a broader immunoglobulin panel alongside IgG and IgA.

Patient Prep: Fasting for at least 8 hours is required for accurate results.
Short Description: Measures fasting insulin, used to help evaluate insulin resistance and certain blood sugar disorders.
Long Description: Insulin is the hormone that helps regulate blood sugar by allowing cells to take up glucose. Measuring fasting insulin levels — usually alongside fasting glucose — helps evaluate insulin resistance (a key feature of prediabetes, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome), investigate causes of low blood sugar, and assess certain hormonal conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), where insulin resistance is common.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is generally preferred, since iron levels naturally vary through the day; fasting may also be requested.
Short Description: Measures total iron in the blood, used to help evaluate iron deficiency or iron overload.
Long Description: This test measures the amount of iron circulating in the blood at the time of the draw. It's commonly ordered together with other iron-related tests (like ferritin, TIBC, and transferrin saturation) to help build a full picture of iron status, since serum iron alone can vary significantly through the day and isn't always a reliable standalone marker. It's used to help investigate suspected anemia, unexplained fatigue, or iron overload conditions such as hemochromatosis.

Patient Prep: Requires careful collection technique (often without a tourniquet, and processed quickly on ice) since improper handling can falsely raise results — follow your provider's or lab's specific instructions.
Short Description: Measures lactic acid (lactate) in the blood, used to help evaluate poor tissue oxygenation and certain metabolic conditions.
Long Description: Lactic acid (lactate) builds up in the blood when the body's tissues aren't getting enough oxygen, causing cells to rely more on anaerobic metabolism. Elevated lactate can be a sign of serious conditions such as sepsis, shock, or significant tissue injury, and is also used to help evaluate certain metabolic or mitochondrial disorders and unexplained muscle symptoms. Because lactate can rise artificially with improper collection technique, careful specimen handling is especially important for this test.

Short Description: Measures total LDH, an enzyme found in many tissues, used as a general marker of cell or tissue damage.
Long Description: Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) is an enzyme found in nearly all body tissues, so elevated levels are a nonspecific sign of cell damage that can be seen in a wide range of conditions — including liver disease, heart attack, certain anemias, muscle injury, and some cancers. Because it's not specific to one organ, LDH is often used alongside other, more targeted tests to help narrow down the underlying cause, or to help monitor certain cancers and blood disorders over time.

Short Description: Breaks down total LDH into its specific isoenzymes to help identify which tissue an elevated LDH result is coming from.
Long Description: When total LDH is elevated, isoenzyme testing separates it into five distinct fractions (LD1 through LD5), each more closely associated with a particular tissue — for example, LD1 with heart and red blood cells, and LD5 with liver and skeletal muscle. This helps a provider narrow down the likely source of an elevated LDH result, supporting the workup of conditions like heart attack, hemolysis, or liver disease.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required.
Short Description: Measures blood lead levels, used to screen for and monitor lead exposure or poisoning.
Long Description: This test measures the amount of lead in the blood, the standard way to detect and monitor lead exposure. It's commonly used for childhood lead screening, occupational exposure monitoring (such as for workers in construction, manufacturing, or battery recycling), and evaluating symptoms that could be related to lead toxicity, since even relatively low levels of lead exposure can affect development in children and various body systems in adults.

Short Description: Measures lipase, a pancreatic enzyme, most often used to help evaluate pancreatitis and other pancreas-related symptoms.
Long Description: Lipase is an enzyme produced by the pancreas that helps digest fat. It's considered a more specific marker of pancreatitis (pancreas inflammation) than amylase, and is commonly ordered for patients with abdominal pain, nausea, or vomiting to help support or rule out a pancreatic cause, often alongside amylase and imaging.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results, consistent with standard lipid testing.
Short Description: Measures HDL cholesterol, the 'good' cholesterol, used as part of cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: HDL (high-density lipoprotein) cholesterol helps remove excess cholesterol from the bloodstream and is often referred to as 'good' cholesterol because higher levels are generally associated with lower cardiovascular risk. This test is a standard component of a lipid panel, interpreted alongside LDL, total cholesterol, and triglycerides to build a full picture of heart disease risk.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw, typically 12 hours after the last dose, is generally recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of lithium, a mood-stabilizing medication, to help ensure safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Lithium is commonly used to treat bipolar disorder, but it has a narrow therapeutic range, meaning the gap between an effective dose and a toxic one can be small. This test measures lithium levels in the blood to help a prescriber confirm the dose is both effective and safe, which is especially important since lithium levels can be affected by hydration, kidney function, and other medications. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle can matter for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures luteinizing hormone (LH), used to help evaluate fertility, ovulation, and reproductive hormone balance.
Long Description: Luteinizing hormone (LH) is produced by the pituitary gland and plays a key role in triggering ovulation in women and stimulating testosterone production in men. This test is commonly used to help evaluate fertility concerns, irregular menstrual cycles, suspected menopause, and certain pituitary or gonadal conditions, typically alongside FSH and other reproductive hormones.

Short Description: Measures total hemolytic complement (CH50), used to help evaluate the overall activity of the immune system's complement pathway.
Long Description: The complement system is a group of blood proteins that work together as part of the immune response to fight infection and clear damaged cells. The CH50 test measures the overall functional activity of this system as a whole, and is used to help screen for inherited or acquired complement deficiencies, which can be associated with recurrent infections or certain autoimmune conditions such as lupus. A low or absent result may prompt testing of individual complement components to pinpoint the specific deficiency.

Short Description: Measures magnesium, an essential mineral used to help evaluate muscle, nerve, heart, and metabolic function.
Long Description: Magnesium is involved in hundreds of processes in the body, including muscle and nerve function, heart rhythm, and blood sugar regulation. Low magnesium can cause symptoms like muscle cramps, weakness, or abnormal heart rhythms, and can be related to poor diet, certain medications, gastrointestinal conditions, or kidney issues, while high magnesium is less common and often related to kidney problems or excess supplementation. It's frequently included in metabolic panels or ordered when these symptoms are present.

Short Description: Measures magnesium levels inside red blood cells, offering a different picture of the body's magnesium status than a standard blood test.
Long Description: Unlike standard serum magnesium, which reflects only a small fraction of the body's total magnesium, RBC (red blood cell) magnesium is thought by some practitioners to better reflect the magnesium stored inside cells, where most of the body's magnesium actually resides. It's often used in wellness or functional-medicine settings to further explore magnesium status, particularly when symptoms suggest a deficiency but standard serum magnesium levels appear normal.

Patient Prep: A random urine sample is generally used; specific handling instructions may apply since results can change with light or air exposure — follow your provider's or lab's instructions.
Short Description: Older urine test that checks for melanin, historically used to help evaluate advanced melanoma.
Long Description: This test checks urine for melanin or melanin-related compounds (melanogens), which can appear when advanced melanoma is present and cause urine to darken on exposure to air. It's a long-standing but now largely outdated test — modern imaging, blood-based tumor markers, and tissue biopsy are generally considered more reliable for detecting and managing melanoma today, so this test is rarely used in current clinical practice.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required.
Short Description: Measures blood mercury levels, used to screen for and evaluate mercury exposure or toxicity.
Long Description: This test measures the amount of mercury in the blood, which reflects relatively recent exposure (blood mercury mainly captures exposure over the past few months, with certain forms lasting longer than others). It's commonly used to evaluate suspected mercury exposure from sources like certain seafood, dental amalgam, occupational exposure, or environmental contamination, and to help investigate symptoms that could be related to mercury toxicity.

Short Description: Screening test for infectious mononucleosis ('mono'), often called a monospot test.
Long Description: This test (commonly known as a 'monospot' test) detects heterophile antibodies, which typically appear during infectious mononucleosis caused by Epstein-Barr virus (EBV). It's a quick screening tool for patients with symptoms like sore throat, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, and fever suggestive of mono; because heterophile antibodies can take a week or two to develop and aren't present in all cases (especially in young children), a negative result early in the illness doesn't fully rule out mono, and more specific EBV antibody testing may be used if mono is still suspected.

Short Description: Measures myoglobin, an early marker of muscle injury, sometimes used in heart attack or muscle damage evaluations.
Long Description: Myoglobin is a protein released into the blood quickly after muscle injury, including heart muscle damage, making it one of the earliest markers to rise after a heart attack — though it's not specific to the heart, since it also rises with skeletal muscle injury. It's sometimes used alongside more specific cardiac markers (like troponin) in the early hours of a suspected heart attack, or to help evaluate significant muscle breakdown (rhabdomyolysis).

Short Description: Measures blood osmolality, used to help evaluate the body's fluid and electrolyte balance.
Long Description: Osmolality reflects the concentration of dissolved particles (like sodium, glucose, and urea) in the blood, and is a sensitive marker of the body's fluid balance. This test is used to help evaluate suspected dehydration or fluid overload, unusual sodium levels, certain toxin exposures (like alcohol or antifreeze ingestion), and hormone-related fluid disorders, sometimes together with urine osmolality for a fuller picture.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider may request a specific collection (first-morning, random, or timed).
Short Description: Measures urine osmolality, used to help evaluate the kidneys' ability to concentrate or dilute urine.
Long Description: Urine osmolality reflects how concentrated or dilute a urine sample is, based on the number of dissolved particles present. This test is used to help evaluate how well the kidneys are regulating water balance, and is often ordered together with blood osmolality and sodium levels to help investigate conditions such as diabetes insipidus, syndrome of inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH), dehydration, or unexplained sodium abnormalities.

Patient Prep: Specific collection instructions (such as avoiding certain foods, medications, or barium studies beforehand) may apply — follow your provider's or lab's guidance. Multiple samples on different days are sometimes requested.
Short Description: Stool test that checks for intestinal parasites and their eggs, used to help diagnose parasitic gastrointestinal infections.
Long Description: The ova and parasite (O&P) exam examines a stool sample under the microscope for parasites and parasite eggs that can cause gastrointestinal infection. It's commonly ordered for patients with persistent diarrhea, unexplained gastrointestinal symptoms, recent travel to areas with higher parasite exposure risk, or known exposure to contaminated food or water. Because some parasites are shed intermittently, multiple stool samples collected on different days are sometimes needed for accurate detection.

Short Description: Measures angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE), most often used to help support the diagnosis and monitoring of sarcoidosis.
Long Description: Angiotensin converting enzyme (ACE) is often elevated in sarcoidosis, an inflammatory condition that can affect the lungs, lymph nodes, and other organs. This test is commonly used to help support a sarcoidosis diagnosis when the condition is suspected based on symptoms or imaging, and to help monitor disease activity over time, though it's not specific enough to sarcoidosis to be used as a stand-alone diagnostic test.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for past exposure or immune response to adenovirus, a common cause of respiratory and other infections.
Long Description: Adenoviruses are a common group of viruses that can cause a range of illnesses, including respiratory infections, conjunctivitis, and gastrointestinal symptoms. This antibody test detects the immune system's response to adenovirus exposure, and can help support the diagnosis of a current or recent infection, particularly by comparing antibody levels over time (paired samples), or help confirm past exposure.

Specimen Type: Swab (site depends on symptoms - genital, throat, or rectal, per your provider)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Culture-based test that detects Chlamydia trachomatis, a common sexually transmitted infection.
Long Description: This test grows a sample on culture media to detect Chlamydia trachomatis, the bacterium responsible for one of the most common sexually transmitted infections. While many labs now favor faster molecular (NAAT/PCR) testing for chlamydia, culture can still be used in specific clinical situations, including certain non-genital specimen types or cases requiring confirmation. It's typically ordered for patients with symptoms of a sexually transmitted infection, as part of routine STD screening, or when a sexual partner has been diagnosed with chlamydia.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgE antibody response to Penicillin G, helping evaluate a possible penicillin allergy.
Long Description: This test (a RAST-type allergy test) measures IgE antibodies specific to Penicillin G, one of the components of penicillin that can trigger allergic reactions. It's used to help evaluate a suspected penicillin allergy, particularly for patients with a history of reaction to penicillin-class antibiotics, and is often ordered alongside a related test for Penicillin V and other penicillin determinants to build a fuller picture, since a negative result doesn't completely rule out penicillin allergy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgE antibody response to Penicillin V, helping evaluate a possible penicillin allergy.
Long Description: This test (a RAST-type allergy test) measures IgE antibodies specific to Penicillin V, one of the components of penicillin that can trigger allergic reactions. It's typically ordered alongside Penicillin G and other penicillin allergy determinants to help evaluate a suspected penicillin allergy, especially in patients with a history of reaction to penicillin-class antibiotics; results are interpreted together with clinical history rather than on their own.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of phenytoin (Dilantin), an anti-seizure medication, to help confirm safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Phenytoin is used to help control certain seizure types, and has a relatively narrow therapeutic range along with dose-dependent metabolism, meaning small dose changes can sometimes cause larger-than-expected changes in blood levels. This test measures phenytoin concentration in the blood to help a prescriber confirm the medication is working at an effective, non-toxic level, which can be especially useful when adjusting doses, monitoring compliance, or evaluating side effects. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Measures phosphate, a mineral used to help evaluate bone, kidney, and parathyroid health.
Long Description: Phosphate (phosphorus) works closely with calcium to support bone health, energy production, and cell function, and its levels are regulated by the kidneys, parathyroid hormone, and vitamin D. Abnormal phosphate levels can be related to kidney disease, parathyroid disorders, certain nutritional issues, or vitamin D imbalance. It's a routine component of comprehensive metabolic panels and is often interpreted alongside calcium.

Short Description: Measures platelet count, the blood cells responsible for clotting, used to help evaluate bleeding or clotting risk.
Long Description: Platelets are blood cell fragments essential for normal clotting. A low platelet count (thrombocytopenia) can increase bleeding risk and may be linked to bone marrow conditions, certain medications, autoimmune disease, or infection, while a high count (thrombocytosis) can be related to inflammation, iron deficiency, or certain blood disorders. Platelet count is a standard part of a complete blood count (CBC) and routine screening.

Short Description: Measures potassium, an essential electrolyte used to help evaluate heart, muscle, and kidney function.
Long Description: Potassium is critical for normal heart rhythm, muscle function, and nerve signaling, and is tightly regulated by the kidneys. Both low and high potassium levels can be serious, potentially causing muscle weakness or dangerous heart rhythm changes, and can be related to kidney disease, certain medications (like diuretics), dehydration, or other metabolic conditions. It's a routine part of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels.

Patient Prep: Timing relative to the menstrual cycle (or pregnancy stage) matters for interpretation — follow your provider's specific timing instructions.
Short Description: Measures progesterone, a reproductive hormone used to help evaluate ovulation, fertility, and early pregnancy health.
Long Description: Progesterone is a hormone that plays a key role in preparing and maintaining the uterine lining for pregnancy. This test is commonly used to help confirm whether ovulation has occurred, support fertility evaluations, monitor early pregnancy, or investigate irregular menstrual cycles, with results interpreted based on where a person is in their cycle or pregnancy.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is often preferred, and avoiding stress, exercise, or nipple stimulation beforehand may be recommended, since these can temporarily raise levels — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures prolactin, a pituitary hormone, used to help evaluate irregular periods, fertility issues, or unexplained milk production.
Long Description: Prolactin is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland best known for stimulating breast milk production, but it can also affect menstrual cycles and fertility when elevated. This test is commonly used to help evaluate irregular or absent periods, fertility difficulties, unexplained nipple discharge, or symptoms suggestive of a pituitary tumor (prolactinoma), which is one of the more common causes of significantly elevated prolactin.

Short Description: Separates blood proteins into distinct fractions, used to help evaluate certain immune, liver, kidney, or blood conditions.
Long Description: Protein electrophoresis separates the proteins in blood (such as albumin and different globulin fractions) based on their electrical charge, producing a pattern that can reveal abnormalities not visible from a total protein level alone. It's commonly used to help investigate conditions such as multiple myeloma and other plasma cell disorders, chronic inflammation or infection, certain liver and kidney diseases, and unexplained abnormal total protein results.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of primidone, an anti-seizure medication, to help confirm safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Primidone is used to help control certain seizure types and is partly converted by the body into another anti-seizure medication, phenobarbital. This test measures primidone concentration in the blood to help a prescriber confirm the medication is at an effective, well-tolerated level — particularly useful for monitoring compliance, adjusting doses, or investigating side effects. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Measures total protein in the blood, a general marker used to help evaluate nutrition, liver, kidney, and immune health.
Long Description: Total protein reflects the combined amount of albumin and globulins (a broad category of immune and transport proteins) in the blood. Abnormal levels can point toward liver or kidney disease, malnutrition, dehydration, chronic inflammation, or certain immune conditions.
