It's a routine component of comprehensive metabolic panels and is typically interpreted alongside albumin and, if needed, protein electrophoresis for more detail.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures total protein lost in urine over 24 hours, alongside creatinine, used to help evaluate kidney health.
Long Description: This test measures how much protein is excreted in urine over a full 24-hour period, along with creatinine to help confirm the collection was complete and adequately timed. Elevated urine protein (proteinuria) can be an early sign of kidney disease, and this test is often used to help diagnose and monitor kidney conditions, evaluate high blood pressure or diabetes-related kidney effects, or investigate an abnormal urinalysis screening result.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should not be drawn from an arm currently receiving IV fluids or heparin, if avoidable.
Short Description: Measures activated partial thromboplastin time (PTT), a clotting test used to help evaluate bleeding disorders and monitor certain blood thinners.
Long Description: Activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) measures how long it takes blood to clot through one of the body's two major clotting pathways. It's commonly used to help evaluate unexplained bleeding or bruising, investigate suspected clotting factor deficiencies (such as hemophilia) or lupus anticoagulant, and monitor patients on certain blood-thinning medications like heparin.

Short Description: Measures the number of red blood cells, used to help evaluate anemia and other blood conditions.
Long Description: The red blood cell (RBC) count reflects how many oxygen-carrying red blood cells are present in a given volume of blood. It's a core component of a complete blood count (CBC) and helps evaluate anemia (low count), dehydration or certain blood disorders that raise red blood cell numbers (high count), and is typically interpreted alongside hemoglobin, hematocrit, and red cell indices for a fuller picture.

Short Description: Determines ABO blood type, most commonly used before blood transfusion, surgery, or as part of prenatal testing.
Long Description: ABO blood grouping identifies which of the four major blood types (A, B, AB, or O) a person has, which is essential information before receiving a blood transfusion or certain medical procedures, and is a standard part of prenatal testing to help anticipate and manage potential blood type incompatibilities between a mother and her baby.

Short Description: Determines Rh factor (positive or negative), commonly used in prenatal care and before blood transfusion.
Long Description: Rh typing determines whether a person's red blood cells carry the Rh (D) antigen, making them Rh-positive or Rh-negative. This is especially important during pregnancy, since an Rh-negative mother carrying an Rh-positive baby may need preventive treatment (such as RhoGAM) to avoid future pregnancy complications, and it's also essential information for safe blood transfusion matching.

Short Description: Measures reticulocyte count, an indicator of how actively the bone marrow is producing new red blood cells.
Long Description: Reticulocytes are young, newly made red blood cells, so counting them reflects how actively the bone marrow is producing new cells. This test is commonly used to help evaluate anemia — for example, distinguishing anemia caused by reduced production (low reticulocyte count) from anemia caused by increased blood loss or red cell destruction (high reticulocyte count) — and to monitor recovery after treatment for anemia or bone marrow suppression.

Short Description: Antibody screening test used to detect red blood cell antibodies, with automatic follow-up identification and titer testing if positive.
Long Description: This test screens the blood for unexpected antibodies against red blood cell antigens, which can be important in pregnancy (to assess risk to the baby) and before blood transfusion (to help ensure compatible blood is used). If the initial screen is positive, the lab automatically performs further testing to identify the specific antibody and measure its titer (strength), helping guide clinical management.

Short Description: Screening test for syphilis, with automatic confirmatory testing if the initial result is positive, used for diagnosis and treatment monitoring.
Long Description: The RPR (rapid plasma reagin) test screens for antibodies associated with syphilis infection. This version includes reflex testing, meaning a positive screen automatically triggers a confirmatory treponemal antibody test to help rule out a false positive before reporting a confirmed result. RPR titers are also commonly used to monitor how well syphilis treatment is working over time, since levels are expected to decline with successful treatment.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to rubella (German measles), often used in prenatal and pre-pregnancy screening.
Long Description: This test detects rubella (German measles) IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from past infection or vaccination. It's a standard part of prenatal care because rubella infection during pregnancy can cause serious birth defects, so confirming immunity helps guide whether vaccination or extra precautions are needed. It's also used more generally to confirm immune status before certain jobs, travel, or medical procedures.

Product Name (Source): QST0000809 SED RATE BY MOD WEST
Short Description: Measures erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), a general marker of inflammation in the body.
Long Description: The sedimentation rate (ESR, 'sed rate') measures how quickly red blood cells settle in a tube over one hour — a faster rate can indicate increased inflammation somewhere in the body. It's a nonspecific but useful screening tool for inflammatory or autoimmune conditions (such as rheumatoid arthritis or certain vasculitis), infections, and some cancers, and is often used together with other markers (like CRP) and monitored over time to track disease activity or treatment response.

Short Description: Measures AST, a liver (and heart/muscle) enzyme used to help evaluate liver health.
Long Description: Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) is an enzyme found in the liver, as well as heart and muscle tissue, so elevated levels can point to liver damage but can also be affected by muscle injury or heart conditions. It's a standard part of liver function panels, typically interpreted alongside ALT and other liver markers to help evaluate suspected liver disease, medication effects, or alcohol-related liver stress.

Short Description: Measures ALT, a liver enzyme used as one of the most specific markers of liver cell injury.
Long Description: Alanine aminotransferase (ALT) is an enzyme found predominantly in the liver, making it one of the more specific markers of liver cell damage compared to AST. It's a standard part of liver function panels and general wellness screening, commonly used to help evaluate suspected liver disease, monitor medications known to affect the liver, or investigate symptoms like fatigue or abdominal discomfort.

Short Description: Screening test for sickle cell trait or disease, an inherited condition affecting red blood cells.
Long Description: This test screens for the presence of hemoglobin S, the abnormal form of hemoglobin responsible for sickle cell trait and sickle cell disease, an inherited condition that causes red blood cells to become rigid and sickle-shaped under certain conditions. It's commonly used for newborn screening, pre-surgical or anesthesia risk assessment, and evaluating patients with a family history or symptoms suggestive of sickle cell disease. A positive screen is typically followed by hemoglobin electrophoresis to confirm and further characterize the result.

Short Description: Measures sodium, the body's main electrolyte for fluid balance, used to help evaluate hydration and metabolic health.
Long Description: Sodium is the primary electrolyte responsible for regulating fluid balance, blood pressure, and nerve and muscle function. Abnormal sodium levels can be caused by dehydration, excess fluid intake, kidney or hormone-related conditions, or certain medications, and can range from mild to medically significant. It's a routine component of basic and comprehensive metabolic panels.

Short Description: Measures beta-2-microglobulin, a protein used to help evaluate certain blood cancers and monitor kidney function.
Long Description: Beta-2-microglobulin is a small protein found on the surface of most cells and filtered out by the kidneys. Elevated levels can be seen in certain blood cancers such as multiple myeloma and some lymphomas, where it's used as a prognostic marker, as well as in reduced kidney function, since the kidneys normally clear it from the blood. It's typically ordered alongside other tests as part of a cancer workup or kidney function evaluation, rather than as a stand-alone diagnostic test.

Short Description: Measures total T3 (triiodothyronine), a thyroid hormone, used to help evaluate thyroid function.
Long Description: Triiodothyronine (T3) is one of the main hormones produced by the thyroid gland and plays a central role in regulating metabolism. This test measures total T3 levels and is commonly used, alongside TSH and T4, to help evaluate suspected hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid) or investigate persistent thyroid symptoms when other thyroid tests are inconclusive.

Short Description: Older thyroid test used alongside other thyroid hormone levels to help estimate free thyroid hormone activity.
Long Description: T3 uptake is a test that indirectly reflects how much of the thyroid hormone-binding protein in the blood is already occupied, helping providers estimate the proportion of thyroid hormone that's 'free' (biologically active) versus bound. It's most useful when interpreted together with total T4 (to calculate a free thyroxine index) and is less commonly ordered today, since direct free T4 tests are now widely available and often preferred.

Short Description: Measures free T4, the active form of the main thyroid hormone, used to help evaluate thyroid function.
Long Description: Free thyroxine (free T4) reflects the amount of active, unbound thyroid hormone available for the body to use, making it one of the most useful direct measures of thyroid function. This test is commonly used, together with TSH, to help diagnose and monitor hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) and hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid), and to guide thyroid medication dosing.

Short Description: Measures total T4 (thyroxine), a thyroid hormone, used to help evaluate thyroid function.
Long Description: Total thyroxine (T4) measures both the free (active) and protein-bound forms of this thyroid hormone. Because binding protein levels can affect total T4 results independent of actual thyroid function (for example, during pregnancy or with certain medications), free T4 is now more commonly used as the primary measure, though total T4 may still be ordered as part of some thyroid panels or when historical results need to be compared.

Short Description: Measures thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG), a carrier protein, used to help correctly interpret thyroid hormone test results.
Long Description: Thyroxine-binding globulin (TBG) is the main protein that carries thyroid hormone in the blood. Because changes in TBG levels (from pregnancy, certain medications, liver disease, or inherited conditions) can affect total T4 and T3 results without reflecting an actual change in thyroid function, measuring TBG helps a provider correctly interpret confusing or inconsistent thyroid test results.

Patient Prep: Morning collection (before 10 AM) is generally recommended, since testosterone levels naturally peak earlier in the day.
Short Description: Measures total testosterone, used to help evaluate low testosterone, fertility concerns, and certain hormone-related conditions.
Long Description: Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone, though it's also present and important in women. This test is commonly used to help evaluate symptoms of low testosterone in men (such as low energy, reduced libido, or muscle loss), investigate irregular periods or excess hair growth in women (which can relate to elevated testosterone), and support fertility or broader hormone evaluations. Because testosterone naturally varies through the day, morning collection is generally recommended for consistent results.

Short Description: Measures transferrin, an iron-carrying protein, used alongside other tests to help evaluate iron status.
Long Description: Transferrin is the main protein responsible for transporting iron through the blood. This test is typically ordered together with serum iron and ferritin to help calculate total iron-binding capacity and transferrin saturation, giving a fuller picture of whether a patient has iron deficiency, iron overload, or another cause of abnormal iron-related results, such as chronic disease or liver conditions.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results, consistent with standard lipid testing.
Short Description: Measures triglycerides, a type of fat in the blood, used as part of cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: Triglycerides are the most common type of fat in the bloodstream, and elevated levels are associated with increased cardiovascular risk and, at very high levels, risk of pancreatitis. This test is a standard component of a lipid panel, interpreted alongside total cholesterol, LDL, and HDL to help build a full picture of heart disease risk and guide lifestyle or treatment decisions.

Short Description: Measures thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH), the primary screening test for thyroid function.
Long Description: Thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is produced by the pituitary gland to regulate thyroid hormone production, and is considered the most sensitive first-line test for thyroid function. Elevated TSH generally suggests an underactive thyroid (hypothyroidism), while low TSH suggests an overactive thyroid (hyperthyroidism). It's widely used for thyroid screening, diagnosis, and monitoring of thyroid medication dosing, often alongside free T4.

Short Description: Measures uric acid, used to help evaluate gout and certain kidney conditions.
Long Description: Uric acid is a waste product formed from the breakdown of purines (found in certain foods and produced naturally by the body). Elevated uric acid levels are associated with gout, a painful form of arthritis caused by uric acid crystal buildup in joints, and can also relate to kidney stones or reduced kidney function. This test is commonly used to help diagnose and monitor gout, evaluate kidney stone risk, or investigate unexplained joint pain.

Product Name (Source): QST0000906 Coccidioides Antibody, Complement Fixation, Serum
Short Description: Antibody test that helps diagnose Coccidioides infection (Valley Fever), a fungal infection most common in the southwestern U.S.
Long Description: Coccidioidomycosis (commonly called Valley Fever) is a fungal infection caused by Coccidioides species, most often acquired by inhaling spores found in soil in parts of the southwestern United States and other arid regions. This complement fixation antibody test helps detect the immune response to the infection and can also give a general sense of disease severity, since higher antibody titers are often associated with more extensive infection. It's typically ordered for patients with respiratory symptoms and relevant geographic exposure or travel history.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of valproic acid (Depakote), used for seizures and mood stabilization, to help confirm safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Valproic acid is used to treat certain seizure disorders, bipolar disorder, and migraine prevention. This test measures the drug's concentration in the blood to help a prescriber confirm the dose is effective and well-tolerated, which is especially useful for checking compliance, adjusting doses, or monitoring for potential toxicity, since valproic acid can affect the liver and blood counts. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Measures vitamin A levels, used to help evaluate suspected vitamin A deficiency or excess.
Long Description: Vitamin A is important for vision, immune function, and skin health. This test measures blood vitamin A levels and is used to help evaluate suspected deficiency (which can cause vision problems and increased infection risk, often related to malabsorption conditions or poor diet) or excess (which can occur with high-dose supplementation and cause its own set of symptoms).

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though your provider may request it depending on what else is being tested.
Short Description: Measures vitamin B6 levels, used to help evaluate suspected deficiency related to nerve, skin, or blood symptoms.
Long Description: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) plays a role in nerve function, red blood cell production, and metabolism. This test is used to help evaluate suspected B6 deficiency, which can cause symptoms such as nerve tingling or numbness (peripheral neuropathy), skin changes, or certain types of anemia, and can be related to poor diet, alcohol use, malabsorption, or certain medications.

Short Description: Measures vitamin B12 levels, used to help evaluate fatigue, certain types of anemia, and nerve-related symptoms.
Long Description: Vitamin B12 is essential for red blood cell production and nervous system health. Low B12 can cause megaloblastic anemia, fatigue, and neurological symptoms like numbness, tingling, or balance problems, and is often related to dietary insufficiency (especially in vegetarians/vegans), malabsorption conditions, or pernicious anemia. This test is commonly ordered to evaluate these symptoms or as part of routine wellness screening.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — special handling may be required
Patient Prep: Specimen may need to be protected from light and processed/frozen quickly, since vitamin C degrades rapidly — follow your provider's or lab's specific handling instructions.
Short Description: Measures vitamin C levels, used to help evaluate suspected vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) or nutritional status.
Long Description: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is important for collagen formation, wound healing, and immune function. This test is used to help evaluate suspected vitamin C deficiency, which can cause symptoms like fatigue, gum problems, poor wound healing, and easy bruising in more severe cases (scurvy), and is often considered in the context of poor diet, malabsorption, smoking, or certain chronic illnesses that increase vitamin C needs.

Short Description: Measures vitamin E levels, used to help evaluate suspected deficiency, often related to fat malabsorption.
Long Description: Vitamin E is a fat-soluble antioxidant important for protecting cells from oxidative damage and supporting immune and nerve function. Because it's absorbed along with dietary fat, deficiency is most often related to conditions that impair fat absorption (such as certain liver, pancreatic, or intestinal disorders) rather than diet alone. This test is used to help evaluate suspected deficiency, particularly in patients with these underlying conditions or unexplained neurological symptoms.

Short Description: Measures white blood cell (WBC) count, used to help evaluate infection, inflammation, and immune system health.
Long Description: The white blood cell (WBC) count reflects the number of infection-fighting cells circulating in the blood. An elevated count often points to infection, inflammation, or stress on the body, while a low count can indicate reduced immune function from various causes, including certain medications, viral infections, or bone marrow conditions. It's a core component of a complete blood count (CBC) and one of the most frequently ordered lab tests.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though your provider may request it.
Short Description: Measures zinc, an essential trace mineral, used to help evaluate nutritional status and certain deficiency symptoms.
Long Description: Zinc plays an important role in immune function, wound healing, and taste and smell. This test is used to help evaluate suspected zinc deficiency, which can cause symptoms such as poor wound healing, hair loss, reduced taste or smell, or skin changes, often related to poor diet, malabsorption conditions, or increased needs during illness or growth.

Short Description: Measures zinc protoporphyrin, used to help screen for lead exposure and iron deficiency.
Long Description: Zinc protoporphyrin (ZPP) builds up in red blood cells when the body can't properly incorporate iron into heme (the oxygen-carrying part of hemoglobin) — a situation that occurs in both iron deficiency and lead poisoning, since lead interferes with the same process. This test is commonly used for occupational lead exposure screening and, in some settings, as an additional marker when evaluating iron deficiency, though it doesn't distinguish between the two causes on its own.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to measles, often used in prenatal, employment, or school-related screening.
Long Description: This test detects measles (rubeola) IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from past infection or vaccination. It's commonly used to confirm immune status before pregnancy, for healthcare workers or students who need to document immunity, or when there's uncertainty about a person's vaccination history or past exposure to measles.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (CEA) most often used to help monitor colorectal and certain other cancers.
Long Description: Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is a protein that can be elevated in several types of cancer, most notably colorectal cancer, though it can also rise with certain other cancers and some non-cancerous conditions (like smoking or inflammatory bowel disease). It's primarily used to help monitor known cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence, alongside imaging and clinical evaluation, rather than as a stand-alone screening test for the general population.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma), fasting and then timed samples after a glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a glucose solution, with blood drawn at set time intervals afterward — plan for the appointment to take a couple of hours.
Short Description: Glucose tolerance test used to screen for gestational diabetes during pregnancy.
Long Description: This test checks how the body processes sugar by measuring blood glucose while fasting and then at set intervals after drinking a standardized glucose solution. It's the standard way to screen for and diagnose gestational diabetes, a form of diabetes that can develop during pregnancy, typically ordered between 24-28 weeks of pregnancy or earlier if risk factors are present.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures how much calcium is excreted in urine over 24 hours, alongside creatinine, used to help evaluate calcium and kidney health.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of calcium excreted in urine over a full 24-hour period, along with creatinine to help confirm the collection was complete. It's commonly used to help evaluate kidney stone risk, parathyroid disorders, and other conditions affecting calcium balance, since urine calcium can be abnormally high or low depending on the underlying cause.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; a random urine sample is typically used.
Short Description: Measures protein in a random urine sample, alongside creatinine, used as a quicker alternative to a 24-hour collection for evaluating kidney health.
Long Description: This test measures protein and creatinine in a single, randomly collected urine sample, and calculates a protein-to-creatinine ratio that estimates 24-hour protein excretion without requiring a full-day collection. It's commonly used to help screen for and monitor kidney disease, evaluate high blood pressure or diabetes-related kidney effects, or follow up on an abnormal urinalysis.

Product Name (Source): QST0001748 O AND P W/GIARDIA AG
Patient Prep: Specific collection instructions may apply — follow your provider's or lab's guidance. Multiple samples on different days are sometimes requested.
Short Description: Stool test that checks for intestinal parasites and their eggs, plus a targeted antigen test for Giardia.
Long Description: This combines a standard ova and parasite (O&P) microscopic stool exam with a Giardia antigen test, which is more sensitive for detecting this specific parasite than microscopy alone. It's commonly ordered for patients with persistent diarrhea, gastrointestinal symptoms after travel, or known exposure to contaminated water, since Giardia is one of the more common causes of parasitic gastrointestinal infection.

Short Description: Complete blood count (CBC) — a core panel that evaluates red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets as part of routine and diagnostic testing.
Long Description: The complete blood count (CBC) is one of the most commonly ordered lab tests, providing a snapshot of overall blood health by measuring red blood cells (including hemoglobin and hematocrit), white blood cells, and platelets. It's used broadly to help evaluate anemia, infection, inflammation, and clotting-related conditions, and is a routine part of general wellness checkups, pre-surgical workups, and monitoring for many chronic conditions and medications.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Measures Protein C activity, part of a workup for inherited or acquired blood clotting disorders.
Long Description: Protein C is a natural anticoagulant that helps regulate blood clotting. This test measures how well a patient's Protein C is functioning, and low activity — whether inherited or acquired — is associated with an increased risk of abnormal blood clots (venous thromboembolism). It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners and by pregnancy or oral contraceptive use — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Measures Protein S activity, part of a workup for inherited or acquired blood clotting disorders.
Long Description: Protein S works together with Protein C as a natural anticoagulant that helps regulate blood clotting. This test measures how well a patient's Protein S is functioning, and low activity — whether inherited or acquired — is associated with an increased risk of abnormal blood clots (venous thromboembolism). It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Bermuda Grass, a grass pollen allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Bermuda Grass, a common outdoor allergen and frequent cause of seasonal hay fever symptoms (sneezing, itchy or watery eyes, runny or congested nose) during grass pollen season. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Orchard Grass, a common outdoor allergen and frequent cause of seasonal hay fever symptoms (sneezing, itchy or watery eyes, runny or congested nose) during grass pollen season. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Screening antibody test for hepatitis C, with automatic confirmatory testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: Hepatitis C antibody testing checks for exposure to the hepatitis C virus, a common cause of chronic liver disease. This version includes reflex testing: if the initial antibody screen is positive, the lab automatically runs a confirmatory test (checking for active virus) to distinguish a current infection from a past, resolved one — since antibodies can remain positive even after the virus is gone. It's commonly used for routine hepatitis C screening, risk-based testing, or investigating abnormal liver enzymes.

Product Name (Source): QST0003020 UA, COMP with RFL CULT
Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended; follow your provider's collection instructions.
Short Description: Complete urinalysis with automatic follow-up culture if results suggest a urinary tract infection.
Long Description: This test combines a complete urinalysis (checking the urine's physical, chemical, and microscopic properties) with reflex culture, meaning if the results suggest infection (such as the presence of bacteria, white blood cells, or nitrites), a urine culture is automatically performed to confirm and identify the causative organism. It's commonly ordered for symptoms like burning with urination, urgency, or pelvic pain, or as part of routine health screening.

Patient Prep: Specific collection method depends on what's being tested for; follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Specialized urine culture used to identify less common organisms not detected by a routine urine culture.
Long Description: This is a specialized urine culture that targets specific organisms beyond what a routine urine culture typically covers, often used when a standard culture doesn't explain a patient's symptoms or when a particular organism is suspected based on clinical history.
