Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required. Follows OSHA-specified reporting requirements for workplace lead exposure monitoring.
Short Description: Measures blood lead levels following OSHA guidelines, used for workplace lead exposure monitoring.
Long Description: This test measures blood lead levels using the specimen handling and reporting standards required under OSHA's occupational lead exposure regulations. It's used for employers and employees in industries with lead exposure risk (such as construction, manufacturing, or battery recycling) to meet regulatory medical surveillance requirements and monitor for lead toxicity over time.

Patient Prep: Collected via masturbation into a sterile container after a period of abstinence (typically 2-5 days) — follow the lab's specific collection and delivery instructions, including how quickly the sample must reach the lab.
Short Description: Semen analysis performed after a vasectomy to confirm the procedure was successful and it's safe to stop using other birth control.
Long Description: This test examines a semen sample under the microscope to check for the presence or absence of sperm after a vasectomy. A result confirming no (or, per some protocols, very few non-motile) sperm indicates the vasectomy was successful, which is the standard way providers confirm it's safe to rely on the procedure alone for contraception. Testing is typically done starting around 8-16 weeks post-procedure, sometimes requiring more than one sample to confirm.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to B. Tropicalis, a dust mite allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to B. Tropicalis, a common indoor allergen linked to year-round allergy and asthma symptoms. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures free (unbound) valproic acid levels, used for more precise drug monitoring in certain patients.
Long Description: Most valproic acid in the blood is bound to protein and inactive; only the free (unbound) portion is biologically active. This test is used instead of, or alongside, total valproic acid when a more precise picture of drug activity is needed — for example, in patients with low albumin, kidney or liver disease, pregnancy, or other conditions that can affect protein binding and make total levels misleading. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to T. Rubrum, a mold allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to T. Rubrum, a mold/fungal allergen that can trigger year-round or seasonal allergy and asthma symptoms, especially in damp environments. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Walnut, a food allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Walnut, a food allergen that can trigger reactions ranging from mild (hives, itching, digestive upset) to severe (anaphylaxis) in sensitive individuals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for past exposure to herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1), commonly associated with oral herpes/cold sores.
Long Description: This test detects IgG antibodies to herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1), which most often causes oral herpes (cold sores) but can also cause genital infections. A positive result indicates past exposure and lifelong infection with the virus, since HSV remains dormant in the body after initial infection. It's commonly used to help evaluate unexplained sores, confirm a suspected diagnosis, or assess exposure status, particularly when type-specific information (distinguishing HSV-1 from HSV-2) is needed.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for past exposure to Toxoplasma gondii, a parasite of particular concern during pregnancy.
Long Description: Toxoplasma gondii is a parasite that most people who are exposed to never notice, but which can pose risks to a developing baby if a person is newly infected during pregnancy, and can cause serious illness in people with weakened immune systems. This IgG antibody test helps determine whether someone has been exposed in the past (and is therefore generally considered immune to new infection) versus having no prior exposure, which is relevant information during pregnancy planning or for immunocompromised patients.

Short Description: Measures the number and/or activity of natural killer (NK) cells, a part of the immune system sometimes evaluated in reproductive immunology or immune deficiency workups.
Long Description: Natural killer (NK) cells are white blood cells that play a role in the body's innate immune defense against infected or abnormal cells. This test measures NK cell levels and, depending on the specific assay, activity, and is used in a few different contexts — including general immune function evaluation, and by some reproductive specialists investigating recurrent pregnancy loss, though the use of NK cell testing in that setting is debated and not universally accepted in mainstream reproductive medicine. Results are best interpreted by a specialist familiar with the specific clinical question being asked.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle matters for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures estradiol, the main form of estrogen, used to help evaluate fertility, menstrual cycle health, and menopause.
Long Description: Estradiol is the primary and most potent form of estrogen in people of reproductive age. This test is commonly used to help evaluate fertility treatment monitoring, irregular menstrual cycles, suspected menopause or perimenopause, and, in men, certain hormone imbalances. Because estradiol levels vary significantly through the menstrual cycle, timing of collection matters for accurate interpretation.

Short Description: Confirmatory antibody test for syphilis, typically used to follow up a positive screening test.
Long Description: The FTA-ABS (fluorescent treponemal antibody absorption) test detects antibodies specific to the bacterium that causes syphilis, and is used as a confirmatory test after a positive initial screening test (such as RPR), to rule out a false-positive result. Unlike RPR, FTA-ABS typically remains positive for life once a person has been infected, even after successful treatment, so it's used to confirm past or current infection rather than to monitor treatment response.

Short Description: Screening test for syphilis, with automatic confirmatory testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: This RPR (rapid plasma reagin) test screens for antibodies associated with syphilis infection, with reflex testing meaning a positive screen automatically triggers a confirmatory treponemal antibody test to help rule out a false positive. RPR is commonly used for syphilis screening and, in patients with known past infection, to help monitor treatment response over time, since titers are expected to decline with successful treatment.

Patient Prep: Requires collection in the morning (generally before 9-10 AM), since cortisol follows a daily pattern and this test is specifically timed to capture the morning level.
Short Description: Measures morning cortisol level, used to help evaluate adrenal gland function at the time cortisol is normally at its highest.
Long Description: Cortisol naturally peaks in the early morning and declines through the day. This test captures the morning level specifically, which is useful for evaluating suspected adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease) — since a low morning cortisol is more concerning than a low afternoon level — or as part of a broader workup for Cushing's syndrome or other adrenal conditions, often compared against an afternoon/evening sample.

Patient Prep: Requires collection in the afternoon or evening, since this test is specifically timed to capture cortisol's normal daily low point.
Short Description: Measures afternoon/evening cortisol level, used alongside morning cortisol to help evaluate the body's normal daily cortisol rhythm.
Long Description: Cortisol is normally lowest in the evening compared to the morning. This test captures the afternoon/evening level specifically, and is often ordered together with a morning cortisol sample to check whether a patient's cortisol still follows this expected daily pattern — a loss of the normal rhythm can be a clue to conditions like Cushing's syndrome.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma), fasting and then a timed sample 1 hour after a glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a glucose solution, with a blood draw exactly 1 hour later — plan for the appointment to take a little over an hour.
Short Description: Glucose tolerance test that checks blood sugar at fasting and 1 hour after a glucose challenge, often used in diabetes or gestational diabetes screening.
Long Description: This test measures blood glucose while fasting and again exactly one hour after drinking a standardized glucose solution, to see how effectively the body processes sugar. It's commonly used as part of gestational diabetes screening during pregnancy, or in a broader glucose tolerance workup for suspected diabetes or prediabetes.

Product Name (Source): QST0004408 C DIFF TOXIN B QL
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample should ideally be collected during active diarrhea symptoms.
Short Description: Stool test that detects C. difficile toxin, used to help diagnose C. diff infection, a common cause of antibiotic-associated diarrhea.
Long Description: Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) is a bacterium that can cause diarrhea and colitis, often following antibiotic use that disrupts normal gut bacteria. This qualitative test detects C. diff toxin B in stool, helping confirm an active infection in patients with compatible symptoms — most notably diarrhea that develops during or after antibiotic treatment, or in a healthcare setting.

Short Description: Antibody test used to help evaluate rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune joint conditions.
Long Description: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an antibody found in many, though not all, people with rheumatoid arthritis, and can also appear in some other autoimmune or chronic conditions, and occasionally in healthy individuals. This test is commonly used to help support a rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis in patients with joint pain, swelling, and stiffness, though it's typically interpreted alongside other tests (like anti-CCP antibodies) and clinical findings, since it's neither fully sensitive nor fully specific on its own.

Short Description: Measures C-reactive protein (CRP), a general marker of inflammation used to help evaluate infection, autoimmune disease, or cardiovascular risk.
Long Description: C-reactive protein (CRP) is produced by the liver and rises quickly in response to inflammation anywhere in the body, making it a sensitive but nonspecific marker. It's commonly used to help evaluate suspected infection or inflammatory/autoimmune conditions, monitor disease activity or treatment response in known inflammatory conditions, and — in a more sensitive form (hs-CRP) — as part of cardiovascular risk assessment.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to varicella-zoster virus (chickenpox), often used to confirm immune status.
Long Description: This test detects varicella-zoster virus (VZV) IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from either past chickenpox infection or vaccination. It's commonly used to confirm immune status when vaccination history is uncertain — for example, before pregnancy, for healthcare workers, or after known exposure to someone with chickenpox or shingles — to help determine whether preventive treatment is needed.

Specimen Type: Swab or tissue/fluid sample, per site being tested
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected before starting antibiotics, from the specific site of suspected infection.
Short Description: General bacterial culture that grows both oxygen-requiring and oxygen-independent bacteria, used to identify the cause of a wound, tissue, or fluid infection.
Long Description: This culture tests a sample (such as from a wound, abscess, or body fluid) for both aerobic bacteria (which need oxygen to grow) and anaerobic bacteria (which grow without oxygen), since many infections — especially deep or wound infections — can involve either or both types. If bacteria grow, susceptibility testing is often performed to identify which antibiotics are likely to be effective, helping guide targeted treatment.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected during active diarrhea symptoms.
Short Description: Stool culture that specifically identifies Campylobacter, a common bacterial cause of gastrointestinal infection.
Long Description: Campylobacter is one of the most common bacterial causes of foodborne gastrointestinal illness, typically causing diarrhea (sometimes bloody), abdominal cramping, and fever. This culture specifically targets Campylobacter growth conditions and is commonly ordered for patients with compatible symptoms, especially after eating undercooked poultry or unpasteurized dairy, or as part of a broader stool pathogen workup.

Specimen Type: Sample type depends on the site being tested (e.g., tissue, body fluid, or other non-skin/hair/nail specimen)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample should be collected from the affected site before starting antifungal treatment when possible.
Short Description: Fungal culture for specimen types other than skin, hair, nails, or blood — used to identify fungal infections at other body sites.
Long Description: This fungal culture and identification test is used for specimen types beyond the common skin, hair, or nail samples — such as tissue, body fluids, or other sites — to detect and identify fungal organisms responsible for an infection. It's typically ordered when a fungal infection is suspected at a site other than the skin, scalp, or nails, based on symptoms or other clinical findings.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures small amounts of albumin in urine over 24 hours, an early marker of kidney damage, especially in diabetes.
Long Description: Microalbumin refers to small amounts of the protein albumin in urine that aren't detectable on a standard urinalysis but can be an early warning sign of kidney damage, particularly in people with diabetes or high blood pressure. This 24-hour collection version measures total microalbumin excretion over a full day, helping detect and monitor early kidney changes before more significant kidney disease develops.

Patient Prep: Requires five separate blood draws, often spaced out per your provider's protocol (such as at intervals through the day or across a cycle) — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) across five separate samples, used to help average out natural hour-to-hour hormone fluctuations.
Long Description: FSH is released in pulses throughout the day, so a single measurement can sometimes be misleading. This test pools or analyzes five separately timed blood samples to give a more representative picture of a patient's average FSH level, which can be useful in certain fertility or pituitary function evaluations where a single reading isn't considered reliable enough.

Patient Prep: Requires five separate blood draws, often spaced out per your provider's protocol (such as at intervals through the day or across a cycle) — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures luteinizing hormone (LH) across five separate samples, used to help average out natural hour-to-hour hormone fluctuations.
Long Description: LH is released in pulses throughout the day, so a single measurement can sometimes be misleading. This test pools or analyzes five separately timed blood samples to give a more representative picture of a patient's average LH level, which can be useful in certain fertility or pituitary function evaluations where a single reading isn't considered reliable enough.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) — timed 2 hours after a meal
Patient Prep: Requires eating a meal (or a specific glucose load, per your provider's instructions) and returning for a blood draw exactly 2 hours later.
Short Description: Measures blood glucose 2 hours after eating, used to help screen for and monitor diabetes.
Long Description: This test measures blood glucose exactly two hours after a meal (postprandial), which helps evaluate how well the body manages blood sugar after eating — a key part of diagnosing and monitoring diabetes and prediabetes, since post-meal glucose control is often affected before fasting glucose becomes abnormal.

Short Description: Measures IgA cardiolipin antibodies, part of a workup for antiphospholipid syndrome, a clotting-related autoimmune condition.
Long Description: Cardiolipin antibodies are part of a group of antiphospholipid antibodies associated with antiphospholipid syndrome, a condition that increases the risk of blood clots and pregnancy complications. This test measures the IgA class specifically, and is typically ordered alongside IgG and IgM cardiolipin antibodies as part of a broader antiphospholipid antibody panel when this syndrome is suspected, such as after unexplained blood clots or recurrent pregnancy loss.

Short Description: Measures IgG cardiolipin antibodies, part of a workup for antiphospholipid syndrome, a clotting-related autoimmune condition.
Long Description: Cardiolipin antibodies are part of a group of antiphospholipid antibodies associated with antiphospholipid syndrome, a condition that increases the risk of blood clots and pregnancy complications. This test measures the IgG class, which is generally considered the most clinically significant of the three antibody types, and is typically ordered alongside IgA and IgM cardiolipin antibodies when this syndrome is suspected, such as after unexplained blood clots or recurrent pregnancy loss.

Short Description: Measures IgM cardiolipin antibodies, part of a workup for antiphospholipid syndrome, a clotting-related autoimmune condition.
Long Description: Cardiolipin antibodies are part of a group of antiphospholipid antibodies associated with antiphospholipid syndrome, a condition that increases the risk of blood clots and pregnancy complications. This test measures the IgM class specifically, and is typically ordered alongside IgA and IgG cardiolipin antibodies as part of a broader antiphospholipid antibody panel when this syndrome is suspected, such as after unexplained blood clots or recurrent pregnancy loss.

Patient Prep: Requires two separate blood draws, often spaced a set number of minutes apart per your provider's protocol, since a single prolactin reading can be affected by stress or the draw itself.
Short Description: Measures prolactin across two separate samples, used to help rule out falsely elevated results from stress or the blood draw process itself.
Long Description: Prolactin can rise temporarily just from the stress of having blood drawn, which can cause a falsely elevated single reading. This protocol collects two separately timed samples (often through an indwelling line, minutes apart) to get a more reliable prolactin level, which is especially useful when an initial elevated result needs to be confirmed before pursuing further workup for conditions like a pituitary tumor.

Patient Prep: Requires three separate blood draws, spaced per your provider's protocol, since a single prolactin reading can be affected by stress or the draw itself.
Short Description: Measures prolactin across three separate samples, used to help rule out falsely elevated results from stress or the blood draw process itself.
Long Description: Prolactin can rise temporarily just from the stress of having blood drawn, which can cause a falsely elevated single reading. This protocol collects three separately timed samples to get a more reliable average prolactin level, which is especially useful when an initial elevated result needs to be confirmed before pursuing further workup for conditions like a pituitary tumor.

Patient Prep: Requires four separate blood draws, spaced per your provider's protocol, since a single prolactin reading can be affected by stress or the draw itself.
Short Description: Measures prolactin across four separate samples, used to help rule out falsely elevated results from stress or the blood draw process itself.
Long Description: Prolactin can rise temporarily just from the stress of having blood drawn, which can cause a falsely elevated single reading. This protocol collects four separately timed samples to get a more reliable average prolactin level, which is especially useful when an initial elevated result needs to be confirmed before pursuing further workup for conditions like a pituitary tumor.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (CA 19-9) most often used to help monitor pancreatic and certain other gastrointestinal cancers.
Long Description: CA 19-9 is a protein that can be elevated in pancreatic cancer and some other gastrointestinal cancers, though it can also rise with non-cancerous conditions like gallstones or pancreatitis. It's primarily used to help monitor known cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence, alongside imaging, rather than as a stand-alone screening test for the general population, since it's not sensitive or specific enough to diagnose cancer on its own.

Patient Prep: Requires five separate blood draws, spaced per your provider's protocol, since a single prolactin reading can be affected by stress or the draw itself.
Short Description: Measures prolactin across five separate samples, used to help rule out falsely elevated results from stress or the blood draw process itself.
Long Description: Prolactin can rise temporarily just from the stress of having blood drawn, which can cause a falsely elevated single reading. This protocol collects five separately timed samples to get a more reliable average prolactin level, which is especially useful when an initial elevated result needs to be confirmed before pursuing further workup for conditions like a pituitary tumor.

Short Description: Measures prealbumin, a short-term marker of nutritional status, often used to help evaluate recent protein/nutrition intake.
Long Description: Prealbumin (transthyretin) is a protein made by the liver with a much shorter half-life than albumin, making it a more sensitive indicator of recent changes in nutritional status. This test is commonly used in hospital or clinical settings to help evaluate malnutrition, monitor nutritional support (such as for hospitalized patients or those on specialized nutrition plans), and track short-term response to nutritional interventions.

Product Name (Source): QST0004848 HEP B CORE IGM AB
Short Description: Antibody test used to help identify a recent or acute hepatitis B infection.
Long Description: Hepatitis B core IgM antibody appears during an acute hepatitis B infection and is one of the markers used to distinguish a new, recent infection from a chronic, longstanding one. It's typically ordered as part of a hepatitis B panel when acute infection is suspected, particularly in patients with symptoms of acute hepatitis or a known recent exposure.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks immunity levels to tetanus, sometimes used to help guide booster vaccination decisions.
Long Description: This test measures antibody levels against tetanus toxoid, reflecting how much protective immunity a person has from past tetanus vaccination. It's used in specific clinical situations — such as wound management, occupational exposure evaluation, or uncertainty about vaccination history — to help a provider decide whether a booster vaccine is needed.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks immunity levels to diphtheria, sometimes used to help guide booster vaccination decisions.
Long Description: This test measures antibody levels against diphtheria toxoid, reflecting how much protective immunity a person has from past diphtheria vaccination. It's used in specific clinical situations — such as uncertainty about vaccination history, occupational requirements, or before travel to areas where diphtheria is more common — to help a provider decide whether a booster vaccine is needed.

Short Description: Antibody test (anti-Scl-70) that helps support the diagnosis of scleroderma, an autoimmune connective tissue disease.
Long Description: Anti-Scl-70 (anti-topoisomerase I) antibodies are strongly associated with systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), particularly the more widespread, diffuse form of the disease, which can affect the skin, lungs, and other organs. This test is commonly ordered when a patient has symptoms suggestive of scleroderma, such as skin thickening, Raynaud's phenomenon, or unexplained lung involvement, to help support the diagnosis alongside clinical evaluation and other autoimmune antibody testing.

Specimen Type: Swab (site depends on symptoms - genital, eye, or other, per your provider)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Rapid antigen test that detects Chlamydia trachomatis directly from a sample, used to help diagnose chlamydia infection.
Long Description: This test uses direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) staining to detect Chlamydia trachomatis antigens directly in a patient sample, offering a relatively quick way to check for infection. While many labs now favor molecular (NAAT/PCR) testing for chlamydia due to higher sensitivity, this method can still be used in specific situations, including certain eye infections (like chlamydial conjunctivitis) where it remains a useful diagnostic option.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for hepatitis D (delta virus), an infection that only occurs in people who also have hepatitis B.
Long Description: Hepatitis D (delta) virus is a unique virus that can only infect people who already have hepatitis B, and when it does, it often makes the liver disease more severe. This antibody test is used to check for hepatitis D infection in patients known to have hepatitis B, particularly those with unusually severe or rapidly worsening liver disease, since hepatitis D isn't part of routine hepatitis screening otherwise.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps evaluate suspected immune-related low platelet count (immune thrombocytopenia).
Long Description: This test checks for antibodies directly bound to a patient's platelets, which can cause the immune system to destroy them, leading to a low platelet count (thrombocytopenia). It's used to help support a diagnosis of immune thrombocytopenia (ITP) or investigate other causes of unexplained low platelets, typically as part of a broader hematology workup alongside a complete blood count and clinical evaluation.

Short Description: Measures vitamin B1 (thiamine) levels, used to help evaluate suspected deficiency related to nerve, heart, or metabolic symptoms.
Long Description: Vitamin B1 (thiamine) is essential for converting food into energy and supporting nerve function. Deficiency, though uncommon in developed countries with fortified foods, can occur with poor diet, alcohol use disorder, or certain gastrointestinal conditions, and can cause serious nerve and heart-related symptoms (such as in beriberi or Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome). This test is used to help evaluate suspected deficiency in at-risk patients or those with compatible symptoms.

Patient Prep: Timing is important — this test is generally performed at a specific point in pregnancy (commonly around 15-20 weeks) — follow your provider's timing instructions.
Short Description: Prenatal blood test that measures maternal alpha-fetoprotein, used to help screen for certain birth defects during pregnancy.
Long Description: Maternal serum AFP is a screening test that measures alpha-fetoprotein, a protein produced by the developing baby, in the mother's blood during pregnancy. Higher-than-expected levels can be associated with neural tube defects (like spina bifida) or certain other conditions, while lower-than-expected levels can be associated with chromosomal conditions like Down syndrome; results are typically interpreted alongside other prenatal screening tests (as part of a broader panel) rather than on their own, and an abnormal result generally leads to further testing rather than a diagnosis.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps evaluate autoimmune thyroid conditions, most notably Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
Long Description: Thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies are among the most common markers of autoimmune thyroid disease, particularly Hashimoto's thyroiditis (a common cause of hypothyroidism) and, less often, Graves' disease. This test is commonly ordered alongside TSH and free T4 when autoimmune thyroid disease is suspected, such as in patients with an enlarged thyroid (goiter), abnormal thyroid function tests, or a family history of thyroid autoimmunity.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though timing relative to the last dose may be requested for accurate interpretation.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of ibuprofen, a common over-the-counter pain reliever, used to help evaluate suspected overdose or excessive use.
Long Description: Ibuprofen is a widely used over-the-counter pain and anti-inflammatory medication. This test measures its concentration in the blood and is most often used in the context of a suspected overdose or to help evaluate unexplained symptoms in someone known to be taking the medication, particularly at higher-than-recommended doses.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps identify a recent or acute Chlamydia trachomatis infection.
Long Description: Chlamydia trachomatis IgM antibodies typically indicate a more recent infection, in contrast to IgG antibodies, which can reflect either current or past exposure. This test is less commonly used than direct molecular (NAAT) testing for genital chlamydia, but can be useful in certain situations, such as evaluating for chlamydia-related infections beyond the genital tract (like certain eye or respiratory infections in newborns).

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Measures antithrombin III, a natural blood-clotting regulator, part of a workup for inherited or acquired clotting disorders.
Long Description: Antithrombin III is a natural anticoagulant protein that helps prevent excessive blood clotting. Low activity — whether inherited or acquired (such as from liver disease or certain medications) — is associated with an increased risk of abnormal blood clots (venous thromboembolism). This test is typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners and by pregnancy or oral contraceptive use — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Measures total Protein S antigen level, part of a workup for inherited or acquired blood clotting disorders.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of Protein S protein present in the blood, distinct from Protein S activity testing, which measures how well it functions. Together, these help distinguish different types of Protein S deficiency. It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Short Description: Measures nickel levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate occupational or environmental nickel exposure.
Long Description: Nickel is a metal found in certain occupational settings (such as metal plating, welding, or stainless steel manufacturing) and some consumer products. This test measures nickel excreted in urine and is used to help evaluate suspected occupational or environmental nickel exposure, or investigate symptoms potentially related to nickel toxicity.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended, consistent with standard lipid testing.
Short Description: Measures Apolipoprotein A1, a protein component of HDL cholesterol, used as part of a more detailed cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: Apolipoprotein A1 (Apo A1) is the main protein found in HDL ('good') cholesterol particles, and reflects the number of protective HDL particles somewhat more directly than HDL cholesterol level alone. This test is sometimes used alongside a standard lipid panel and Apolipoprotein B to give a more detailed picture of cardiovascular risk, particularly in patients with a strong family history of heart disease or complex lipid abnormalities.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended, consistent with standard lipid testing.
Short Description: Measures Apolipoprotein B, a protein component of LDL and other 'bad' cholesterol particles, used as part of a more detailed cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: Apolipoprotein B (Apo B) is found on LDL and other atherogenic ('bad') cholesterol particles, with each particle carrying exactly one Apo B molecule — meaning this test essentially counts the number of potentially artery-clogging particles, which some research suggests may be a more precise cardiovascular risk marker than LDL cholesterol level alone. It's often used alongside a standard lipid panel and Apolipoprotein A1, particularly for patients with elevated triglycerides, diabetes, or a strong family history of heart disease.

Patient Prep: Requires resting quietly (often lying down) for a period before the draw, and avoiding caffeine, strenuous exercise, and certain medications beforehand — follow your provider's specific preparation instructions.
Short Description: Measures fractionated catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, dopamine), used to help evaluate suspected pheochromocytoma or related adrenal tumors.
Long Description: Catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine, and dopamine) are stress hormones produced mainly by the adrenal glands and nervous system. This test measures each one individually ('fractionated') and is primarily used to help evaluate suspected pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma — rare tumors that can cause dangerously high blood pressure, palpitations, and other symptoms from excess catecholamine release. Because levels are very sensitive to stress, position, and certain foods or medications, careful preparation and specimen handling are important for accurate results.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required.
Short Description: Measures blood chromium levels, used to help evaluate occupational or environmental chromium exposure.
Long Description: Chromium is a metal used in certain industrial processes (such as stainless steel production, chrome plating, and leather tanning) and is also relevant to monitoring some metal hip/joint implants. This test measures chromium levels in the blood and is used to help evaluate suspected occupational or environmental exposure, or to monitor patients with certain metal implants for signs of metal release.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected before starting antibiotics.
Short Description: Bacterial culture that identifies Bordetella pertussis, the cause of whooping cough.
Long Description: Bordetella pertussis is the bacterium responsible for whooping cough (pertussis), a respiratory infection best known for causing severe coughing fits. This culture, taken from a nasopharyngeal swab, helps confirm an active infection and is commonly ordered for patients with a prolonged or severe cough illness, especially with known exposure to pertussis or in communities experiencing an outbreak.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though ejaculation, prostate exams, or certain procedures shortly before the test may be asked to be avoided, since they can temporarily affect results.
Short Description: Measures total PSA (prostate-specific antigen), used to help screen for and monitor prostate conditions, including prostate cancer.
Long Description: Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a protein produced by the prostate gland, and elevated levels can be associated with prostate cancer, but also with non-cancerous conditions like an enlarged prostate (BPH) or prostatitis (prostate inflammation). This test is commonly used for prostate cancer screening discussions in men, and to monitor known prostate cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence; screening decisions are typically made together with a healthcare provider given ongoing debate about optimal screening approaches.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required.
Short Description: Measures blood bismuth levels, used to help evaluate suspected bismuth toxicity, often related to certain medications.
Long Description: Bismuth is found in some over-the-counter medications (such as bismuth subsalicylate, used for upset stomach) and, less commonly, in certain occupational settings. This test measures bismuth levels in the blood and is used to help evaluate suspected toxicity, particularly with prolonged or excessive use of bismuth-containing products, which can in rare cases cause neurological symptoms.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Complete urinalysis that evaluates the physical, chemical, and microscopic properties of urine, used as a broad screening tool for urinary and general health.
Long Description: A complete urinalysis examines urine's appearance, chemical composition (such as protein, glucose, and blood), and microscopic contents (such as cells, crystals, and bacteria). It's one of the most common lab tests, used to help screen for urinary tract infections, kidney disease, diabetes, and other conditions, and is a routine part of general wellness checkups, pregnancy care, and pre-surgical evaluations.

Short Description: Measures selenium levels, used to help evaluate suspected nutritional deficiency or toxicity.
Long Description: Selenium is a trace mineral important for antioxidant defense, thyroid function, and immune health. This test is used to help evaluate suspected deficiency, which can occur with certain restrictive diets, malabsorption conditions, or in specific geographic regions with selenium-poor soil, or to evaluate suspected toxicity from excessive supplementation or occupational exposure.

Patient Prep: Requires the sample to be collected and transported on ice and processed quickly, since ammonia levels rise rapidly in improperly handled specimens; fasting may also be recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood ammonia levels, used to help evaluate liver disease and certain causes of confusion or altered mental status.
Long Description: Ammonia is a waste product normally converted by the liver into urea for elimination. When the liver isn't functioning well — such as in advanced liver disease — ammonia can build up in the blood and contribute to confusion, drowsiness, or altered mental status (hepatic encephalopathy). This test is used to help evaluate suspected liver-related causes of these symptoms, and in some cases to investigate certain inherited metabolic disorders, particularly in children.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — special handling may be required
Patient Prep: Specimen may need to be protected from light and processed promptly — follow your provider's or lab's specific handling instructions.
Short Description: Measures fractionated porphyrins in plasma, part of a workup for porphyria, a group of rare metabolic disorders.
Long Description: Porphyrins are compounds involved in making heme, a component of hemoglobin. This test measures and separates ('fractionates') different porphyrin types in the blood, helping to diagnose and classify porphyria — a group of rare inherited disorders that can cause skin sensitivity to light, abdominal pain, or neurological symptoms depending on the specific type. It's typically ordered when porphyria is suspected based on symptoms, often alongside urine and stool porphyrin testing.

Product Name (Source): QST0005616 Iron TIBC and Ferritin Panel
Patient Prep: Morning collection is often preferred for the most standard interpretation of iron-related results.
Short Description: Panel that combines iron, total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), and ferritin to give a fuller picture of the body's iron status.
Long Description: This panel combines three related tests — serum iron, total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), and ferritin — to give a more complete picture of iron status than any one test alone. Together, they help distinguish between iron deficiency anemia, iron overload conditions, and anemia related to chronic disease or inflammation, and are commonly ordered when evaluating unexplained anemia, fatigue, or abnormal results from a complete blood count.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; typically collected around 36-37 weeks of pregnancy per standard prenatal screening guidelines.
Short Description: Culture that screens for Group B Streptococcus, a bacterium that can affect newborns if present during delivery.
Long Description: Group B Streptococcus (GBS) is a common bacterium that doesn't usually cause problems in healthy adults but can be passed to a baby during childbirth and cause serious infection in a newborn. This culture is a standard part of prenatal care, typically performed in the third trimester, to determine whether antibiotics should be given during labor to help prevent transmission to the baby.

Short Description: Measures complement components C3 and C4, part of the immune system, used to help evaluate certain autoimmune and inflammatory conditions.
Long Description: C3 and C4 are proteins that are part of the complement system, a component of the immune response. Levels can be abnormally low during active autoimmune disease (such as lupus flares, where complement is 'consumed') or in certain inherited complement deficiencies, and are commonly used to help diagnose and monitor disease activity in autoimmune conditions, particularly lupus.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma), fasting and then a timed sample after a 75-gram glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a standardized 75-gram glucose solution, with blood drawn at set time intervals afterward — plan for the appointment to take a couple of hours.
Short Description: Glucose tolerance test using a 75-gram glucose challenge, used to help diagnose diabetes, prediabetes, or gestational diabetes.
Long Description: This test measures blood glucose while fasting and then at intervals after drinking a standardized 75-gram glucose solution, to evaluate how effectively the body processes sugar. It's a standard test for diagnosing diabetes and prediabetes in the general population, and is also used (sometimes with a different glucose dose per specific protocols) to help diagnose gestational diabetes during pregnancy.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps evaluate Graves' disease, an autoimmune condition that causes an overactive thyroid.
Long Description: TSH-binding inhibitory immunoglobulin (TBII) testing detects antibodies that interfere with or mimic TSH's action on the thyroid gland, which are commonly found in Graves' disease, an autoimmune cause of hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid). This test is used to help confirm a Graves' disease diagnosis, particularly when the cause of hyperthyroidism isn't clear from other testing, and can help predict the likelihood of relapse or guide treatment decisions.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to rubella (German measles), historically required as part of premarital health screening.
Long Description: This test detects rubella (German measles) IgG antibodies, confirming immunity from past infection or vaccination. Historically required in some jurisdictions as part of premarital health screening, it remains useful for confirming immune status before pregnancy, since rubella infection during pregnancy can cause serious birth defects.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks immunity levels to rabies, most often used to monitor vaccination status in people with ongoing exposure risk.
Long Description: This test measures rabies antibody levels, most commonly used to confirm adequate immunity in people who receive rabies vaccination due to ongoing occupational exposure risk (such as veterinarians, animal control workers, or researchers handling the virus), helping determine whether a booster vaccination is needed.

Short Description: Antibody test (anti-Jo-1) that helps support the diagnosis of certain inflammatory muscle diseases.
Long Description: Anti-Jo-1 antibodies are associated with a subset of inflammatory muscle diseases (myositis), particularly when combined with lung involvement, joint pain, and other features sometimes grouped as 'antisynthetase syndrome.' This test is commonly ordered when a patient has symptoms of muscle weakness, along with lung or joint symptoms, to help support this specific diagnosis alongside clinical evaluation and other testing.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (CA 15-3) most often used to help monitor breast cancer.
Long Description: CA 15-3 is a protein that can be elevated in breast cancer, particularly in more advanced or metastatic disease, though it can also be affected by some non-cancerous conditions. It's primarily used to help monitor known breast cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence, alongside imaging and clinical evaluation, rather than as a stand-alone screening test for detecting breast cancer in the general population.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though a specific metal-free collection tube is typically required.
Short Description: Measures blood aluminum levels, used to help evaluate aluminum exposure, particularly in patients with kidney disease.
Long Description: Aluminum exposure is a particular concern for patients with reduced kidney function, since the kidneys normally help clear it from the body, and for those on dialysis or receiving aluminum-containing medications (like certain antacids or phosphate binders). This test measures blood aluminum levels and is used to help monitor for aluminum accumulation and related toxicity in these higher-risk patients.

Short Description: Measures aluminum levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate aluminum exposure or accumulation.
Long Description: This test measures aluminum excreted in urine, used to help evaluate aluminum exposure or accumulation, particularly in patients with reduced kidney function or those exposed to aluminum through certain medications, dialysis, or occupational sources.

Specimen Type: 24-hour or random urine sample, per collection instructions
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider may specify a particular collection method (24-hour vs. random).
Short Description: Measures bismuth levels in urine, used to help evaluate suspected bismuth toxicity, often related to certain medications.
Long Description: This test measures bismuth excreted in urine, used to help evaluate suspected bismuth toxicity, particularly with prolonged or excessive use of bismuth-containing over-the-counter products (like bismuth subsalicylate), which can in rare cases cause neurological symptoms.

Specimen Type: 24-hour or random urine sample, per collection instructions
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider may specify a particular collection method.
Short Description: Measures chromium levels in urine, used to help evaluate occupational or environmental chromium exposure.
Long Description: This test measures chromium excreted in urine, used to help evaluate suspected occupational or environmental chromium exposure — for example, in workers involved in metal plating, welding, or stainless steel manufacturing — or to monitor patients with certain metal implants.

Short Description: Qualitative urine drug screen that checks for dextromethorphan, a common over-the-counter cough suppressant that can be misused.
Long Description: Dextromethorphan (DXM) is an ingredient in many over-the-counter cough and cold medications, but can cause dissociative or hallucinogenic effects at high doses and is sometimes misused for this reason. This qualitative urine test checks for its presence and is commonly used in clinical toxicology, substance use evaluation, or workplace/monitoring drug testing programs.

Short Description: Measures magnesium levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate the cause of abnormal blood magnesium levels.
Long Description: This test measures magnesium excreted in urine, most often used alongside blood magnesium to help distinguish whether abnormal magnesium levels are due to a kidney-related cause (where the kidneys aren't retaining or excreting magnesium normally) versus other causes like poor dietary intake or gastrointestinal losses.

Short Description: Measures manganese levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate occupational or environmental manganese exposure.
Long Description: Manganese exposure is most often a concern in certain occupational settings, such as welding, mining, or metal manufacturing, where excessive exposure can affect the nervous system over time. This test measures manganese excreted in urine and is used to help evaluate suspected exposure or investigate related neurological symptoms.

Short Description: Urine drug screen with confirmatory testing that checks for pentazocine, a prescription opioid pain medication.
Long Description: Pentazocine is an opioid pain medication. This test screens urine for its presence, with confirmatory testing to verify a positive screening result. It's commonly used in pain management monitoring, substance use evaluation, or workplace/clinical drug testing programs to confirm appropriate use or detect unauthorized use.

Short Description: Qualitative urine drug screen that checks for phenothiazines, a class of antipsychotic and anti-nausea medications.
Long Description: Phenothiazines are a class of medications used to treat certain psychiatric conditions and, in lower doses, nausea. This qualitative urine test checks for their presence and is commonly used in clinical toxicology or medication compliance monitoring, particularly to help investigate unexplained symptoms or confirm medication use.

Short Description: Qualitative urine test that checks for salicylate, most commonly associated with aspirin, used to help evaluate suspected overdose or excessive use.
Long Description: Salicylates are found in aspirin and some other over-the-counter and herbal products. This qualitative urine test checks for their presence and is most often used to help evaluate a suspected overdose or unexplained symptoms in someone who may have taken a salicylate-containing product, particularly in urgent or toxicology settings.

Short Description: Measures selenium levels in blood, used to help evaluate suspected nutritional deficiency or toxicity.
Long Description: Selenium is a trace mineral important for antioxidant defense, thyroid function, and immune health. This blood-based test is used to help evaluate suspected deficiency, which can occur with certain restrictive diets or malabsorption conditions, or suspected toxicity from excessive supplementation or occupational/environmental exposure.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though the sample may need to be protected from light — follow your provider's or lab's specific collection instructions.
Short Description: Measures delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) in urine, used to help evaluate suspected lead poisoning or certain porphyria disorders.
Long Description: Delta-aminolevulinic acid (ALA) is a compound involved in making heme, and its levels can rise with lead poisoning (since lead interferes with this process) or with certain types of porphyria, a group of rare metabolic disorders. This test is used to help evaluate suspected lead exposure or investigate symptoms suggestive of porphyria, such as abdominal pain or neurological symptoms.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though avoiding certain foods (like bananas, coffee, and vanilla) and medications beforehand may be recommended — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures homovanillic acid (HVA) in urine, used to help evaluate suspected neuroblastoma and related tumors, primarily in children.
Long Description: Homovanillic acid (HVA) is a breakdown product of dopamine, and elevated levels can be a sign of certain tumors — most notably neuroblastoma, a cancer that occurs mainly in young children — that produce excess catecholamines. This test is typically ordered, often alongside vanillylmandelic acid (VMA), when this type of tumor is suspected based on symptoms or other findings.

Short Description: Measures zinc levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate zinc status alongside blood testing.
Long Description: This test measures zinc excreted in urine, sometimes used alongside blood zinc testing to help build a fuller picture of zinc status, particularly when evaluating unexplained symptoms like poor wound healing, hair loss, or reduced taste or smell that could relate to zinc deficiency or, less commonly, excess.

Short Description: Measures zinc levels inside red blood cells, offering a longer-term picture of zinc status than a standard blood test.
Long Description: Unlike standard plasma/serum zinc, which can be affected by recent diet and the time of day, RBC (red blood cell) zinc is thought by some practitioners to better reflect the body's longer-term zinc stores. It's often used in wellness or functional-medicine settings to further explore zinc status, particularly when standard zinc testing is inconclusive.

Short Description: Complete blood count with differential and platelets — a detailed panel that evaluates red blood cells, the different types of white blood cells, and platelets.
Long Description: This expanded complete blood count includes a differential, which breaks down the different types of white blood cells (such as neutrophils, lymphocytes, and monocytes) in addition to standard red blood cell and platelet measurements. This added detail is useful for more specifically evaluating infection, inflammation, certain blood disorders, and immune conditions, beyond what a basic CBC alone can show.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps diagnose Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever, a tick-borne bacterial infection, with automatic reflex testing.
Long Description: Rocky Mountain Spotted Fever is a potentially serious tick-borne bacterial infection. This test checks for IgG and IgM antibodies, with reflex testing to help confirm results, and is commonly ordered for patients with fever, rash, and other symptoms following a tick bite or exposure in an area where the infection is more common; because antibodies take time to develop, treatment decisions are often made based on symptoms and exposure history rather than waiting for antibody test results.

Short Description: Antibody panel that helps diagnose current, recent, or past infection with Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), the cause of mononucleosis.
Long Description: This panel measures several different Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) antibodies together, which allows a provider to distinguish between a current/acute infection, a recent infection, and a past infection from long ago, since different antibody types rise and fall at different stages. It's commonly used when infectious mononucleosis is suspected but a quicker heterophile ('monospot') test is negative, inconclusive, or when more detailed information about infection timing is needed.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Basic urinalysis that checks the physical and chemical properties of urine, without a microscopic exam.
Long Description: This macroscopic urinalysis evaluates the visual appearance and chemical properties of urine (such as pH, protein, glucose, and blood) using a dipstick-style test, without examining the sample under a microscope. It's used as a quicker, more limited screening tool compared to a complete urinalysis, often when a fast initial check is needed or microscopic exam isn't required based on the initial findings.

Short Description: Combined panel that screens for multiple types of viral hepatitis (A, B, and C) in a single test.
Long Description: This panel combines screening tests for hepatitis A, B, and C into a single order, offering an efficient way to check for exposure or infection with the most common types of viral hepatitis at once. It's commonly used for general hepatitis screening, evaluating unexplained liver enzyme abnormalities, or assessing risk in patients with relevant exposure history.

Product Name (Source): QST0006517 MICROALBUMIN, RANDOM UR w/ CREATININE
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; a random urine sample is typically used.
Short Description: Measures small amounts of albumin (microalbumin) alongside creatinine in a random urine sample, an early marker of kidney damage.
Long Description: This test measures microalbumin — small amounts of the protein albumin not detectable on a standard urinalysis — along with creatinine in a single, randomly collected urine sample, calculating a ratio that estimates 24-hour albumin excretion without a full-day collection. It's commonly used to help detect early kidney damage, especially in people with diabetes or high blood pressure, since it can pick up kidney changes before more significant disease develops.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) — special handling and timing may apply
Patient Prep: Morning collection is often recommended, since corticosterone follows a daily pattern similar to cortisol — follow your provider's specific timing instructions.
Short Description: Measures corticosterone, an adrenal hormone, sometimes used in specialized evaluations of adrenal hormone production.
Long Description: Corticosterone is a steroid hormone produced by the adrenal glands, playing a more central role in some animals than in humans, where cortisol is the primary stress hormone. In people, corticosterone testing is used less often than cortisol, typically in specialized evaluations of specific adrenal enzyme pathways or certain rare adrenal conditions, usually ordered by an endocrinologist investigating an unusual hormone pattern.

Product Name (Source): QST0006646 LYME AB WITH REFLEX BLOT
Short Description: Screening antibody test for Lyme disease, with automatic confirmatory Western blot testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: This test screens for antibodies to the bacterium that causes Lyme disease, a tick-borne illness. If the initial screen is positive or equivocal, the lab automatically performs a confirmatory Western blot test, which looks for a more specific antibody pattern to help reduce false positives, consistent with standard two-tiered Lyme disease testing guidelines. It's commonly ordered for patients with symptoms like a rash, fever, joint pain, or fatigue following a tick bite or exposure in an area where Lyme disease is common.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to B. Cinerea, a mold allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to B. Cinerea, a mold/fungal allergen that can trigger year-round or seasonal allergy and asthma symptoms, especially in damp environments. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures both free and total valproic acid levels together, giving a fuller picture for drug monitoring.
Long Description: This test reports both total valproic acid (bound and unbound combined) and free valproic acid (the active, unbound portion) from the same sample. Ordering both together can help a prescriber get a complete picture in situations where protein binding might be affected — such as low albumin, kidney or liver disease, or pregnancy — which can make total levels alone misleading. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Stool samples — 3 separate collections on different days
Patient Prep: Requires three stool samples collected on three different days, since some parasites are shed intermittently — follow your provider's or lab's specific collection instructions.
Short Description: Stool test that checks for intestinal parasites and their eggs across three separate samples, improving detection of parasites shed intermittently.
Long Description: The ova and parasite (O&P) exam examines stool samples under the microscope for parasites and their eggs. Because some parasites aren't shed in every bowel movement, collecting three samples on different days improves the chances of detecting an infection that a single sample might miss. It's commonly ordered for persistent, unexplained gastrointestinal symptoms, especially after travel or known exposure to contaminated food or water.

Specimen Type: Stool samples — 2 separate collections on different days
Patient Prep: Requires two stool samples collected on two different days, since some parasites are shed intermittently — follow your provider's or lab's specific collection instructions.
Short Description: Stool test that checks for intestinal parasites and their eggs across two separate samples, improving detection of parasites shed intermittently.
Long Description: The ova and parasite (O&P) exam examines stool samples under the microscope for parasites and their eggs. Because some parasites aren't shed in every bowel movement, collecting two samples on different days improves the chances of detecting an infection that a single sample might miss. It's commonly ordered for persistent, unexplained gastrointestinal symptoms, especially after travel or known exposure to contaminated food or water.

Patient Prep: Requires 5 separate blood draws, timed per your provider's specific protocol — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures TSH across 5 separately timed samples, used to help average out natural fluctuations for a more reliable result.
Long Description: Tsh levels can fluctuate significantly over short periods (release patterns vary through the day or in response to stress), which can make a single measurement less reliable. This protocol pools or analyzes 5 separately timed blood samples to capture a more representative average level, which can be useful when standard single-sample testing has been inconclusive, or when your provider wants a more precise baseline for diagnosis or monitoring.

Patient Prep: Requires 4 separate blood draws, timed per your provider's specific protocol — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures TSH across 4 separately timed samples, used to help average out natural fluctuations for a more reliable result.
Long Description: Tsh levels can fluctuate significantly over short periods (release patterns vary through the day or in response to stress), which can make a single measurement less reliable. This protocol pools or analyzes 4 separately timed blood samples to capture a more representative average level, which can be useful when standard single-sample testing has been inconclusive, or when your provider wants a more precise baseline for diagnosis or monitoring.

Short Description: Panel that measures IgG, IgA, and IgM together, giving a fuller picture of overall antibody/immune function.
Long Description: This panel measures the three main immunoglobulin (antibody) classes — IgG, IgA, and IgM — together in a single order, providing a broader picture of a patient's overall antibody production than any one alone. It's commonly used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, recurrent infections, or certain chronic conditions affecting antibody levels.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for both current/recent and past cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection using IgG and IgM together.
Long Description: This test measures both CMV IgM (which suggests a more recent or active infection) and CMV IgG (which indicates past infection or immunity) together, helping distinguish where a patient is in the course of CMV infection or exposure. It's commonly used in pregnancy evaluation, pre-transplant screening, or when a current CMV infection is suspected based on symptoms.
