Specimen Type: Blood or spinal fluid sample, depending on clinical situation
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific specimen type depends on what your provider is evaluating.
Short Description: Rapid antigen panel that screens for several common bacteria at once, sometimes used to help quickly evaluate suspected bacterial infection.
Long Description: This panel tests for antigens from several different bacteria in a single sample, offering a faster (though less definitive) alternative to waiting for culture results. It's used in specific clinical situations — such as suspected bacterial meningitis — where rapid initial information can help guide urgent treatment decisions, though culture and other confirmatory testing are typically still needed.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Mouse Serum, an animal dander allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Mouse Serum, an animal-derived allergen that can trigger allergy and asthma symptoms on exposure to pets or other animals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — 4 timed specimens (fasting plus 3 post-glucose draws)
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a standardized glucose solution, with blood drawn at three separate time points afterward — plan for the appointment to take a few hours.
Short Description: 3-hour gestational diabetes diagnostic test with four timed glucose measurements, used to confirm a gestational diabetes diagnosis.
Long Description: This is the more extensive, diagnostic-level glucose tolerance test used to confirm gestational diabetes after an initial screening test comes back abnormal. It measures glucose while fasting and then at three separate time points after drinking a glucose solution, giving a fuller picture of how the body handles sugar over several hours during pregnancy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Rat Sr Protein, an animal dander allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Rat Sr Protein, an animal-derived allergen that can trigger allergy and asthma symptoms on exposure to pets or other animals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to R. Nigricans, a mold allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to R. Nigricans, a mold/fungal allergen that can trigger year-round or seasonal allergy and asthma symptoms, especially in damp environments. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum), fasting and then a timed sample 2 hours after a glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a glucose solution, with a blood draw exactly 2 hours later — plan for the appointment to take a few hours.
Short Description: Glucose tolerance test that checks blood sugar at fasting and 2 hours after a glucose challenge, used to help diagnose diabetes or prediabetes.
Long Description: This test measures blood glucose while fasting and again exactly two hours after drinking a standardized glucose solution, to evaluate how the body processes sugar over a longer window than a 1-hour test. It's commonly used to help diagnose diabetes and prediabetes in the general population, and in some gestational diabetes testing protocols.

Specimen Type: Urine or urethral/cervical swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Molecular (DNA) test that detects Chlamydia and Gonorrhea from a urine or urethral sample, commonly used for STD screening in men.
Long Description: This test uses molecular (DNA-based) technology to detect Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae, the bacteria responsible for two of the most common sexually transmitted infections, from a urine or urethral swab sample. It's widely used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up, and is valued for its high sensitivity and the convenience of a urine-based option.

Short Description: Basic blood count that measures red blood cells, hemoglobin, hematocrit, and related values, without a white cell differential.
Long Description: A hemogram covers the core red blood cell measurements of a complete blood count — including red blood cell count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit — along with red cell indices, but without a detailed white blood cell differential. It's used to help evaluate anemia and general blood health as part of routine screening or symptom workups such as fatigue or unexplained bruising.

Product Name (Source): QST0007020 THYROID PANEL (QUEST PANEL - SEE DESCRIPTION)
Short Description: Standard thyroid panel combining several thyroid-related tests to help evaluate overall thyroid function.
Long Description: This panel combines multiple thyroid-related tests — most often TSH along with one or more thyroid hormone measurements (such as free T4 or total T4) — into a single order to give a fuller picture of thyroid function than any one test alone. It's commonly used for both initial thyroid evaluation and ongoing monitoring of known thyroid conditions or thyroid medication dosing. Because bundled thyroid panels can vary in exactly which components are included, it's worth confirming with your lab which specific tests are part of this version.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners and by pregnancy or oral contraceptive use — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Combined Protein S activity and antigen testing, giving a fuller picture for a clotting disorder workup.
Long Description: This test combines Protein S activity (how well it functions) and Protein S antigen (how much is present) into a single order, which together help distinguish between different types of Protein S deficiency, a natural anticoagulant that helps regulate blood clotting. It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Basic urine screening test that checks for common abnormalities, often used as a quick initial check before a full urinalysis is ordered.
Long Description: This urinalysis screen checks urine for common indicators of infection, kidney issues, or metabolic conditions, serving as a faster, more limited alternative to a complete urinalysis. It's often used for quick initial screening, with a full urinalysis or culture ordered as a follow-up if the screen suggests further evaluation is needed.

Short Description: Measures white blood cell count along with a breakdown of the different white blood cell types, used to help evaluate infection, inflammation, and immune health.
Long Description: This test combines the total white blood cell (WBC) count with a differential, which breaks down the relative proportions of different white cell types (such as neutrophils, lymphocytes, and monocytes). This added detail helps a provider narrow down the likely cause of an abnormal white count — for example, distinguishing a bacterial infection from a viral one, or investigating certain blood or immune disorders.

Short Description: Combined test that measures vitamin B12 and folate together, both important for red blood cell production and nerve health.
Long Description: Vitamin B12 and folate are both essential for healthy red blood cell production and nervous system function, and deficiencies in either can cause similar symptoms, including fatigue and a specific type of anemia (megaloblastic anemia). Testing them together helps a provider efficiently evaluate suspected deficiency in either nutrient, which is commonly done when investigating unexplained anemia, fatigue, or neurological symptoms like numbness or tingling.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should not be drawn from a patient currently on certain blood thinners without discussing timing with the ordering provider.
Short Description: Screening test for lupus anticoagulant, an antibody linked to blood clotting risk, with automatic follow-up (reflex) testing if results are unclear.
Long Description: Lupus anticoagulant is one of the antiphospholipid antibodies associated with an increased risk of blood clots and pregnancy complications, despite its somewhat confusing name (it doesn't require a lupus diagnosis and doesn't actually prevent clotting in the body, despite prolonging certain clotting tests in the lab). This test screens for its presence, with reflex confirmatory testing automatically performed if initial results are unclear, and is typically ordered as part of a broader workup for unexplained blood clots or recurrent pregnancy loss.

Short Description: Panel that measures IgG, IgA, and IgM together, giving a fuller picture of overall antibody/immune function.
Long Description: This panel measures the three main immunoglobulin (antibody) classes — IgG, IgA, and IgM — together in a single order, providing a broader picture of a patient's overall antibody production than any one alone. It's commonly used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, recurrent infections, or certain chronic conditions affecting antibody levels.

Short Description: Panel used to monitor an existing hepatitis B infection, tracking viral activity and liver-related markers over time.
Long Description: This panel combines several hepatitis B-related tests used to monitor patients already known to have hepatitis B, helping track how active the infection is and whether treatment is working. The exact components can vary, so it's worth confirming with your lab which specific markers (such as viral antigens, antibodies, or viral load) are included in this particular panel version.

Short Description: Panel that checks hepatitis B immunity status, most often used to confirm protection from past vaccination or infection.
Long Description: This panel combines hepatitis B antibody tests to help determine whether a person is immune to hepatitis B, whether from past vaccination or prior infection. It's commonly used for healthcare workers, students, or others who need to document hepatitis B immunity, or to help decide whether a booster vaccine dose is needed.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle can matter for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Combined test that measures both FSH and LH together, used to help evaluate fertility, menstrual cycle health, and pituitary function.
Long Description: Follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) and luteinizing hormone (LH) work together to regulate reproductive function, including ovulation and sperm production. Measuring them together is commonly used to help evaluate fertility concerns, irregular menstrual cycles, suspected menopause, and certain pituitary or gonadal conditions, since interpreting one without the other can miss part of the picture.

Short Description: Basic blood count plus a white blood cell differential, combining red blood cell measurements with a breakdown of white cell types.
Long Description: This test combines a hemogram (core red blood cell measurements including red blood cell count, hemoglobin, and hematocrit) with a white blood cell differential (breaking down the relative proportions of different white cell types). Together, they help evaluate anemia, infection, inflammation, and certain blood disorders in one combined order.

Short Description: Combined antibody test for thyroid peroxidase (TPO) and thyroglobulin, used to help evaluate autoimmune thyroid conditions.
Long Description: This test measures both thyroid peroxidase (TPO) antibodies and thyroglobulin antibodies together, two of the most common markers of autoimmune thyroid disease, including Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease. Testing both together gives a more complete picture than either antibody alone, and is commonly ordered when autoimmune thyroid disease is suspected based on symptoms, an enlarged thyroid, or abnormal thyroid function tests.

Short Description: Breaks down total bilirubin into its direct and indirect components, used to help pinpoint the cause of jaundice or liver issues.
Long Description: Fractionated bilirubin separates total bilirubin into its direct (conjugated, processed by the liver) and indirect (unconjugated, not yet processed) components. This distinction helps a provider narrow down the cause of elevated bilirubin or jaundice — for example, distinguishing liver or bile duct problems (which tend to raise direct bilirubin) from conditions that increase red blood cell breakdown (which tend to raise indirect bilirubin).

Patient Prep: Requires 3 separate blood draws, timed per your provider's specific protocol — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures cortisol across 3 separately timed samples, used to help average out natural fluctuations for a more reliable result.
Long Description: Cortisol levels can fluctuate significantly over short periods (release patterns vary through the day or in response to stress), which can make a single measurement less reliable. This protocol pools or analyzes 3 separately timed blood samples to capture a more representative average level, which can be useful when standard single-sample testing has been inconclusive, or when your provider wants a more precise baseline for diagnosis or monitoring.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle or menopause status can matter for interpretation — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures both estradiol and estrone together, two forms of estrogen, used to help evaluate reproductive hormone balance, especially around menopause.
Long Description: Estradiol and estrone are two of the main forms of estrogen, with their relative proportions shifting significantly around menopause, when estrone often becomes the more dominant form. Measuring both together is used to help evaluate menopausal status, certain fertility or menstrual cycle concerns, and hormone therapy monitoring, giving a fuller picture than either hormone alone.

Short Description: Thyroid panel that includes TSH along with other thyroid hormone measurements, used to give a broad picture of thyroid function.
Long Description: This panel combines TSH, the primary thyroid screening test, with one or more additional thyroid hormone measurements (such as free T4) to provide a fuller picture of thyroid function than TSH alone. It's commonly used for both initial thyroid evaluation and ongoing monitoring of known thyroid conditions or medication dosing; since bundled panels can vary in their exact components, it's worth confirming with your lab which specific tests this version includes.

Patient Prep: Requires 2 separate blood draws, timed per your provider's specific protocol — coordinate timing with your provider.
Short Description: Measures growth hormone (GH) across 2 separately timed samples, used to help average out its naturally pulsatile release pattern.
Long Description: Growth hormone is released in sharp pulses rather than steadily throughout the day, which can make a single measurement misleading. This protocol pools or analyzes 2 separately timed blood samples to capture a more representative average level, which can be useful when evaluating suspected growth hormone deficiency or excess (such as acromegaly) and standard single-sample testing hasn't given a clear answer.

This protocol pools or analyzes 10 separately timed blood samples to capture a more representative average level, which can be useful when evaluating suspected growth hormone deficiency or excess (such as acromegaly) and standard single-sample testing hasn't given a clear answer.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — 5 timed specimens (fasting plus 4 draws at 30-minute intervals)
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a glucose solution, with blood drawn at 30-minute intervals afterward — plan for the appointment to take a few hours.
Short Description: Extended glucose tolerance test with five timed measurements taken every 30 minutes, used for a detailed look at how the body processes sugar.
Long Description: This extended glucose tolerance test measures blood glucose while fasting and then at five time points spaced 30 minutes apart after a glucose challenge, giving a more detailed curve of how blood sugar rises and falls than a standard 2-3 point test. It's used in specific situations where a more detailed picture of glucose handling is needed, such as investigating reactive hypoglycemia or unusual blood sugar patterns.

Specimen Type: 24-hour or random urine sample, per collection instructions
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider may specify a particular collection method (24-hour vs. random).
Short Description: Screens urine for multiple heavy metals at once, used to help evaluate suspected exposure to metals such as lead, mercury, or arsenic.
Long Description: This panel checks a urine sample for several heavy metals together (commonly including metals such as lead, mercury, and arsenic, though the exact panel can vary by lab), offering a broad initial screen for suspected heavy metal exposure or toxicity. It's often used when a patient has symptoms or an exposure history that could relate to more than one type of metal, since testing several at once is more efficient than ordering each individually.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is generally preferred, since iron levels naturally vary through the day.
Short Description: Combined test that measures total iron and total iron-binding capacity (TIBC) together, used to help evaluate iron deficiency or overload.
Long Description: This test combines serum iron with total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), which reflects how much of the iron-carrying protein transferrin is available to bind iron. Together, they're used to calculate transferrin saturation, helping distinguish iron deficiency from iron overload conditions like hemochromatosis, and are commonly ordered as part of an anemia workup.

Short Description: Measures total protein and albumin together, used to help evaluate liver, kidney, and nutritional health.
Long Description: This test combines total protein and albumin measurements, allowing a provider to also calculate the albumin-to-globulin (A/G) ratio, which can offer additional clues about the underlying cause of an abnormal protein result. It's a routine component of comprehensive metabolic panels and general wellness screening, used to help evaluate liver disease, kidney disease, and nutritional status.

Patient Prep: This test requires an amniotic fluid sample, collected by a physician via amniocentesis — a separate procedure with its own preparation and risks that your provider will discuss with you.
Short Description: Measures the lecithin/sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio in amniotic fluid, historically used to help assess whether a baby's lungs are mature enough for early delivery.
Long Description: The L/S ratio compares two substances in amniotic fluid that reflect how mature a baby's lungs are, since adequate levels of lung surfactant are needed for the baby to breathe well after birth. This test has traditionally been used when early delivery is being considered, to help gauge the risk of respiratory distress syndrome in the newborn; it has become less commonly used in some settings as other clinical approaches and gestational dating methods have advanced.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results.
Short Description: Standard lipid (cholesterol) panel that measures total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and triglycerides — a core test for cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: The lipid panel is one of the most common cardiovascular screening tests, measuring total cholesterol, LDL ('bad') cholesterol, HDL ('good') cholesterol, and triglycerides together to build a full picture of heart disease risk. It's widely used for routine health screening, monitoring cholesterol-lowering treatment, and assessing cardiovascular risk factors as part of general wellness care.

Product Name (Source): QST0007788 ABO GROUP AND RH TYPE
Short Description: Determines both ABO blood type and Rh factor together, commonly used in prenatal care and before blood transfusion.
Long Description: This test combines ABO blood group typing (A, B, AB, or O) with Rh factor typing (positive or negative) into a single order. It's a standard part of prenatal care, helping identify potential blood type incompatibilities between a mother and her baby, and is also essential information before blood transfusion or certain surgical procedures.

Short Description: Antibody panel that helps evaluate Sjogren's syndrome, an autoimmune condition that causes dry eyes and dry mouth.
Long Description: Sjogren's syndrome is an autoimmune condition where the immune system attacks moisture-producing glands, most often causing dry eyes and dry mouth, though it can affect other organs too. This panel typically includes SS-A (Ro) and SS-B (La) antibodies, which are commonly found in patients with the condition, and is used to help support a diagnosis when symptoms and clinical evaluation suggest Sjogren's syndrome.

Short Description: Protein electrophoresis with visual scan, used to help evaluate certain immune, liver, kidney, or blood conditions in more detail.
Long Description: This test performs protein electrophoresis (separating blood proteins into fractions based on electrical charge) along with a scanned visual trace of the pattern, giving a provider a clearer look at any abnormal protein bands. It's commonly used to help investigate conditions such as multiple myeloma and other plasma cell disorders, chronic inflammation or infection, and unexplained abnormal total protein results.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Urinalysis with automatic follow-up testing (such as microscopic exam or culture) if the initial results suggest it's needed.
Long Description: This urinalysis includes reflex testing, meaning that if the initial results (such as abnormal protein, blood, or signs suggestive of infection) meet certain criteria, additional testing — like a microscopic exam or culture — is automatically performed without needing a separate order. It's a convenient, efficient way to ensure a more complete workup is done only when the initial screen indicates it's needed.

Short Description: Bundled allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks IgE antibody response to several common mold allergens.
Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering mold spores, which can trigger year-round or seasonal allergy and asthma symptoms, especially in damp environments, rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Panels like this are a convenient, often more cost-effective way to screen for multiple related allergens at once rather than ordering each individually; exact allergens included can vary by region/panel version, so it's worth confirming the specific component list with your lab.

Short Description: Bundled allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks IgE antibody response to several common stinging-insect venom allergens.
Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering insect stings; reactions can range from local swelling to serious systemic allergic reactions (anaphylaxis), rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Panels like this are a convenient, often more cost-effective way to screen for multiple related allergens at once rather than ordering each individually; exact allergens included can vary by region/panel version, so it's worth confirming the specific component list with your lab.

Short Description: Bundled allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks IgE antibody response to several common tree nut and peanut allergens.
Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering tree nuts and peanuts, common food allergens that can trigger reactions ranging from mild to severe (anaphylaxis), rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering seafood and shellfish, common food allergens that can trigger reactions ranging from mild to severe (anaphylaxis), rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Short Description: Panel that measures four major lymphocyte subsets, used to help evaluate immune system function.
Long Description: This lymphocyte subset panel breaks down white blood cells into four major categories of lymphocytes (such as T cells, B cells, and natural killer cells, depending on the specific panel), giving a more detailed look at immune system composition than a standard white blood cell count. It's commonly used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, monitor certain chronic infections (like HIV), or investigate unexplained immune-related symptoms.

Short Description: Qualitative urine test that screens for volatile substances, used to help evaluate suspected exposure to solvents or inhalants.
Long Description: This qualitative test screens urine for volatile organic compounds, which can be relevant in evaluating suspected exposure to industrial solvents, certain cleaning products, or inhalant misuse. It's used in specific toxicology or occupational health contexts when this type of exposure is suspected based on symptoms or history.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners and by pregnancy or oral contraceptive use — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Combined Protein C and Protein S panel with reflex testing, used as part of a broader workup for inherited or acquired clotting disorders.
Long Description: This panel combines Protein C and Protein S testing — two natural anticoagulants that help regulate blood clotting — into a single order, with reflex testing to help clarify results that are initially unclear. It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) plus a timed urine collection (typically 24-hour)
Patient Prep: Requires a timed urine collection (usually 24 hours) along with a blood draw during that same period — follow your provider's specific collection instructions closely, since accuracy depends on a complete, correctly timed collection.
Short Description: Measures how effectively the kidneys filter creatinine from the blood, a more detailed way to assess kidney function than blood tests alone.
Long Description: Creatinine clearance compares creatinine levels in a timed urine collection (usually 24 hours) with a blood sample taken during that period, providing a more direct estimate of how well the kidneys are filtering waste than blood creatinine alone. It's used to help evaluate and monitor kidney function, particularly when a more precise measurement is needed, such as before starting certain medications or in the workup of kidney disease.

Specimen Type: Sample per organism/site being tested (e.g., sputum, tissue, or culture isolate)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the type of specimen and organism being evaluated.
Short Description: Susceptibility testing for Mycobacterium species, used to help identify which medications will be effective against a mycobacterial infection such as tuberculosis.
Long Description: When a mycobacterial infection (such as tuberculosis or certain other Mycobacterium species) is identified through culture, this susceptibility test checks which antibiotics are likely to be effective against that specific organism. It's an important step in guiding effective treatment for mycobacterial infections, particularly given the risk of drug-resistant strains.

Short Description: Bundled allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks IgE antibody response to the grass pollens most common in your geographic region (Region 1).
Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering regional grass pollens, a frequent cause of seasonal hay fever symptoms during grass pollen season, rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Panels like this are a convenient, often more cost-effective way to screen for multiple related allergens at once rather than ordering each individually; exact allergens included can vary by region/panel version, so it's worth confirming the specific component list with your lab.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (whole blood or serum, per test protocol)
Short Description: Measures cholinesterase activity and dibucaine inhibition, used to help evaluate a genetic sensitivity to certain anesthesia-related muscle relaxant drugs.
Long Description: This test measures cholinesterase enzyme activity along with its response to dibucaine, helping identify an inherited enzyme variant that can cause a much longer-than-expected reaction to certain muscle relaxant medications used during anesthesia (such as succinylcholine). It's typically ordered after a patient or family member has had an unusually prolonged reaction to this type of medication during surgery, to help guide future anesthesia planning.

Short Description: Broad chemistry panel that combines multiple metabolic, liver, and kidney markers into a single comprehensive test.
Long Description: This panel bundles together a wide range of standard blood chemistry tests — commonly including electrolytes, kidney and liver markers, and other core metabolic values — into one order, offering an efficient way to get a broad snapshot of metabolic health. Exact components can vary by lab, so it's worth confirming with your lab which specific markers are included in this version.

Short Description: Expanded chemistry panel that combines an even broader set of metabolic, liver, and kidney markers than the standard version.
Long Description: This expanded panel bundles together an even broader set of standard blood chemistry tests than a standard chemistry panel, offering a more comprehensive snapshot of metabolic, liver, and kidney health in a single order. Exact components can vary by lab, so it's worth confirming with your lab which specific markers are included in this version.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Should be drawn without prolonged tourniquet time, since this can affect ionized calcium results.
Short Description: Combined test that measures parathyroid hormone (PTH) and ionized calcium together, used to help evaluate calcium and parathyroid gland health.
Long Description: Parathyroid hormone (PTH) and ionized calcium (the biologically active form of calcium in the blood) work closely together to regulate calcium balance. Measuring them together helps a provider evaluate suspected parathyroid disorders — such as hyperparathyroidism or hypoparathyroidism — as well as other causes of abnormal calcium levels, since interpreting one without the other can miss part of the picture.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin and hematocrit together, two core red blood cell values used to help evaluate anemia.
Long Description: Hemoglobin and hematocrit are two of the most fundamental red blood cell measurements, commonly ordered together as a quick way to screen for anemia or evaluate overall blood health, without running a complete blood count. It's often used for routine screening, monitoring blood loss, or quick follow-up after an abnormal CBC result.

Short Description: Screening test for infectious mononucleosis (mono), with automatic confirmatory testing if the result is unclear.
Long Description: This 'monospot' test detects heterophile antibodies typically present during infectious mononucleosis caused by Epstein-Barr virus, with reflex testing to help clarify borderline or unclear results. It's commonly ordered for patients with symptoms like sore throat, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, and fever suggestive of mono.

Short Description: Measures total T4 (thyroxine), with automatic follow-up testing if results are outside the expected range.
Long Description: This test measures total thyroxine (T4), a key thyroid hormone, and includes reflex testing — meaning if the initial result is abnormal, additional related thyroid testing (such as free T4 or a thyroid uptake ratio) is automatically performed to help clarify the finding. It's used alongside TSH to help evaluate suspected thyroid dysfunction.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; certain foods (like bananas, coffee, chocolate, and vanilla) and medications may need to be avoided beforehand — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures catecholamines and VMA together in a 24-hour urine sample, used to help evaluate suspected pheochromocytoma or related adrenal tumors.
Long Description: This test measures catecholamines (stress hormones like epinephrine and norepinephrine) along with VMA (vanillylmandelic acid), a breakdown product of catecholamines, in a full 24-hour urine collection. Together, they help evaluate suspected pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma — rare tumors that can cause dangerously high blood pressure, palpitations, and other symptoms from excess catecholamine release.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin A1c along with an estimated average glucose (eAG) value, used to help diagnose and monitor diabetes.
Long Description: This test reports hemoglobin A1c — a 2-3 month average of blood sugar control — alongside an estimated average glucose (eAG) value, which translates the A1c percentage into a number that looks more like a familiar day-to-day glucose reading. This makes results easier for some patients to relate to their own glucose monitoring, and the test is used the same way as standard A1c testing to diagnose and monitor diabetes.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Results can be affected by certain blood thinners — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Measures Antithrombin III activity, with automatic follow-up testing if results are abnormal, as part of a clotting disorder workup.
Long Description: Antithrombin III is a natural anticoagulant that helps regulate blood clotting; low activity is associated with an increased risk of abnormal blood clots. This test includes reflex testing, so if the initial activity level is abnormal, further related testing is automatically performed to help clarify the result. It's typically ordered as part of a broader clotting (thrombophilia) workup for patients with unexplained blood clots or a strong family history of clotting disorders.

Short Description: Screening antibody test for autoimmune disease, with automatic follow-up testing to further characterize a positive result.
Long Description: The antinuclear antibody (ANA) test screens for autoantibodies associated with autoimmune conditions such as lupus, Sjogren's syndrome, and other connective tissue diseases. This version includes reflex testing, so a positive screen automatically triggers additional antibody testing to help identify the specific pattern and associated condition. It's typically ordered when a patient has symptoms suggestive of an autoimmune disorder, such as joint pain, rash, or unexplained fatigue.

Specimen Type: Timed urine collection (per your provider's instructions, often 4-hour or overnight)
Patient Prep: Requires a timed urine collection over a specific period (rather than a full 24 hours) — follow your provider's exact collection instructions.
Short Description: Measures microalbumin excreted over a specific timed period, an early marker of kidney damage, especially in diabetes.
Long Description: This test measures microalbumin — small amounts of the protein albumin not detectable on a standard urinalysis — over a specific timed collection period (shorter than a full 24-hour collection). It's used to help detect early kidney damage, particularly in people with diabetes or high blood pressure, offering a more convenient alternative to a full-day collection while still providing timed, quantitative results.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results.
Short Description: Directly measures LDL cholesterol, offering a more precise alternative to the standard calculated LDL value in certain situations.
Long Description: Most standard lipid panels calculate LDL cholesterol using a formula rather than measuring it directly, which can be less accurate when triglycerides are very high or in certain other situations. This test measures LDL cholesterol directly, providing a more reliable result in those specific circumstances, and is often ordered when calculated LDL is expected to be unreliable or when a more precise value is needed for cardiovascular risk assessment.

Short Description: Measures fructosamine, a marker of average blood sugar control over the past 2-3 weeks.
Long Description: Fructosamine reflects average blood glucose levels over a shorter timeframe (roughly 2-3 weeks) than hemoglobin A1c (which reflects about 2-3 months). This makes it useful for more quickly assessing recent changes in blood sugar control, monitoring treatment adjustments, or in situations where A1c results may be unreliable — such as certain blood conditions or anemias that affect red blood cell lifespan.

Short Description: Older, nonspecific tumor-associated marker sometimes used alongside other tests in certain cancer evaluations.
Long Description: Lipid-associated sialic acid (LASA) is a nonspecific marker that can be elevated in a variety of cancers, as well as some non-cancerous conditions, which limits its usefulness as a stand-alone test. It's an older, less commonly used tumor marker today, generally considered alongside more specific, modern tumor markers and imaging rather than relied upon by itself.

Short Description: Measures potassium in a random urine sample, used alongside blood potassium to help evaluate the cause of abnormal potassium levels.
Long Description: This test measures potassium excreted in urine, most often used alongside blood potassium to help distinguish whether abnormal potassium levels are due to a kidney-related cause versus other causes like reduced intake, gastrointestinal losses, or certain medications.

Short Description: Rapid antigen test that detects adenovirus directly from a respiratory sample, used to help diagnose adenovirus infection.
Long Description: This test uses direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) staining to detect adenovirus antigens directly in a respiratory sample, offering a relatively quick way to check for this common cause of respiratory illness, conjunctivitis, and other symptoms. It's commonly used to help evaluate respiratory infections, particularly in young children or in outbreak settings.

Short Description: Rapid antigen test that detects influenza directly from a respiratory sample, used to help diagnose the flu.
Long Description: This test uses direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) staining to detect influenza virus antigens directly in a respiratory sample, offering rapid results compared to viral culture. It's commonly used during flu season to help confirm a suspected influenza infection in patients with fever, cough, and other flu-like symptoms.

Short Description: Rapid antigen test that detects parainfluenza virus types 1, 2, and 3, used to help diagnose respiratory infections, especially in children.
Long Description: This test uses direct fluorescent antibody (DFA) staining to detect parainfluenza virus types 1, 2, and 3 directly in a respiratory sample. Parainfluenza is a common cause of respiratory illness in children, including croup, and this test helps confirm the diagnosis in patients with compatible respiratory symptoms.

Short Description: Expanded panel that measures five major lymphocyte subsets, used to help evaluate immune system function in more detail.
Long Description: This expanded lymphocyte subset panel breaks white blood cells down into five major lymphocyte categories, giving a more detailed look at immune system composition than a standard white blood cell count or a smaller subset panel. It's commonly used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, monitor certain chronic infections (like HIV), or investigate unexplained immune-related symptoms.

Short Description: Measures the amount of hepatitis B virus DNA in the blood, used to monitor how active a hepatitis B infection is and how well treatment is working.
Long Description: This quantitative test measures the actual amount of hepatitis B viral DNA (viral load) in the blood, rather than just detecting its presence. It's commonly used in patients already diagnosed with hepatitis B to help assess how active the infection is, guide treatment decisions, and monitor how well antiviral treatment is working over time.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though timing relative to the last dose may be requested for accurate interpretation.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of fluoxetine (Prozac), an antidepressant medication, used to help confirm appropriate dosing or evaluate compliance.
Long Description: Fluoxetine is a commonly prescribed antidepressant. This test measures its concentration in the blood and is used less routinely than some other drug level tests, but can be helpful to confirm medication compliance, investigate an inadequate treatment response, or evaluate unusual side effects, particularly in patients on interacting medications or with conditions affecting drug metabolism.

Short Description: Quantitative blood test that measures the exact level of hCG, used to confirm and monitor early pregnancy.
Long Description: Unlike a simple positive/negative pregnancy test, this quantitative hCG test measures the exact hormone level in the blood, which can help confirm pregnancy, estimate gestational age, and — when repeated over a couple of days — help evaluate whether an early pregnancy is progressing normally, since hCG is expected to roughly double every 48-72 hours in a typical early pregnancy.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though timing relative to the last dose may be requested for accurate interpretation.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of acyclovir, an antiviral medication, used to help guide dosing in specific clinical situations.
Long Description: Acyclovir is an antiviral medication used to treat herpes-family virus infections. Blood level monitoring isn't routine for most patients, but this test can be useful in specific situations — such as evaluating kidney-impaired patients, investigating a lack of treatment response, or assessing possible drug toxicity — using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) methodology for a precise measurement.

Short Description: Qualitative PCR test that detects HIV-1 DNA directly, sometimes used in specific situations like early infant testing.
Long Description: This qualitative PCR test detects HIV-1 genetic material (DNA) directly, rather than relying on antibody-based testing, which can be unreliable in certain situations — most notably in infants born to HIV-positive mothers, since maternal antibodies can persist and cause false-positive antibody results. It's also sometimes used to help clarify results during early infection, before antibodies have had time to develop.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Peach, a food allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Peach, a food allergen that can trigger reactions ranging from mild (hives, itching, digestive upset) to severe (anaphylaxis) in sensitive individuals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps identify a current or recent Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection, the cause of mononucleosis.
Long Description: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) IgM antibodies typically indicate a current or recent infection, as opposed to IgG antibodies, which can reflect current, recent, or long-past infection. This test is commonly used when infectious mononucleosis is suspected but a quicker heterophile ('monospot') test is negative or inconclusive, or when more specific timing information about the infection is needed.

Short Description: Microscopic stool exam that checks for undigested muscle fibers, used to help evaluate suspected malabsorption or pancreatic insufficiency.
Long Description: This test examines a stool sample under the microscope for undigested muscle fibers, which can be a sign that the digestive system — particularly the pancreas — isn't properly breaking down and absorbing proteins from food. It's used to help evaluate suspected malabsorption or pancreatic insufficiency in patients with symptoms like chronic diarrhea, weight loss, or fatty stools.

Short Description: Qualitative blood test that checks for hCG to confirm whether a person is pregnant.
Long Description: This test checks blood for human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG), the hormone produced during pregnancy, and reports a simple positive or negative result. Blood-based qualitative testing can detect pregnancy slightly earlier than a home urine test in some cases, and is often used to confirm pregnancy in a clinical setting.

Short Description: Measures the pH (acidity or alkalinity) of urine, used to help evaluate certain kidney, metabolic, or urinary tract conditions.
Long Description: Urine pH reflects how acidic or alkaline a urine sample is, which can be influenced by diet, certain medications, and various kidney or metabolic conditions. It's often used alongside other urine tests to help evaluate kidney stone risk (since stone type is affected by urine pH), certain infections, or specific kidney function disorders.

Short Description: Measures creatinine in a random urine sample, often used alongside other urine tests to help interpret results relative to urine concentration.
Long Description: Urine creatinine in a random sample is commonly used as a reference point to help 'normalize' other urine test results (such as protein or albumin) by accounting for how concentrated or dilute the sample is, making results more comparable and reliable than a raw measurement alone.

Short Description: Measures amylase in a random urine sample, sometimes used alongside blood amylase to help evaluate pancreas-related symptoms.
Long Description: This test measures amylase, a digestive enzyme, excreted in urine. It's sometimes used alongside blood amylase testing to help evaluate suspected pancreatitis or other pancreas-related conditions, particularly in situations where urine amylase adds useful additional information to the blood test result.

Short Description: Rapid antigen test that detects respiratory syncytial virus (RSV), a common cause of respiratory infection, especially in young children.
Long Description: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) is a common cause of respiratory illness, particularly serious in infants, young children, and older adults. This rapid antigen test detects RSV directly from a respiratory sample, offering quick results to help confirm the diagnosis in patients with compatible respiratory symptoms such as cough, wheezing, or difficulty breathing.

Product Name (Source): QST0008472 HEP C AB w/REFL HCV
Short Description: Screening antibody test for hepatitis C, with automatic confirmatory HCV testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: This test screens for hepatitis C antibodies, with reflex testing meaning that if the initial screen is positive, the lab automatically performs a confirmatory hepatitis C virus (HCV) test to check for current infection, since antibodies alone can't distinguish between an active and a past, resolved infection. It's commonly used for routine hepatitis C screening or investigating abnormal liver enzymes.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for past exposure to Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), the cause of mononucleosis.
Long Description: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) IgG antibodies indicate past exposure to the virus, which is extremely common — most adults have been infected at some point, often without significant symptoms. This test is used to help confirm past exposure or immune status, and alongside IgM and other EBV markers, to help pinpoint the timing of a current or past infection when mononucleosis is being evaluated.

Product Name (Source): QST0008475 Hepatitis B Surface Antibody Immunity, Quantitative (not for STD testing)
Test Name: Hepatitis B Surface Antibody Immunity, Quantitative (not for STD testing)
Short Description: Quantitative antibody test that measures hepatitis B immunity level, often used to confirm vaccine protection.
Long Description: This test measures the specific quantitative level of hepatitis B surface antibody, providing more detail than a simple positive/negative result. It's commonly used to confirm adequate immunity after hepatitis B vaccination — for example, for healthcare workers or others required to document a specific antibody threshold — and to help determine whether a booster dose is needed.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum) — timed 1 hour after a meal or glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires eating a meal or drinking a glucose solution (per your provider's instructions) and returning for a blood draw exactly 1 hour later.
Short Description: Measures blood glucose 1 hour after eating or a glucose challenge, used to help screen for and monitor diabetes or gestational diabetes.
Long Description: This test measures blood glucose exactly one hour after a meal or glucose challenge (postprandial), helping evaluate how well the body manages blood sugar shortly after eating. It's commonly used as part of gestational diabetes screening during pregnancy, or in a broader glucose tolerance workup.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — timed 1 hour after a 50-gram glucose drink
Patient Prep: No fasting is required beforehand; requires drinking a standardized 50-gram glucose solution, with a blood draw exactly 1 hour later.
Short Description: One-hour glucose screening test using a 50-gram glucose challenge, the standard initial screen for gestational diabetes during pregnancy.
Long Description: This is the standard initial screening test for gestational diabetes, measuring blood glucose one hour after drinking a 50-gram glucose solution — no fasting is required beforehand. If the result is elevated, it's typically followed by a longer, more detailed glucose tolerance test to confirm a gestational diabetes diagnosis. It's routinely offered during pregnancy, typically between 24-28 weeks.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for a current or recent cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection.
Long Description: This test detects CMV IgM antibodies, which typically indicate a current or recent infection, as opposed to IgG antibodies, which reflect past exposure or immunity. It's commonly used in pregnancy evaluation, evaluating symptoms of a mono-like illness, or pre-transplant workups when a current CMV infection needs to be distinguished from past exposure.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for exposure to E. coli O157:H7, a bacterial strain that can cause severe gastrointestinal illness.
Long Description: E. coli O157:H7 is a strain of bacteria that can cause severe gastrointestinal illness, including bloody diarrhea, and in some cases serious complications like hemolytic uremic syndrome. While stool culture is the primary way to diagnose an active infection, this antibody (serology) test can sometimes be used to help support a diagnosis retrospectively or in specific clinical situations where culture wasn't obtained during the acute illness.

Specimen Type: Swab from a genital ulcer or lesion
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; collected directly from a symptomatic lesion.
Short Description: Bacterial culture that identifies Haemophilus ducreyi, the cause of chancroid, a sexually transmitted infection.
Long Description: Haemophilus ducreyi is the bacterium responsible for chancroid, a sexually transmitted infection that causes painful genital ulcers. This culture, collected from an active lesion, helps confirm the diagnosis in patients with genital ulcers, particularly when more common causes (like herpes or syphilis) have been ruled out or when chancroid is specifically suspected based on travel history or exposure.

Short Description: Measures sodium in a random urine sample, used alongside blood sodium to help evaluate the body's fluid and electrolyte balance.
Long Description: This test measures sodium excreted in urine, most often used alongside blood sodium and osmolality testing to help evaluate the cause of abnormal sodium levels or fluid balance issues, such as distinguishing dehydration from certain kidney or hormone-related conditions.

Short Description: Separates blood proteins into distinct fractions, used to help evaluate certain blood cancers and related plasma cell disorders.
Long Description: Protein electrophoresis separates blood proteins (such as albumin and globulin fractions) based on their electrical charge, producing a pattern that can reveal abnormal protein bands. In an oncology context, it's commonly used to help diagnose and monitor multiple myeloma and other plasma cell disorders, which often produce a distinctive abnormal protein spike ('M-spike') on this test.

Short Description: Checks for eosinophils in urine, used to help evaluate certain causes of kidney inflammation.
Long Description: Eosinophils are a type of white blood cell that isn't normally found in urine in significant numbers. Their presence can be a clue to certain conditions, most notably a type of kidney inflammation called acute interstitial nephritis, which is sometimes triggered by a reaction to medications. This test is used to help investigate unexplained kidney function changes, particularly after starting a new medication.

Short Description: Measures the absolute number of lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell, used to help evaluate immune function.
Long Description: This test reports the actual number (rather than just the percentage) of lymphocytes, a category of white blood cells central to the immune system, in a given volume of blood. It's used to help evaluate suspected immune deficiencies, certain infections, and blood or lymphatic system disorders, and is often calculated from a complete blood count with differential.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Microscopic examination of urine sediment, used to look for cells, crystals, casts, and bacteria that can indicate kidney or urinary tract conditions.
Long Description: This test examines urine sediment under the microscope, looking for red blood cells, white blood cells, crystals, casts, and bacteria that aren't assessed by the chemical (dipstick) portion of a urinalysis. It's often ordered as a follow-up to an abnormal urinalysis screen, or as part of a complete urinalysis, to help evaluate suspected urinary tract infection, kidney disease, or kidney stones.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps distinguish a past Epstein-Barr virus (EBV) infection from a current or recent one.
Long Description: EBV nuclear antigen (EBNA) antibodies typically don't appear until several weeks to months after an initial Epstein-Barr virus infection and then generally persist for life. Because of this delayed appearance, a positive EBNA result generally points to a past infection rather than a current or recent one, making it a useful piece of a broader EBV antibody panel used to pinpoint infection timing when mononucleosis is being evaluated.

Short Description: Measures copper levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate copper metabolism and conditions like Wilson's disease.
Long Description: This test measures copper excreted in a single, randomly collected urine sample, offering a more convenient (though less standardized) alternative to a full 24-hour urine copper collection. It's used to help evaluate suspected copper metabolism disorders, including Wilson's disease.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — trough (predose) timing required
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed just before the next scheduled gentamicin dose (the trough) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the trough (lowest) blood level of gentamicin, an antibiotic, used to help ensure the drug is clearing properly between doses.
Long Description: Gentamicin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the trough level — the lowest point, drawn just before the next dose — to confirm the drug is clearing from the body appropriately between doses, helping guide safe dosing intervals. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — timed peak sample
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed shortly after a tobramycin dose (the peak) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the peak (highest) blood level of tobramycin, an antibiotic, used to help confirm the dose reached an effective level.
Long Description: Tobramycin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the peak level — drawn shortly after a dose — to confirm the medication reached an effective concentration, helping guide safe and effective dosing. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — trough (predose) timing required
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed just before the next scheduled tobramycin dose (the trough) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the trough (lowest) blood level of tobramycin, an antibiotic, used to help ensure the drug is clearing properly between doses.
Long Description: Tobramycin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the trough level — the lowest point, drawn just before the next dose — to confirm the drug is clearing from the body appropriately between doses, helping guide safe dosing intervals. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — timed peak sample
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed shortly after a gentamicin dose (the peak) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the peak (highest) blood level of gentamicin, an antibiotic, used to help confirm the dose reached an effective level.
Long Description: Gentamicin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the peak level — drawn shortly after a dose — to confirm the medication reached an effective concentration, helping guide safe and effective dosing. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — trough (predose) timing required
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed just before the next scheduled amikacin dose (the trough) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the trough (lowest) blood level of amikacin, an antibiotic, used to help ensure the drug is clearing properly between doses.
Long Description: Amikacin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the trough level — the lowest point, drawn just before the next dose — to confirm the drug is clearing from the body appropriately between doses, helping guide safe dosing intervals. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma) — timed peak sample
Patient Prep: Requires a blood draw timed shortly after an amikacin dose (the peak) — timing must be coordinated with your provider or infusion team.
Short Description: Measures the peak (highest) blood level of amikacin, an antibiotic, used to help confirm the dose reached an effective level.
Long Description: Amikacin is a powerful antibiotic that requires careful dosing, since too little may not effectively treat infection while too much can cause kidney or hearing damage. This test measures the peak level — drawn shortly after a dose — to confirm the medication reached an effective concentration, helping guide safe and effective dosing. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for a current or recent cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection using an ELISA-based method.
Long Description: This test detects CMV IgM antibodies using an ELISA-based method, which typically indicate a current or recent infection, as opposed to IgG antibodies, which reflect past exposure or immunity. It's commonly used in pregnancy evaluation, evaluating symptoms of a mono-like illness, or pre-transplant workups when a current CMV infection needs to be distinguished from past exposure.

Short Description: Confirmatory Western blot antibody test for Lyme disease, used to verify a positive Lyme screening result.
Long Description: This test looks for a specific pattern of IgG and IgM antibody bands using Western blot methodology, used as the confirmatory second step after a positive or equivocal initial Lyme disease screening test, consistent with standard two-tiered Lyme disease testing guidelines. It helps reduce false positives before reporting a confirmed Lyme disease diagnosis.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to mumps, often used to confirm vaccine protection or investigate a suspected infection.
Long Description: This test detects mumps virus IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from past infection or vaccination. It's used to help confirm immune status when vaccination history is uncertain, or to help support a diagnosis when mumps infection is suspected based on symptoms like swollen salivary glands.

Short Description: Antigen test that specifically detects Giardia, a common parasitic cause of gastrointestinal infection.
Long Description: This test detects Giardia antigen directly in a stool sample, offering greater sensitivity for this specific parasite than standard microscopic stool exams alone. It's commonly ordered for patients with persistent diarrhea, especially after travel, camping, or exposure to contaminated water, since Giardia is one of the more common causes of parasitic gastrointestinal infection.

Specimen Type: Sample type depends on the site being tested (e.g., skin scraping, tissue, or body fluid)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample should be collected from the affected site.
Short Description: Rapid microscopic stain that checks for fungal elements in a sample, offering quick preliminary results before a fungal culture is complete.
Long Description: This test uses a special stain to look for fungal elements directly under the microscope, providing much faster preliminary results than waiting for a fungal culture to grow (which can take days to weeks). It's often used alongside culture to help quickly support or rule out a fungal infection while more definitive culture results are pending.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample is typically collected from a suspicious skin lesion or burrow.
Short Description: Microscopic skin exam that checks for scabies mites, eggs, or fecal matter, used to confirm a scabies infestation.
Long Description: This test examines a skin scraping under the microscope for scabies mites, their eggs, or fecal pellets, providing direct confirmation of a scabies infestation. It's typically ordered when a patient has an intensely itchy rash with skin findings suggestive of scabies, to confirm the diagnosis before starting treatment.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected during active diarrhea symptoms.
Short Description: Stool culture specifically targeting E. coli O157:H7, a bacterial strain that can cause severe gastrointestinal illness.
Long Description: E. coli O157:H7 is a strain of bacteria that can cause severe gastrointestinal illness, including bloody diarrhea, and in some cases serious complications like hemolytic uremic syndrome. This culture uses specialized growth conditions to specifically detect this strain, and is commonly ordered for patients with bloody diarrhea or suspected foodborne illness, especially after eating undercooked ground beef or unpasteurized products.

Short Description: Combined antibody test for Toxoplasma gondii, checking both IgG and IgM together for a fuller picture of infection timing.
Long Description: This test measures both Toxoplasma IgG (indicating past exposure) and IgM (suggesting a more recent infection) together, helping a provider better determine when exposure to this parasite may have occurred. It's especially relevant during pregnancy, since a new infection during pregnancy carries risks to the developing baby, and in patients with weakened immune systems.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgG antibody response to yellow hornet venom, sometimes used to help monitor venom immunotherapy.
Long Description: This test measures IgG antibodies to yellow hornet venom, which is different from the more standard IgE testing used to diagnose an insect sting allergy. IgG venom antibody testing is sometimes used to help monitor a patient's response to venom immunotherapy (allergy shots for insect stings), rather than to diagnose the initial allergy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgG antibody response to paper wasp venom, sometimes used to help monitor venom immunotherapy.
Long Description: This test measures IgG antibodies to paper wasp venom, which is different from the more standard IgE testing used to diagnose an insect sting allergy. IgG venom antibody testing is sometimes used to help monitor a patient's response to venom immunotherapy (allergy shots for insect stings), rather than to diagnose the initial allergy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgG antibody response to honey bee venom, sometimes used to help monitor venom immunotherapy.
Long Description: This test measures IgG antibodies to honey bee venom, which is different from the more standard IgE testing used to diagnose an insect sting allergy. IgG venom antibody testing is sometimes used to help monitor a patient's response to venom immunotherapy (allergy shots for insect stings), rather than to diagnose the initial allergy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgG antibody response to white-faced hornet venom, sometimes used to help monitor venom immunotherapy.
Long Description: This test measures IgG antibodies to white-faced hornet venom, which is different from the more standard IgE testing used to diagnose an insect sting allergy. IgG venom antibody testing is sometimes used to help monitor a patient's response to venom immunotherapy (allergy shots for insect stings), rather than to diagnose the initial allergy.

Short Description: Allergy blood test that checks for an IgG antibody response to yellow jacket venom, sometimes used to help monitor venom immunotherapy.
Long Description: This test measures IgG antibodies to yellow jacket venom, which is different from the more standard IgE testing used to diagnose an insect sting allergy. IgG venom antibody testing is sometimes used to help monitor a patient's response to venom immunotherapy (allergy shots for insect stings), rather than to diagnose the initial allergy.

Short Description: Measures the exact antibody titer (dilution endpoint) for a positive ANA result, giving more detail than a simple positive/negative screen.
Long Description: When an ANA (antinuclear antibody) screen is positive, this test determines the specific titer — how many times the sample can be diluted and still show a positive result — which reflects the strength of the antibody response. Higher titers are generally considered more clinically significant, and this information helps a provider assess the likelihood of an underlying autoimmune condition alongside symptoms and other testing.

Short Description: Measures alpha subunit, a hormone component shared by several pituitary hormones, used in specialized pituitary tumor evaluations.
Long Description: Several pituitary hormones (including TSH, LH, and FSH) share a common 'alpha subunit' building block. Certain pituitary tumors can produce this subunit alone, in excess of the complete, functional hormones. This specialized test is used by endocrinologists to help evaluate suspected pituitary tumors, particularly when other hormone tests suggest an unusual pattern.

Short Description: Quantitative blood clot breakdown marker used to help evaluate suspected blood clots, such as deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism.
Long Description: D-dimer is a breakdown product formed when the body dissolves blood clots, so elevated levels can be a clue that a clot is present or recently formed somewhere in the body. This quantitative version measures the exact level rather than just a positive/negative result, and is commonly used to help evaluate suspected deep vein thrombosis (DVT) or pulmonary embolism, and to help assess clotting risk in certain other conditions. A normal result is particularly useful for helping rule out a clot, since D-dimer is very sensitive though not very specific.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for a current or recent varicella-zoster virus (VZV) infection.
Long Description: This test detects varicella-zoster virus (VZV) IgM antibodies, which typically suggest a more recent infection, as opposed to IgG antibodies, which indicate past infection or immunity. It's used to help support a diagnosis when a current chickenpox or shingles infection is suspected but needs further confirmation beyond clinical exam.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should be performed while still eating gluten-containing foods for accurate results.
Short Description: Antibody test (tissue transglutaminase IgA) that helps screen for celiac disease.
Long Description: Tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA antibody is the primary first-line blood test used to screen for celiac disease, an autoimmune condition triggered by gluten. It's commonly ordered for patients with digestive symptoms, unexplained nutrient deficiencies, or a family history of celiac disease; because testing accuracy depends on ongoing gluten intake, it's important not to start a gluten-free diet before this test unless directed by a provider.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Should be drawn without prolonged tourniquet time, since this can affect calcium results.
Short Description: Combined test that measures intact parathyroid hormone (PTH) and calcium together, used to help evaluate parathyroid gland and calcium health.
Long Description: Intact PTH and calcium work closely together to regulate the body's calcium balance. Measuring them together helps a provider evaluate suspected parathyroid disorders — such as hyperparathyroidism or hypoparathyroidism — as well as other causes of abnormal calcium levels, since interpreting one without the other can miss part of the picture.

Short Description: Measures how long it takes blood to clot through one clotting pathway, reported with an INR value for standardized interpretation, often used to monitor blood thinners like warfarin.
Long Description: Prothrombin time (PT) measures how long it takes blood to clot through one of the body's major clotting pathways, and the INR (international normalized ratio) standardizes this result so it can be compared consistently across different labs and testing methods. This combined test is one of the most common ways to monitor patients on the blood thinner warfarin, helping ensure their dose keeps clotting time in a safe, effective range, and is also used more broadly to help evaluate bleeding disorders or liver function.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should be performed while still eating gluten-containing foods for accurate results.
Short Description: Older antibody test (anti-gliadin IgG/IgA) sometimes used alongside newer markers in a celiac disease workup.
Long Description: Anti-gliadin antibodies were among the earlier blood tests used to help screen for celiac disease, though they've largely been replaced by more accurate markers like tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies in most current testing protocols. This test may still be used in certain specific situations, such as evaluating for a related condition (non-celiac gluten sensitivity) or in patients where other celiac markers have given ambiguous results.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though results are interpreted differently depending on whether the sample is fasting or random.
Short Description: Measures blood glucose (sugar) level in plasma at a random, non-fasting point in time.
Long Description: This test measures glucose in a plasma sample taken at a random time, without regard to when the patient last ate. It's commonly used for quick screening, evaluating symptoms like excessive thirst or fatigue, or in urgent situations where waiting for a fasting sample isn't practical; results are interpreted with this random timing in mind.

Short Description: Specialized clotting test that helps determine whether a prolonged clotting time is due to a missing clotting factor or an inhibitor antibody.
Long Description: When a screening clotting test (like PT or PTT) comes back prolonged, a mixing study combines the patient's plasma with normal plasma to see whether the clotting time corrects. If it corrects, a missing clotting factor is more likely; if it doesn't fully correct, an inhibitor (an antibody interfering with clotting) is more likely. This test is typically ordered by a specialist as part of a more detailed workup for unexplained bleeding or abnormal clotting test results.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Banana, a food allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Banana, a food allergen that can trigger reactions ranging from mild (hives, itching, digestive upset) to severe (anaphylaxis) in sensitive individuals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Latex, an occupational/contact allergen.
