This test is commonly used to help diagnose heart failure in patients with symptoms like shortness of breath or swelling, distinguish heart-related causes of these symptoms from other causes (like lung disease), and monitor known heart failure patients over time.

Short Description: Genetic test that looks at a variation in the ACE gene, sometimes studied in relation to cardiovascular health and physical performance.
Long Description: This test identifies which variant of the ACE (angiotensin converting enzyme) gene a person carries. This genetic variation has been studied in relation to blood pressure regulation, cardiovascular risk, and athletic performance characteristics, though its practical clinical use is more limited and specialized compared to other genetic and cardiovascular tests, and it's often ordered in specific research or specialty contexts.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should be performed while still eating gluten-containing foods for accurate results.
Short Description: Older antibody test (anti-gliadin IgG) sometimes used alongside newer markers in a celiac disease workup.
Long Description: Anti-gliadin IgG antibodies were among the earlier blood tests used to help screen for celiac disease, though they've largely been replaced by more accurate markers like tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies in most current testing protocols. This test may still be used in certain specific situations, such as evaluating for a related condition (non-celiac gluten sensitivity) or in patients where other celiac markers have given ambiguous results.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should be performed while still eating gluten-containing foods for accurate results.
Short Description: Older antibody test (anti-gliadin IgA) sometimes used alongside newer markers in a celiac disease workup.
Long Description: Anti-gliadin IgA antibodies were among the earlier blood tests used to help screen for celiac disease, though they've largely been replaced by more accurate markers like tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies in most current testing protocols. This test may still be used in certain specific situations, such as evaluating for a related condition (non-celiac gluten sensitivity) or in patients where other celiac markers have given ambiguous results.

Short Description: Measures kappa and lambda free light chains and their ratio, used to help diagnose and monitor multiple myeloma and related plasma cell disorders.
Long Description: Kappa and lambda are the two types of light chain proteins that make up antibodies, and an abnormal ratio between them can be an early and sensitive clue to multiple myeloma and other plasma cell disorders. This test is commonly used to help diagnose these conditions, monitor treatment response, and screen for disease recurrence, often alongside protein electrophoresis.

Short Description: Bundled allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks IgE antibody response to several common seafood and shellfish allergens.
Long Description: This panel groups together several individual ImmunoCAP allergen-specific IgE tests covering seafood and shellfish, common food allergens that can trigger reactions ranging from mild to severe (anaphylaxis), rather than testing one allergen at a time. A positive (elevated) result for any allergen in the panel indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against it, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, so results should be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. Panels like this are a convenient, often more cost-effective way to screen for multiple related allergens at once rather than ordering each individually; exact allergens included can vary by region/panel version, so it's worth confirming the specific component list with your lab.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures free cortisol excreted in urine over 24 hours, one of the standard tests used to help diagnose Cushing's syndrome.
Long Description: This test measures the amount of cortisol excreted, unbound, in urine over a full 24-hour period, which reflects overall cortisol production averaged across the whole day rather than a single point-in-time blood level. It's one of the standard first-line tests used to help diagnose Cushing's syndrome, a condition caused by excess cortisol production.

Product Name (Source): QST0011290 FECAL IMMUNOCHEM Insure One Colon Screen
Test Name: FECAL IMMUNOCHEM Insure One Colon Screen
Specimen Type: Stool sample (collected at home using a kit)
Patient Prep: Uses an at-home collection kit; no dietary or medication restrictions are typically required, unlike older guaiac-based stool tests — follow the kit's specific instructions.
Short Description: Fecal immunochemical test (FIT) used to screen for colorectal cancer by checking for hidden blood in the stool.
Long Description: This fecal immunochemical test (FIT) checks for hidden (occult) blood in the stool, which can be an early sign of colorectal cancer or precancerous polyps. It's a standard, widely recommended colorectal cancer screening option for average-risk adults, done conveniently at home, and unlike older stool blood tests, doesn't require dietary restrictions beforehand. A positive result is typically followed by a colonoscopy for further evaluation.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures magnesium excreted in urine over 24 hours, used to help evaluate the cause of abnormal blood magnesium levels.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of magnesium excreted in urine over a full 24-hour period, most often used alongside blood magnesium to help distinguish whether abnormal magnesium levels are due to a kidney-related cause versus other causes like poor dietary intake or gastrointestinal losses.

Product Name (Source): QST0011348 Hepatitis C Viral RNA Quantitative Real-Time PCR with Reflex to Genotype LiPA
Test Name: Hepatitis C Viral RNA Quantitative Real-Time PCR with Reflex to Genotype LiPA
Short Description: Measures hepatitis C viral load, with automatic reflex testing to determine the viral genotype if virus is detected.
Long Description: This test quantifies hepatitis C virus RNA (viral load) in the blood, and includes reflex genotype testing, meaning if virus is detected, the specific hepatitis C genotype is automatically determined as well. Genotype can influence treatment choice and duration, so combining these tests helps a provider get both pieces of information needed to plan or monitor antiviral treatment in one order.

Specimen Type: Urine or urethral/cervical swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Molecular (DNA) test that detects Chlamydia trachomatis using strand displacement amplification technology.
Long Description: This test uses strand displacement amplification (SDA), a molecular (DNA-based) technology, to detect Chlamydia trachomatis, one of the most common sexually transmitted infections. It's used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up, valued for its high sensitivity.

Product Name (Source): QST0011362 Neisseria gonorrhoeae RNA, TMA, Urogenital (Gonorrhea)
Specimen Type: Urine or urogenital swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Molecular (RNA) test that detects Neisseria gonorrhoeae, the bacterium responsible for gonorrhea.
Long Description: This test uses transcription-mediated amplification (TMA), a highly sensitive molecular technology, to detect Neisseria gonorrhoeae RNA from a urine or urogenital sample. It's widely used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up, given its high sensitivity for detecting this common sexually transmitted infection.

Specimen Type: Urine or urethral/cervical swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Combined molecular test that detects both Chlamydia and Gonorrhea using strand displacement amplification technology.
Long Description: This test uses strand displacement amplification (SDA), a molecular (DNA-based) technology, to detect both Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae from a single sample, offering an efficient way to screen for two of the most common sexually transmitted infections at once. It's widely used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up.

Product Name (Source): QST0014312 HIV-1 and HIV-2 RNA, Qualitative Real-Time PCR
Test Name: HIV-1 and HIV-2 RNA, Qualitative Real-Time PCR
Short Description: Qualitative PCR test that detects both HIV-1 and HIV-2, used to help diagnose early or unusual HIV infections.
Long Description: This qualitative PCR test checks for genetic material from both HIV-1 (the most common type worldwide) and HIV-2 (less common, found mostly in West Africa), reporting a positive or negative result. It's used in specific situations where standard antibody-based HIV testing may be insufficient — such as very early infection before antibodies have developed, or when HIV-2 is a specific concern based on exposure history.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; your provider may specify a random or timed collection.
Short Description: Measures total protein in a urine sample without a creatinine comparison, used to help evaluate kidney health.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of protein in a urine sample without calculating a ratio against creatinine. Elevated urine protein (proteinuria) can be an early sign of kidney disease, and this test is used to help evaluate and monitor kidney conditions, though a protein-to-creatinine ratio test is often preferred when accounting for urine concentration is important.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though results can take longer to process than typical blood tests, since cells need to be grown and analyzed.
Short Description: Genetic test that checks for chromosomal mosaicism, a condition where a person has two or more genetically different cell lines.
Long Description: Chromosomal mosaicism occurs when a person has two or more cell populations with different genetic makeups, often due to an error that occurred after conception. This test examines a patient's cells for evidence of mosaicism, which can be relevant to certain developmental conditions, unexplained symptoms, or specific genetic syndromes, and is typically ordered by a geneticist investigating one of these situations.

Short Description: Measures the number and/or activity of natural killer (NK) cells, a part of the immune system sometimes evaluated in reproductive immunology or immune deficiency workups.
Long Description: Natural killer (NK) cells are white blood cells that play a role in the body's innate immune defense against infected or abnormal cells. This test measures NK cell levels and, depending on the specific assay, activity, and is used in a few different contexts — including general immune function evaluation, and by some reproductive specialists investigating recurrent pregnancy loss, though the use of NK cell testing in that setting is debated and not universally accepted in mainstream reproductive medicine. Results are best interpreted by a specialist familiar with the specific clinical question being asked.

Short Description: Measures CK-MB, a heart-specific enzyme, along with its ratio to total CK, used to help evaluate suspected heart attack or heart muscle injury.
Long Description: CK-MB is a form of creatine kinase found more specifically in heart muscle than the total CK measurement alone, and calculating its ratio (relative index) to total CK helps determine whether an elevated CK is more likely coming from the heart or from skeletal muscle. This test is used to help evaluate suspected heart attack or other heart muscle injury, though troponin has largely become the preferred primary marker in most modern cardiac workups.

Product Name (Source): QST0014839 Helicobacter pylori, Urea Breath Test (H. Pylori)
Test Name: Helicobacter pylori, Urea Breath Test (H. Pylori)
Specimen Type: Breath sample (collected in-office after drinking a test solution)
Patient Prep: Requires fasting for several hours beforehand and avoiding certain medications (such as antibiotics, bismuth, and acid-reducing drugs) for one to two weeks prior — follow your provider's specific preparation instructions closely, since these can affect results.
Short Description: Breath test that detects an active Helicobacter pylori (H. pylori) infection, a common cause of stomach ulcers.
Long Description: H. pylori is a bacterium that can infect the stomach lining and is a leading cause of peptic ulcers and a risk factor for stomach cancer. This breath test detects byproducts produced by active H. pylori bacteria after drinking a specially labeled test solution, offering a non-invasive and highly accurate way to confirm an active infection, as well as to confirm successful treatment afterward.

Product Name (Source): QST0014852 LIPID PNL W/REF DIR LDL
Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results.
Short Description: Standard lipid panel with automatic reflex to a direct LDL measurement when needed, for more accurate cardiovascular risk assessment.
Long Description: This lipid panel includes reflex testing, meaning that if calculated LDL cholesterol is expected to be unreliable (such as when triglycerides are very high), a direct LDL measurement is automatically performed instead, ensuring a more accurate result without requiring a separate order. It's used the same way as a standard lipid panel — for cardiovascular risk assessment and monitoring cholesterol-lowering treatment.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (serum or plasma, depending on the specific components)
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though results can be affected by certain blood thinners — let your provider know about current medications.
Short Description: Combined antiphospholipid antibody panel used to help evaluate antiphospholipid syndrome, a clotting-related autoimmune condition.
Long Description: This panel combines several antiphospholipid antibody tests (which can include cardiolipin antibodies, lupus anticoagulant, and beta-2 glycoprotein antibodies, depending on the specific version) into one order, offering a more complete evaluation for antiphospholipid syndrome than any single antibody test alone. It's typically ordered as part of a broader workup for unexplained blood clots or recurrent pregnancy loss; it's worth confirming with your lab exactly which component tests are included.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is often preferred, and avoiding stress, exercise, or nipple stimulation beforehand may be recommended, since these can temporarily raise levels — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures prolactin, a pituitary hormone, used to help evaluate irregular periods, fertility issues, or unexplained milk production.
Long Description: Prolactin is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland best known for stimulating breast milk production, but it can also affect menstrual cycles and fertility when elevated. This test is commonly used to help evaluate irregular or absent periods, fertility difficulties, unexplained nipple discharge, or symptoms suggestive of a pituitary tumor (prolactinoma), which is one of the more common causes of significantly elevated prolactin.

Short Description: Measures core electrolytes (sodium, potassium, chloride, and CO2) using a plasma sample, used to help evaluate fluid and acid-base balance.
Long Description: This panel measures the body's core electrolytes — sodium, potassium, chloride, and carbon dioxide (bicarbonate) — using a plasma sample. It's commonly used to help evaluate hydration status, kidney function, and acid-base balance, and is a routine part of general health screening and monitoring for many chronic conditions and medications.

Specimen Type: Tumor tissue sample (from a prior biopsy or surgical specimen)
Patient Prep: This test requires a tumor tissue sample, typically from a previous biopsy or surgery — no separate blood draw or new procedure is usually needed if tissue is already available.
Short Description: Genetic tumor test that checks for microsatellite instability (MSI), used to help evaluate certain cancers, including for Lynch syndrome and to help guide certain cancer treatment decisions.
Long Description: Microsatellite instability (MSI) reflects a specific type of genetic instability that occurs in certain tumors, most notably some colorectal and endometrial cancers, and can be a sign of Lynch syndrome, an inherited condition that raises cancer risk. This test is used to help evaluate newly diagnosed cancers for this feature, both to help identify patients who should be screened for Lynch syndrome and because MSI status can influence eligibility for certain modern cancer treatments (such as immunotherapy).

Specimen Type: Urine or urogenital swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Molecular (RNA) test that detects Chlamydia trachomatis using an alternate transcription-mediated amplification protocol.
Long Description: This test uses transcription-mediated amplification (TMA), a highly sensitive molecular technology, to detect Chlamydia trachomatis RNA, using an alternate assay protocol/specimen pathway from other chlamydia molecular tests in the catalog. It's used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up.

Specimen Type: Urine or urogenital swab, depending on your provider's preference
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on the site being tested.
Short Description: Molecular (RNA) test that detects Neisseria gonorrhoeae using transcription-mediated amplification technology.
Long Description: This test uses transcription-mediated amplification (TMA), a highly sensitive molecular technology, to detect Neisseria gonorrhoeae RNA, the bacterium responsible for gonorrhea. It's widely used for routine STD screening, symptomatic evaluation, or partner notification follow-up.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; typically collected around 36-37 weeks of pregnancy per standard prenatal screening guidelines.
Short Description: Group B Streptococcus culture with antibiotic susceptibility testing, used in prenatal screening.
Long Description: This test cultures a vaginal/rectal swab for Group B Streptococcus (GBS), a bacterium that can affect newborns if present during delivery, and includes susceptibility testing to identify which antibiotics would be effective if treatment is needed — particularly important information for patients with a penicillin allergy. It's a standard part of prenatal care, typically performed in the third trimester.

Specimen Type: 24-hour or random urine sample, per collection instructions
Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider may specify a particular collection method.
Short Description: Screens urine for multiple heavy metals at once, used to help evaluate suspected exposure to metals such as lead, mercury, or arsenic.
Long Description: This panel checks a urine sample for several heavy metals together (commonly including metals such as lead, mercury, and arsenic, though the exact panel can vary by lab), offering a broad initial screen for suspected heavy metal exposure or toxicity. It's often used when a patient has symptoms or an exposure history that could relate to more than one type of metal, since testing several at once is more efficient than ordering each individually.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though ejaculation, prostate exams, or certain procedures shortly before the test may be asked to be avoided, since they can temporarily affect results.
Short Description: Measures total PSA (prostate-specific antigen) reported against a lower 2.5 ng/mL screening threshold, sometimes used for closer prostate monitoring.
Long Description: This test measures total PSA the same way as a standard PSA test, but results are reported and flagged against a lower cutoff value (2.5 ng/mL) than the traditional 4.0 ng/mL threshold. Some providers use this more sensitive threshold for younger men or those considered at higher risk, aiming to catch potential prostate concerns earlier, though this approach involves tradeoffs in false positives that are worth discussing with a provider.

Short Description: Measures kappa and lambda free light chains with reflex immunofixation, used to help diagnose and monitor multiple myeloma and related plasma cell disorders.
Long Description: This test measures kappa and lambda free light chain levels and their ratio, with reflex testing meaning that if results are abnormal, immunofixation (a more detailed protein test) is automatically performed to help further characterize the finding. It's commonly used to help diagnose and monitor multiple myeloma and other plasma cell disorders.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though timing relative to the last dose may be requested for accurate interpretation.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of levetiracetam (Keppra), an anti-seizure medication, to help confirm safe and effective dosing.
Long Description: Levetiracetam is a commonly prescribed anti-seizure medication. This test measures its concentration in the blood to help a prescriber confirm the medication is at an effective, well-tolerated level, which can be useful for checking compliance, adjusting doses during pregnancy or with other medication changes, or investigating side effects. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period.
Short Description: Measures 17-hydroxycorticosteroids in a 24-hour urine sample, an older test used to help evaluate adrenal gland function.
Long Description: 17-hydroxycorticosteroids are breakdown products of cortisol and related adrenal hormones, and measuring them in a 24-hour urine collection was historically used to help evaluate overall adrenal hormone production. It has become less commonly used as more specific and convenient tests (like free cortisol or ACTH stimulation testing) have become the preferred approach in most modern adrenal function workups.

Patient Prep: For therapeutic drug monitoring, a trough (predose) draw is typically recommended.
Short Description: Measures blood levels of cyclosporine using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used to help guide dosing in transplant patients.
Long Description: Cyclosporine is an immunosuppressant medication commonly used to help prevent organ rejection after transplant. This test measures its concentration in the blood using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, to help a prescriber confirm the medication is at an effective, well-tolerated level. This is a drug-monitoring test meant to support, not replace, guidance from a treating clinician.

Patient Prep: No fasting required; specific timing may be requested — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Detailed steroid hormone ratio panel used to help evaluate congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), a group of inherited adrenal gland disorders.
Long Description: This panel measures several adrenal steroid hormones and calculates specific ratios between them to help evaluate congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), a group of inherited conditions that affect the adrenal glands' ability to produce certain hormones properly. It's typically ordered by an endocrinologist investigating symptoms suggestive of CAH, offering more detailed information than measuring individual hormones alone.

Short Description: Genetic test that looks for mutations in the alpha-1-antitrypsin (AAT) gene, used to help confirm a diagnosis of AAT deficiency.
Long Description: This genetic test looks directly for specific mutations in the AAT gene, used to confirm a diagnosis of alpha-1-antitrypsin deficiency — an inherited condition linked to early-onset emphysema/COPD and certain liver diseases — typically after blood AAT protein levels come back low. It's also used to determine which specific gene variant a patient carries, which can provide additional information about disease risk and help with family genetic counseling.

Short Description: Measures ferritin, the body's main iron storage protein, with automatic follow-up testing if results are abnormal.
Long Description: Ferritin reflects how much iron is stored in the body, and this version includes reflex testing, meaning if the result is abnormal, additional related iron testing is automatically performed to help clarify the finding. It's commonly ordered to investigate fatigue, suspected anemia, or to monitor iron status in people with known iron disorders or exposure-related concerns.

Short Description: Thyroid panel with automatic reflex testing based on the initial TSH result, used to give an efficient, tailored picture of thyroid function.
Long Description: This thyroid panel starts with TSH, the primary thyroid screening test, and includes reflex testing, meaning if the TSH result is abnormal, additional related thyroid tests (such as free T4) are automatically performed without needing a separate order. This tailored approach avoids unnecessary testing when TSH is normal, while still ensuring a fuller picture is obtained when it isn't.

Short Description: Screening version of the rheumatoid factor test, used to help evaluate rheumatoid arthritis and other autoimmune joint conditions.
Long Description: Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an antibody found in many, though not all, people with rheumatoid arthritis, and can also appear in some other autoimmune or chronic conditions. This screening version reports a qualitative or semi-quantitative result, commonly used as an initial check when rheumatoid arthritis or another autoimmune joint condition is suspected based on symptoms like joint pain, swelling, and stiffness.

Short Description: Measures the exact antibody titer for rheumatoid factor, giving more detail than a simple positive/negative screen.
Long Description: This test determines the specific titer of rheumatoid factor (RF) antibodies, giving a more precise measure of antibody strength than a simple positive/negative or screening-level result. It's used to help further characterize a positive RF screen, particularly useful for monitoring disease activity or supporting a rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis alongside clinical findings.

Short Description: Combined antibody test for influenza B, checking both IgG and IgM together for a fuller picture of infection timing.
Long Description: This test measures both influenza B IgG (indicating past exposure or immunity) and IgM (suggesting a more recent infection) together, helping a provider determine the likely timing of exposure to this strain of flu virus. It's used less commonly than rapid antigen or PCR testing for diagnosing an active case, but can be useful in specific situations requiring information about past exposure or immune response.

Short Description: Older thyroid test used alongside other thyroid hormone levels to help estimate free thyroid hormone activity, with automatic follow-up testing if needed.
Long Description: T3 uptake is a test that indirectly reflects how much of the thyroid hormone-binding protein in the blood is already occupied, helping estimate the proportion of thyroid hormone that's 'free' (biologically active). This version includes reflex testing to help clarify results when needed. It's most useful when interpreted together with total T4, and is less commonly ordered today since direct free T4 tests are now widely available and often preferred.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle matters for interpretation in menstruating patients — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Rapid (STAT) estradiol test, used when a quick result is needed to guide urgent fertility treatment or clinical decisions.
Long Description: This is a rapid-turnaround version of the standard estradiol test, processed on an expedited basis rather than routine timing. It's most often used in fertility treatment monitoring, where same-day estradiol results are often needed to guide timing of medications or procedures, and is interpreted the same way as a standard estradiol result.

Short Description: Panel that screens for antibodies to several fungal pathogens at once, used to help evaluate suspected fungal infection.
Long Description: This panel combines antibody tests for several different fungal organisms into one order, offering a broad initial screen when a fungal infection is suspected but the specific organism isn't yet known. Exact fungal targets included can vary by lab, so it's worth confirming with your lab which specific organisms this panel covers; it's typically ordered based on symptoms, imaging findings, or relevant geographic/exposure history.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is often preferred, since testosterone levels naturally peak earlier in the day.
Short Description: Measures total testosterone using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, especially useful for lower testosterone ranges seen in women and children.
Long Description: This test measures total testosterone using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method that performs particularly well at the lower testosterone levels typically seen in women and children, where standard immunoassay methods can be less accurate. It's used to help evaluate conditions like PCOS, excess hair growth, or other hormone-related concerns where a more precise testosterone measurement matters.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Basic urinalysis with automatic follow-up microscopic exam if the initial results suggest it's needed.
Long Description: This macroscopic urinalysis evaluates the visual appearance and chemical properties of urine, and includes reflex testing, meaning if results suggest an abnormality (such as signs of infection or blood), a microscopic exam is automatically added to provide more detail. This tailored approach avoids unnecessary microscopic exams when the initial screen is unremarkable.

Short Description: PCR test that detects Chlamydia pneumoniae, a bacterium that can cause respiratory infections such as walking pneumonia.
Long Description: Chlamydia pneumoniae is a bacterium (distinct from the sexually transmitted Chlamydia trachomatis) that can cause respiratory infections, including bronchitis and a relatively mild form of pneumonia sometimes called 'walking pneumonia.' This PCR test detects the organism's genetic material directly from a respiratory sample, helping confirm the diagnosis in patients with compatible respiratory symptoms.

Short Description: Antibody test (anticentromere) that helps evaluate a limited form of scleroderma, an autoimmune connective tissue disease.
Long Description: Anticentromere antibodies are strongly associated with a limited form of systemic sclerosis (scleroderma), sometimes referred to as CREST syndrome, which tends to progress more slowly than the diffuse form of the disease. This test is commonly ordered when a patient has symptoms suggestive of scleroderma, such as Raynaud's phenomenon, skin thickening limited to the fingers/face, or difficulty swallowing, to help support this specific diagnosis.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though results can take longer to process than typical blood tests given the detailed genetic analysis involved.
Short Description: Chromosomal microarray analysis that scans the entire genome for genetic gains, losses, or other abnormalities.
Long Description: This branded chromosomal microarray test (comparative genomic hybridization, or CGH) scans a patient's entire genome at high resolution, looking for small genetic gains, losses, or other structural abnormalities that traditional chromosome (karyotype) analysis might miss. It's commonly used to help evaluate unexplained developmental delay, intellectual disability, autism spectrum features, or multiple birth defects, and can also be used in certain prenatal or pregnancy loss evaluations.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though your provider will advise on whether to continue or pause aspirin before testing, depending on what's being evaluated.
Short Description: Platelet function test that checks whether aspirin is effectively reducing platelet clotting activity.
Long Description: This test evaluates how well a patient's platelets respond to aspirin therapy, helping identify 'aspirin resistance' — a reduced anti-clotting effect from the medication in some patients. It's used to help guide treatment decisions in patients on aspirin for cardiovascular protection, particularly when there's concern the medication isn't working as expected.

Patient Prep: A clean-catch, midstream urine sample is generally recommended to reduce contamination.
Short Description: Microscopic urinalysis with automatic follow-up testing if results suggest it's needed.
Long Description: This test examines urine sediment under the microscope for cells, crystals, casts, and bacteria, and includes reflex testing, meaning additional related testing is automatically performed if the findings suggest it would add useful information. It's often ordered as a follow-up to an abnormal urinalysis screen to help evaluate suspected urinary tract infection, kidney disease, or kidney stones.

Short Description: Measures IGF-1 using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used to help evaluate growth hormone-related conditions.
Long Description: Insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF-1) reflects average growth hormone activity over time, making it more stable and reliable for testing than growth hormone itself, which fluctuates significantly through the day. This test measures IGF-1 using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, and is used to help evaluate suspected growth hormone deficiency or excess (such as acromegaly).

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin A1c with automatic follow-up testing if results are unusual or inconsistent with other findings.
Long Description: Hemoglobin A1c reflects average blood sugar control over roughly the prior 2-3 months. This version includes reflex testing, meaning if the result is unusual or doesn't fit the expected clinical picture (such as in certain blood conditions that can affect A1c accuracy), additional testing is automatically performed to help clarify the finding. It's used the same way as standard A1c testing to diagnose and monitor diabetes.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; sample should ideally be collected during active diarrhea symptoms.
Short Description: Qualitative stool test that detects C. difficile toxin B using a sensitive molecular method, used to help diagnose C. diff infection.
Long Description: Clostridioides difficile (C. diff) is a bacterium that can cause diarrhea and colitis, often following antibiotic use. This qualitative test detects C. diff toxin B in stool using a highly sensitive molecular detection method, helping confirm an active infection in patients with compatible symptoms — most notably diarrhea that develops during or after antibiotic treatment, or in a healthcare setting.

Product Name (Source): QST0016506 Chlamydia/Neisseria gonorrhoeae RNA, TMA, Rectal
Short Description: Molecular (RNA) test that detects both Chlamydia and Gonorrhea from a rectal sample.
Long Description: This test uses transcription-mediated amplification (TMA), a highly sensitive molecular technology, to detect both Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae from a rectal swab. It's used for STD screening or symptomatic evaluation at anatomical sites beyond the standard genital/urine sample, which can be missed if only genital-site testing is performed.

Short Description: Measures 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, the active form of vitamin D, used in specialized evaluations of calcium and kidney disorders.
Long Description: Unlike standard 25-hydroxyvitamin D testing (which reflects overall vitamin D stores), this test measures 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, the hormonally active form that's tightly regulated by the kidneys and parathyroid hormone. It's used in specific situations — such as evaluating certain kidney diseases, parathyroid disorders, or rare conditions affecting calcium metabolism — rather than as a routine vitamin D status check.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — timed samples after a glucose drink, no fasting required
Patient Prep: No fasting is required beforehand; requires drinking a glucose solution, with blood drawn at set time intervals afterward.
Short Description: Gestational glucose tolerance test that doesn't require fasting beforehand, used to help screen for or diagnose gestational diabetes.
Long Description: This version of the glucose tolerance test doesn't require fasting before the glucose challenge, offering a more convenient option for gestational diabetes testing during pregnancy. It measures blood glucose at set intervals after drinking a glucose solution to evaluate how effectively the body processes sugar.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though avoiding iodine-containing supplements or contrast dye exposure beforehand may be recommended — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures iodine levels in the blood, used to help evaluate suspected iodine deficiency or excess, which can affect thyroid function.
Long Description: Iodine is an essential mineral needed to make thyroid hormone, and both too little and too much can affect thyroid function. This test measures iodine levels in the blood and is used to help evaluate suspected iodine deficiency or excess, particularly in patients with thyroid dysfunction or relevant dietary or supplement history.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though avoiding iodine-containing supplements or contrast dye exposure beforehand may be recommended — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures iodine levels in a random urine sample, used to help evaluate suspected iodine deficiency or excess.
Long Description: This test measures iodine excreted in a single, randomly collected urine sample, offering a convenient way to assess iodine status. It's used to help evaluate suspected iodine deficiency or excess, particularly in patients with thyroid dysfunction or relevant dietary or supplement history.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete 24-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab; avoid missing any voids during the collection period. Avoiding iodine-containing supplements or contrast dye exposure beforehand may also be recommended.
Short Description: Measures iodine excreted over 24 hours, giving a more complete picture of iodine status than a random urine sample.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of iodine excreted in urine over a full 24-hour period, offering a more complete and reliable picture of iodine status than a single random sample. It's used to help evaluate suspected iodine deficiency or excess, particularly in patients with thyroid dysfunction or relevant dietary or supplement history.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin A1c with automatic follow-up testing if results are unusual or inconsistent with other findings.
Long Description: Hemoglobin A1c reflects average blood sugar control over roughly the prior 2-3 months. This version includes reflex testing, meaning if the result is unusual or doesn't fit the expected clinical picture (such as in certain blood conditions that can affect A1c accuracy), additional testing is automatically performed to help clarify the finding. It's used the same way as standard A1c testing to diagnose and monitor diabetes.

Short Description: Measures calprotectin in stool, a marker of intestinal inflammation used to help evaluate inflammatory bowel disease.
Long Description: Calprotectin is a protein released by white blood cells during active intestinal inflammation, and this quantitative stool test measures its level. It's commonly used to help distinguish inflammatory bowel disease (like Crohn's disease or ulcerative colitis) from non-inflammatory conditions (like irritable bowel syndrome), and to help monitor disease activity and treatment response in patients with known inflammatory bowel disease.

Short Description: Measures hemoglobin A1c along with an estimated average glucose (eAG) value, used to help diagnose and monitor diabetes.
Long Description: This test reports hemoglobin A1c — a 2-3 month average of blood sugar control — alongside an estimated average glucose (eAG) value, which translates the A1c percentage into a number that looks more like a familiar day-to-day glucose reading. This makes results easier for some patients to relate to their own glucose monitoring, and the test is used the same way as standard A1c testing to diagnose and monitor diabetes.

Product Name (Source): QST0016845 Aldosterone/Plasma Renin Activity Ratio, LC/MS/MS
Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — special handling and positioning may apply
Patient Prep: Your provider may specify a particular body position (seated or standing) and time of day for collection, since this ratio is sensitive to posture and other factors — follow their specific instructions closely.
Short Description: Measures the ratio of aldosterone to plasma renin activity, the standard first-line test used to screen for primary aldosteronism, a treatable cause of high blood pressure.
Long Description: This test compares aldosterone (a hormone that regulates sodium and blood pressure) to plasma renin activity, calculating a ratio that's the standard first-line screening test for primary aldosteronism (Conn's syndrome) — a condition where the adrenal glands produce too much aldosterone, causing high blood pressure and low potassium. It's commonly ordered for patients with difficult-to-control high blood pressure, especially when combined with low potassium levels.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — special handling and positioning may apply
Patient Prep: Your provider may specify a particular body position (seated or standing) and time of day for collection — follow their specific instructions closely.
Short Description: Measures plasma renin activity using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used alongside aldosterone to help evaluate blood pressure and adrenal hormone disorders.
Long Description: Renin is an enzyme that plays a central role in regulating blood pressure and sodium balance. This test measures plasma renin activity using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, and is most often used together with aldosterone to help evaluate difficult-to-control high blood pressure and screen for conditions like primary aldosteronism.

Short Description: Measures carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT), a blood marker used to help detect heavy or chronic alcohol use.
Long Description: Carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT) rises with sustained heavy alcohol consumption, typically over a period of two or more weeks, making it a useful marker for identifying chronic heavy drinking that's more specific than some other liver-related tests. It's used in clinical, occupational, and legal/monitoring contexts where objective evidence of heavy alcohol use is relevant.

Patient Prep: Requires a complete, precisely timed 12-hour urine collection using a container provided by the lab — follow your provider's specific collection instructions.
Short Description: Measures total protein excreted over a 12-hour period, alongside creatinine, used to help evaluate kidney health.
Long Description: This test measures the total amount of protein excreted in urine over a 12-hour timed collection, along with creatinine to help confirm the collection was complete. It offers an alternative to a full 24-hour collection for evaluating kidney disease, monitoring proteinuria, or investigating high blood pressure or diabetes-related kidney effects.

Product Name (Source): QST0017169 Herpes Simplex Virus 1 and 2 (IgG), with Reflex to HSV-2 Inhibition
Test Name: Herpes Simplex Virus 1 and 2 (IgG), with Reflex to HSV-2 Inhibition
Short Description: Type-specific herpes antibody test that checks for HSV-1 and HSV-2 exposure, with automatic follow-up testing to confirm ambiguous HSV-2 results.
Long Description: This test checks for IgG antibodies to both herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1, usually oral herpes) and type 2 (HSV-2, usually genital herpes) separately, and includes reflex testing: if the initial HSV-2 result falls in a borderline range, an inhibition (confirmatory) test is automatically performed to improve accuracy, since standard HSV-2 antibody tests can occasionally give false-positive results in this range. It's used to help evaluate exposure status or support a diagnosis when herpes infection is suspected or being clarified.

Short Description: Herpes simplex virus type 2 (HSV-2) antibody test with automatic confirmatory testing for borderline results.
Long Description: This test checks for HSV-2 (genital herpes) IgG antibodies, and includes reflex testing: if the initial result falls in a borderline range, a confirmatory inhibition test is automatically performed to improve accuracy, since standard HSV-2 antibody tests can occasionally give false-positive results in this range. It's used to help evaluate genital herpes exposure status.

Patient Prep: No fasting required; specific timing may be requested — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures 17-hydroxyprogesterone using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used to help evaluate congenital adrenal hyperplasia.
Long Description: 17-hydroxyprogesterone is a hormone that's typically elevated in congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH), a group of inherited adrenal gland disorders. This test measures it using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method preferred for accurate diagnosis and monitoring of CAH, including as part of newborn screening follow-up testing.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — special handling and positioning may apply
Patient Prep: Your provider may specify a particular body position (seated or standing) and time of day for collection — follow their specific instructions closely.
Short Description: Measures aldosterone using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used to help evaluate blood pressure and adrenal hormone disorders.
Long Description: Aldosterone is a hormone that helps regulate sodium, potassium, and blood pressure. This test measures aldosterone using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, commonly used alongside renin testing to help evaluate difficult-to-control high blood pressure and screen for conditions like primary aldosteronism.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Morning collection is often preferred.
Short Description: Measures androstenedione using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, an adrenal/gonadal hormone used to help evaluate excess hair growth or irregular periods.
Long Description: Androstenedione is a hormone that serves as a precursor the body converts into testosterone and estrogen, produced by both the adrenal glands and ovaries/testes. This test measures it using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, and is used to help evaluate symptoms like excess or unwanted hair growth, irregular periods, or suspected adrenal or ovarian conditions.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle or pregnancy stage matters for interpretation — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures progesterone using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, used to help evaluate ovulation, fertility, and early pregnancy health.
Long Description: Progesterone is a hormone that plays a key role in preparing and maintaining the uterine lining for pregnancy. This test measures it using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method, and is commonly used to help confirm ovulation, support fertility evaluations, monitor early pregnancy, or investigate irregular menstrual cycles.

Short Description: Measures 25-hydroxyvitamin D using a highly precise mass spectrometry method, the standard test for evaluating overall vitamin D status.
Long Description: This test measures 25-hydroxyvitamin D — the main indicator of overall vitamin D status — using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), a highly precise and specific method considered by many labs to be the gold standard for vitamin D testing. It's used to help evaluate suspected vitamin D deficiency or excess, relevant to bone health, immune function, and several chronic conditions.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Intended for blood, organ, or tissue donor screening purposes.
Short Description: Antibody screening test for HTLV-I and HTLV-II, used specifically for blood, organ, or tissue donor screening.
Long Description: This test screens for antibodies to human T-lymphotropic virus (HTLV) types I and II, viruses that can rarely cause certain blood cancers or neurological conditions. It's specifically intended for donor screening purposes — such as blood, organ, or tissue donation — to help protect the safety of the donated material, rather than for general diagnostic use.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required, though ejaculation, prostate exams, or certain procedures shortly before the test may be asked to be avoided, since they can temporarily affect results.
Short Description: Measures total PSA (prostate-specific antigen), with automatic follow-up testing (such as free PSA) if results fall in a certain range.
Long Description: This test measures total PSA and includes reflex testing, meaning if the result falls in a range where additional information would be helpful (such as a borderline elevated level), further related testing (like free PSA) is automatically performed to help refine risk assessment. It's used the same way as standard PSA testing — for prostate cancer screening discussions and monitoring known prostate conditions.

Short Description: Measures CK-MB, a heart-specific enzyme, used to help evaluate suspected heart attack or heart muscle injury.
Long Description: CK-MB is a form of creatine kinase found more specifically in heart muscle than the total CK measurement alone. This test is used to help evaluate suspected heart attack or other heart muscle injury, though troponin has largely become the preferred primary marker in most modern cardiac workups, with CK-MB sometimes used as an additional or follow-up marker.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Should be performed while still eating gluten-containing foods for accurate results.
Short Description: Celiac disease antibody panel that excludes gliadin antibody testing, focusing on more specific modern markers.
Long Description: This celiac disease panel focuses on the more specific, modern antibody markers (such as tissue transglutaminase) without including the older, less specific anti-gliadin antibody test. It's used to help screen for celiac disease using the antibody tests generally considered most accurate by current clinical guidelines.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; a random urine sample is typically used.
Short Description: Measures small amounts of albumin (microalbumin) in a random urine sample, an early marker of kidney damage.
Long Description: This test measures microalbumin — small amounts of the protein albumin not detectable on a standard urinalysis — in a single, randomly collected urine sample. It's commonly used to help detect early kidney damage, especially in people with diabetes or high blood pressure, since it can pick up kidney changes before more significant disease develops; pairing with a creatinine measurement is often done to account for urine concentration.

Short Description: Genetic test that checks for the Factor V Leiden mutation, the most common inherited blood clotting risk factor.
Long Description: Factor V Leiden is a genetic variant that's the most common inherited cause of increased blood clotting risk (thrombophilia). This test looks directly for the mutation in a patient's DNA, and is typically ordered as part of a clotting disorder workup for patients with unexplained blood clots, recurrent pregnancy loss, or a strong family history of clotting disorders. Unlike some other clotting tests, results aren't affected by blood thinners, so this test can be done even while a patient is on anticoagulant therapy.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — 7 timed specimens, special positioning may apply
Patient Prep: Requires seven separate blood draws at specific timed intervals, per your provider's detailed protocol — coordinate timing and positioning instructions closely with your provider.
Short Description: Specialized test that tracks the aldosterone-to-cortisol ratio across seven timed samples, used in detailed adrenal hormone evaluations.
Long Description: This specialized test measures aldosterone and cortisol together across seven separately timed blood samples, calculating their ratio at each point to evaluate the pattern of adrenal hormone release over time. It's used by endocrinologists in detailed evaluations of certain adrenal or blood pressure-related hormone disorders, where a single measurement wouldn't capture the full picture.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; best collected within the first several days of symptom onset.
Short Description: PCR test that detects dengue virus, a mosquito-borne infection, used to help confirm active dengue fever.
Long Description: Dengue fever is a mosquito-borne viral illness common in many tropical and subtropical regions, causing fever, headache, joint and muscle pain, and rash. This PCR test detects the virus's genetic material directly in the blood, most useful in the first several days of illness, and is commonly ordered for patients with compatible symptoms and relevant travel history to a dengue-endemic area.

Short Description: Quantitative hCG test that includes a reference table correlating hormone levels to estimated gestational age.
Long Description: This quantitative hCG test measures the exact pregnancy hormone level and includes a reference table showing expected hCG ranges at different points in early pregnancy, helping a provider get a rough sense of gestational age or flag results that fall outside the expected range for further evaluation, such as monitoring for early pregnancy complications.

Short Description: Measures antisperm antibodies (IgA and IgG), used as part of a fertility evaluation.
Long Description: Antisperm antibodies are immune proteins that can interfere with sperm function and movement, potentially contributing to infertility in either partner. This test measures both IgA and IgG antisperm antibodies and is used as part of a broader fertility workup when unexplained infertility or certain other fertility-related findings raise suspicion for an immune-related cause.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required. Best collected while the patient is symptomatic.
Short Description: Quantitative PCR test that measures the amount of varicella-zoster virus (VZV) DNA in the blood.
Long Description: Unlike a qualitative VZV PCR test (which simply reports positive or negative), this quantitative version measures the actual amount of varicella-zoster virus DNA present. It's used in specific clinical situations — such as more detailed monitoring of active or severe VZV infections, particularly in immunocompromised patients — rather than routine diagnosis.

Short Description: Combines AFP with its more specific L3 fraction, used to help improve early detection and monitoring of liver cancer.
Long Description: This test measures total AFP alongside AFP-L3, a specific fraction of AFP that's more closely associated with liver cancer (hepatocellular carcinoma) than total AFP alone, which can also be elevated in non-cancerous liver conditions. Measuring both together, along with the AFP-L3 percentage, can help improve the accuracy of liver cancer risk assessment, particularly in patients with chronic liver disease being monitored for cancer development.

Patient Prep: Fasting for 9-12 hours is generally recommended for the most accurate results.
Short Description: Standard lipid panel that also calculates key cardiovascular risk ratios, such as total cholesterol to HDL.
Long Description: This lipid panel includes the standard measurements (total cholesterol, LDL, HDL, and triglycerides) along with calculated ratios between them, such as the total cholesterol-to-HDL ratio, which some providers use as an additional data point in cardiovascular risk assessment alongside the individual values.

Patient Prep: Requires resting quietly (often lying down) for a period before the draw, and avoiding caffeine, strenuous exercise, and certain medications beforehand — follow your provider's specific preparation instructions.
Short Description: Measures fractionated metanephrines in the blood, a sensitive test used to help evaluate suspected pheochromocytoma or related adrenal tumors.
Long Description: Metanephrines are breakdown products of catecholamines (stress hormones), and measuring them individually ('fractionated') in the blood is considered one of the most sensitive tests available for evaluating suspected pheochromocytoma or paraganglioma — rare tumors that can cause dangerously high blood pressure, palpitations, and other symptoms from excess catecholamine release. Because levels are sensitive to stress and position, careful preparation is important for accurate results.

Specimen Type: Vaginal or urethral swab, or urine sample
Patient Prep: No special preparation required; specific collection method depends on your provider's preference.
Short Description: Molecular (RNA) test that detects Trichomonas vaginalis, a common sexually transmitted parasitic infection.
Long Description: Trichomonas vaginalis is a parasite that causes one of the most common curable sexually transmitted infections, though many people have no symptoms. This molecular test detects the organism's genetic material, offering high sensitivity for diagnosis, and is commonly ordered as part of routine STD screening or when compatible symptoms (such as discharge, itching, or discomfort) are present.

Short Description: Measures 1,5-anhydroglucitol, a marker sometimes used to help detect short-term spikes in blood sugar not captured by A1c.
Long Description: This test measures 1,5-anhydroglucitol, a substance that drops when blood sugar has recently spiked above a certain threshold, making it useful for detecting short-term, day-to-day glucose swings that a longer-term marker like hemoglobin A1c might miss. It's sometimes used in diabetes management to help identify patients with significant glucose variability despite a seemingly well-controlled A1c.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to onset of chest pain or other symptoms may matter for interpretation — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures troponin I, the primary marker used to help diagnose a heart attack, using a specific testing platform.
Long Description: Troponin I is a protein released into the blood when heart muscle is damaged, making it the primary and most specific marker used to diagnose a heart attack (myocardial infarction). This test measures troponin I using a specific analyzer platform, and is typically ordered urgently for patients with chest pain or other symptoms suggestive of a heart attack, often repeated over several hours to track the trend.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though your provider may request it.
Short Description: Measures Coenzyme Q10 levels, an antioxidant involved in cellular energy production, used to help evaluate suspected deficiency.
Long Description: Coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) plays a key role in how cells produce energy and also acts as an antioxidant. Levels can be affected by certain medications (notably statins, which can lower CoQ10), some inherited metabolic or mitochondrial disorders, and general nutritional status. This test is used to help evaluate suspected CoQ10 deficiency, particularly in patients with muscle symptoms on statin therapy or certain mitochondrial disease evaluations.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — timed 1 hour after a 50-gram glucose drink
Patient Prep: No fasting is required beforehand; requires drinking a standardized 50-gram glucose solution, with a blood draw exactly 1 hour later.
Short Description: One-hour glucose screening test using a 50-gram glucose challenge and a 140 mg/dL threshold, used to screen for gestational diabetes during pregnancy.
Long Description: This is a standard initial screening test for gestational diabetes, measuring blood glucose one hour after drinking a 50-gram glucose solution — no fasting is required beforehand. This version uses a 140 mg/dL cutoff for flagging an abnormal result (some protocols use 135 mg/dL instead). If the result is elevated, it's typically followed by a longer, more detailed glucose tolerance test to confirm a gestational diabetes diagnosis.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma), fasting and then a timed sample after a 50-gram glucose drink
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a standardized 50-gram glucose solution, with a blood draw at a set time afterward.
Short Description: Fasting plus 1-hour glucose screening test using a 140 mg/dL threshold, used to screen for gestational diabetes during pregnancy.
Long Description: This version of the gestational diabetes screening test includes a fasting glucose measurement in addition to the standard 1-hour post-glucose reading, using a 140 mg/dL cutoff to flag an abnormal result. It's used to help screen for gestational diabetes during pregnancy, typically between 24-28 weeks.

Patient Prep: No fasting required, though morning collection is sometimes preferred.
Short Description: Measures unconjugated (free) DHEA, an adrenal hormone precursor, used in specialized hormone evaluations.
Long Description: Most DHEA in the blood circulates in its sulfated form (DHEA-S), which is more commonly tested; this test measures the unconjugated (non-sulfated) form specifically. It's used in more specialized adrenal or hormone evaluations, sometimes providing additional information beyond standard DHEA-S testing, particularly by endocrinologists investigating specific hormone patterns.

Patient Prep: Requires collecting saliva at specific times (often several samples through the day) using a kit — avoid eating, drinking, or brushing teeth for a period beforehand, per your provider's or the kit's instructions.
Short Description: Measures cortisol levels in saliva, often collected at multiple points through the day to evaluate the body's natural cortisol rhythm.
Long Description: Salivary cortisol testing offers a non-invasive way to measure cortisol, and is often collected at several time points throughout the day to evaluate whether a patient's cortisol follows its expected daily pattern (high in the morning, low at night). It's used to help evaluate suspected Cushing's syndrome, adrenal insufficiency, or general adrenal/stress hormone patterns, and is sometimes used in sleep or chronic stress-related evaluations.

Patient Prep: Requires prompt, careful specimen processing per your provider's or lab's specific instructions, since this marker can be affected by handling delays.
Short Description: Measures complement C4a, an immune system marker sometimes used in specialized evaluations of chronic inflammatory conditions.
Long Description: C4a is a fragment released when the C4 complement protein is activated as part of an immune response. This test is used in specialized clinical contexts, including by some practitioners evaluating chronic inflammatory response patterns, though its use for this purpose is not universally accepted in mainstream medicine and results are best interpreted by a provider familiar with the specific clinical question being asked.

Patient Prep: This test requires a bacterial isolate from a prior culture — coordinate with your provider or lab on submission requirements.
Short Description: Specialized susceptibility test that determines the minimum concentration of an antibiotic needed to kill (rather than just inhibit) up to five aerobic bacteria.
Long Description: Minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) testing determines the lowest antibiotic concentration needed to actually kill a bacterium, rather than just stop it from growing (which is what standard susceptibility testing measures). This specialized test evaluates up to five aerobic organisms and is typically ordered by a specialist in complex or difficult-to-treat infections where this more detailed information is clinically useful.

Short Description: Antibody test that helps identify a current or recent Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection, a common cause of respiratory illness.
Long Description: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a common cause of respiratory infections, including a relatively mild form of pneumonia sometimes called 'walking pneumonia.' This test detects IgM antibodies, which typically indicate a current or recent infection, and is commonly ordered for patients with a persistent cough or respiratory symptoms when this organism is suspected.

Short Description: Screens for hepatitis B surface antigen, with automatic confirmatory testing if the initial result is positive.
Long Description: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) is the first marker to appear in an active hepatitis B infection, and a positive result indicates the person is currently infected and potentially able to spread the virus. This version includes a confirmatory ('supplemental') test that automatically runs if the initial screen is positive, to help rule out a false-positive result before reporting a confirmed diagnosis.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to rubella (German measles), often used in prenatal and pre-pregnancy screening.
Long Description: This test detects rubella (German measles) IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from past infection or vaccination. It's a standard part of prenatal care because rubella infection during pregnancy can cause serious birth defects, so confirming immunity helps guide whether vaccination or extra precautions are needed.

Short Description: Screening test for syphilis, historically used as part of premarital health screening, with automatic confirmatory testing if positive.
Long Description: This RPR (rapid plasma reagin) test screens for antibodies associated with syphilis infection, historically required in some jurisdictions as part of premarital health screening. This version includes reflex testing, so a positive screen automatically triggers a confirmatory treponemal antibody test to help rule out a false positive before reporting a confirmed result.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to menopause status can matter for interpretation — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures estrone, a form of estrogen that becomes more prominent after menopause, used to help evaluate hormone balance.
Long Description: Estrone is one of the main forms of estrogen, and typically becomes the more dominant estrogen type after menopause, when estradiol production declines. This test is used to help evaluate menopausal hormone status, certain hormone-related conditions, and hormone therapy monitoring.

Specimen Type: Blood draw (plasma) — 3 timed specimens (fasting plus 2 post-glucose draws)
Patient Prep: Requires fasting beforehand, then drinking a glucose solution, with blood drawn at two separate time points afterward — plan for the appointment to take a few hours.
Short Description: Glucose tolerance test with three timed measurements, used for a more detailed look at how the body processes sugar than a standard 1-2 point test.
Long Description: This glucose tolerance test measures blood glucose while fasting and then at two separate time points after a glucose challenge, giving a more detailed picture of how blood sugar rises and falls than a standard 1-2 point test. It's used in situations where a more detailed glucose curve is needed, such as investigating reactive hypoglycemia, unusual blood sugar patterns, or confirming a diabetes or gestational diabetes diagnosis.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to Sunflower Seed, an occupational/contact allergen.
Long Description: This ImmunoCAP-based test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to Sunflower Seed, an allergen associated with contact, inhalation, or occupational exposure, such as latex. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this specific allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures. It's commonly ordered individually to pin down a suspected trigger, or as part of a broader regional, seasonal, or food allergen panel.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (RAST) that checks for an IgE antibody response to raspberry, a food allergen.
Long Description: This test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to raspberry, a food allergen that can trigger reactions ranging from mild (hives, itching, digestive upset) to severe (anaphylaxis) in sensitive individuals. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures.

Short Description: Determines ABO blood type and Rh factor, with automatic follow-up testing if results are unclear or require confirmation.
Long Description: This test determines ABO blood group (A, B, AB, or O) and Rh factor (positive or negative), and includes reflex testing, meaning if initial results are weak, unclear, or need confirmation, additional testing is automatically performed. It's a standard part of prenatal care and is essential information before blood transfusion or certain surgical procedures.

Short Description: Allergy blood test (ImmunoCAP) that checks for an IgE antibody response to yellow dock, a weed pollen allergen.
Long Description: This test measures allergen-specific IgE antibodies to yellow dock, a weed pollen and common outdoor allergen that can be a frequent cause of seasonal hay fever symptoms, especially in late summer and fall. A positive (elevated) result indicates the immune system has produced antibodies against this allergen, which is associated with — but doesn't on its own confirm — a true clinical allergy, since results need to be interpreted alongside the patient's symptom history and known exposures.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Strenuous exercise shortly before the test can temporarily raise results.
Short Description: Measures total creatine kinase (CK), with automatic follow-up testing (such as CK isoenzymes) if results are abnormal.
Long Description: Creatine kinase (CK) is an enzyme found mainly in muscle tissue, and elevated levels can indicate muscle damage from various causes. This version includes reflex testing, meaning if the result is abnormal, additional related testing (such as CK isoenzymes) is automatically performed to help pinpoint the likely source — heart, skeletal muscle, or brain.

Patient Prep: Morning collection is generally preferred, since iron levels naturally vary through the day.
Short Description: Combined iron panel (iron, TIBC, and percent saturation) with automatic follow-up testing if results suggest it's needed.
Long Description: This panel combines serum iron, total iron-binding capacity (TIBC), and calculated percent transferrin saturation, with reflex testing meaning additional related testing (such as ferritin) is automatically performed if the initial results suggest further evaluation would help. Together, these help distinguish iron deficiency from iron overload conditions and other causes of abnormal iron-related results.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (CA 125) most often used to help monitor ovarian cancer.
Long Description: CA 125 is a protein that can be elevated in ovarian cancer, though it can also rise with several non-cancerous conditions, including menstruation, endometriosis, and pelvic inflammatory disease. It's primarily used to help monitor known ovarian cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence, and is sometimes used alongside imaging in evaluating a pelvic mass, though it's not sensitive or specific enough to be used as a general population screening test for ovarian cancer.

Short Description: Antibody test that checks for immunity to varicella-zoster virus (chickenpox), with automatic follow-up testing if results are unclear.
Long Description: This test detects varicella-zoster virus (VZV) IgG antibodies, which indicate immunity from past infection or vaccination, and includes reflex testing to help clarify borderline or unclear results. It's commonly used to confirm immune status when vaccination history is uncertain, such as before pregnancy or for healthcare workers.

Short Description: Antibody panel that checks for exposure to Chlamydia trachomatis using multiple antibody markers together.
Long Description: This panel combines several Chlamydia trachomatis antibody tests into one order, offering a broader assessment of past exposure than a single antibody test alone. It's used less commonly than direct molecular (NAAT) testing for diagnosing an active genital infection, but can be useful in evaluating certain chlamydia-related complications or past exposure history.

Short Description: Measures total glycated hemoglobin, reflecting average blood sugar control, used to help diagnose and monitor diabetes.
Long Description: This test measures total glycated hemoglobin — hemoglobin that has glucose attached — similar in concept to hemoglobin A1c, reflecting average blood sugar control over time. It's used to help diagnose and monitor diabetes, functioning much like standard A1c testing.

Short Description: Tumor marker blood test (CA 27.29) most often used to help monitor breast cancer.
Long Description: CA 27.29 is a protein that can be elevated in breast cancer, particularly in more advanced or metastatic disease, similar in purpose to CA 15-3 though measured with a different assay method. It's primarily used to help monitor known breast cancer patients for treatment response or recurrence, alongside imaging and clinical evaluation, rather than as a stand-alone screening test.

Short Description: Measures erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) using the traditional manual Westergren method, a general marker of inflammation.
Long Description: The Westergren method is the traditional, manually performed technique for measuring sedimentation rate (ESR), a nonspecific but useful marker of inflammation in the body. It's used the same way as other ESR testing methods — to help evaluate inflammatory or autoimmune conditions, infections, and some cancers — with the Westergren method often considered a reference standard for accuracy.

Patient Prep: No special preparation required; ideally collected during active diarrhea symptoms.
Short Description: Stool test that detects Shiga toxins, used to help identify E. coli strains and other bacteria that can cause severe gastrointestinal illness.
Long Description: Shiga toxin-producing bacteria, including certain E. coli strains like O157:H7, can cause severe gastrointestinal illness, including bloody diarrhea and, in some cases, serious complications like hemolytic uremic syndrome. This test detects the toxins directly, helping identify these more dangerous strains, and is commonly ordered for patients with bloody diarrhea or severe gastrointestinal symptoms.

Short Description: Combined panel that measures thyroglobulin along with thyroglobulin antibodies, most often used to monitor thyroid cancer.
Long Description: This panel measures thyroglobulin (a protein made by thyroid tissue, used as a tumor marker after thyroid cancer treatment) together with thyroglobulin antibodies, which can interfere with accurate thyroglobulin measurement if present. Testing both together helps a provider correctly interpret thyroglobulin results when monitoring for thyroid cancer recurrence after surgery and/or radioactive iodine treatment.

Patient Prep: No fasting required. Timing relative to the menstrual cycle or menopause status matters for interpretation — follow your provider's instructions.
Short Description: Measures estradiol using an ultrasensitive mass spectrometry method, especially useful for very low levels seen in men, children, and postmenopausal women.
Long Description: This test measures estradiol using liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) at an ultrasensitive level, capable of accurately detecting the very low estradiol levels typically seen in men, children, and postmenopausal women, where standard immunoassay methods can be less reliable. It's used in specialized hormone evaluations where a more precise, low-range estradiol measurement matters.

Short Description: Combined antibody panel (IgG, IgA, and IgM) for Candida albicans, sometimes used in evaluations of suspected candida-related symptoms.
Long Description: This panel measures IgG, IgA, and IgM antibodies to Candida albicans, a yeast that's a normal part of the body's microbiome but can occasionally overgrow or cause infection. Antibody testing for candida is used by some practitioners, particularly in functional and integrative medicine, to explore possible candida-related symptoms, though mainstream medicine generally relies on direct culture, microscopy, or clinical exam to diagnose an active candida infection rather than antibody levels, since antibodies are common in healthy people due to normal candida exposure. Anyone considering this test should discuss its limitations with their provider.

Short Description: Older screening test for syphilis, similar in purpose to RPR testing.
